📖 IS Theory Encyclopedia

PhD Comprehensive Exam Study Resource | Student-Compiled Theory Repository

70 Total Theories
7 Meta-Frameworks
15 IS-Native
55 Borrowed

📚 About This Encyclopedia

⚠️ Disclaimer: This is not an official UNT document. It is a student-compiled resource based on coursework (BCIS 6670, 6680, 6360) and IS literature, intended to support PhD comprehensive exam preparation.

A curated collection of Information Systems theories for PhD comprehensive exam preparation. Each theory includes: foundational origins, theoretical core, evolution in IS research, critical analysis, and practical exam applications. This resource compiles insights from coursework and relevant IS literature.

📊 Coverage: 70 core theories (15 IS-native + 55 borrowed) plus 9 philosophy of science frameworks, 7 meta-theory guides, and 8 research methods guides.

🎯 How to use: Browse by category below, use search (Ctrl+K), or jump to specific theories. Each entry includes exam-ready examples and measurement strategies.

📋 Exam Blueprint for PhD Comprehensive Exam (IS / BCIS)

🎯 Exam-Prep Domains

Theory Foundations 40% Core theories, meta-frameworks, theory connections
Research Methods & Study Design 25% Validity, causality, SLR, mixed methods, grounded theory
Measurement & Operationalization 20% Constructs, scales, common method bias, reliability
Critique & Synthesis Skills 15% Connecting theories, identifying boundary conditions

📚 Suggested Study Paths

⚡ Fast Track (7–14 Days)

  1. Days 1-2: Part 0C (Meta-Theory) + Foundation concepts (research definition, Gregor's 5 types, DSR frameworks)
  2. Days 3-5: Part A (Core IS Theories) + Part C (Behavioral) - highest exam frequency
  3. Days 6-7: Part B (Organizational) + Research Methods section
  4. Days 8-10: Part D (Security/Criminological) + Practice all questions
  5. Days 11-14: Review practice questions, create flashcards, simulate exam conditions (90-100 word answers)

Focus on theories tagged Core for comps and practice writing concise answers

📖 Standard Track (3–6 Weeks)

  1. Week 1: Meta-Theory + Philosophy of Science foundations (Kuhn, Popper, Gregor, Whetten)
  2. Week 2: Part A (Core IS Theories) - deep reading with practice question attempts
  3. Week 3: Part B (Organizational) + Part C (Behavioral) - largest sections
  4. Week 4: Part D (Security) + Research Methods section - methodological foundations
  5. Week 5: Practice questions (all parts), identify weak areas, review connections
  6. Week 6: Targeted review, flashcard mastery, full exam simulation with time limits

Study all theories systematically, practice synthesizing across domains

🏷️ Priority Tag Guide

Core for comps = Widely-used foundational theories appearing frequently in exam questions (study first)
Important = Valuable theories with strong exam applicability (study thoroughly)
Optional / niche = Specialized theories for specific research contexts (review if time permits)

Philosophy of Science Kuhn, 1962

Kuhn: Paradigms and Scientific Revolutions

Scientific progress through paradigm shifts and revolutionary change

Core Concept: Science advances not through steady accumulation but through revolutionary paradigm shifts when anomalies accumulate and a new worldview replaces the old.

🔬 Normal Science: Puzzle-solving within established paradigms
⚡ Paradigm Shift: Revolutionary change when anomalies mount
🌍 Incommensurability: Different paradigms see different worlds
📚 Scientific Community: Social structure of science shapes what counts as knowledge
IS Relevance: Understanding how IS research evolves through paradigmatic shifts (e.g., from technical systems to sociotechnical practice)
📚 Paradigmatic Shifts in IS Research

Kuhn's framework is particularly useful for understanding how IS has shifted from technical rationality (systems as objective problem-solving tools) to sociotechnical practice (systems embedded in social meaning and power). A key exam move is to show how earlier anomalies - why IT adoption failed despite technical superiority, why dashboards didn't improve decisions as predicted - accumulated until a new paradigm (interpretive or critical) emerged. This is more nuanced than simply saying "new theories replaced old ones"; it shows understanding of paradigm shifts as scientific revolutions.

🔗 Connections to Other Theories
Kuhn and Popper: Popper believed science advances through falsification of bold conjectures, but Kuhn observed that paradigm shifts are not driven by single refutations - scientists defend their paradigm against anomalies. In exams, use Kuhn when explaining why a dominant theory (like TAM) persists despite contradictory findings; use Popper when arguing that a theory should be abandoned if predictions fail. Incommensurability is Kuhn's key challenge to Popper: competing paradigms may not even agree on what counts as evidence.
Kuhn and Lakatos: Lakatos tried to merge Kuhn and Popper by introducing research programmes with hard cores and protective belts. A progressive programme adds novel predictions; a degenerating one just adjusts auxiliary hypotheses to save the core. In IS comps, you can argue that TAM extensions (adding moderators, new contexts) represent either a progressive programme (if they predict new phenomena) or a degenerating one (if they just explain away contradictions).
📖 Key People and Seminal Papers
Citation foundation: Kuhn, T. S. (1962). The structure of scientific revolutions. University of Chicago Press. (Note: The 3rd edition, 1996, added important clarifications about paradigms.) In IS, cite Kuhn when framing IS research as undergoing paradigmatic shifts. For example: Bhattacherjee and Grover (2013) discuss how information systems research moved from purely technical to sociotechnical orientations - a Kuhnian lens helps explain this evolution. Critical point: Don't overuse Kuhn as a general theory label; use it specifically when analyzing how an entire research community shifted its fundamental assumptions.
🎯 Exam Strategy
When to use Kuhn: Deploy this lens when the exam asks about research evolution, paradigm wars, or why older theories are abandoned. For example: "How has IS research understanding of digital transformation changed over the past two decades?" Answer: Moved from IT-centric paradigms (systems as exogenous change agents) to sociotechnical paradigms (systems as embedded in organizations and power). Show the accumulating anomalies (implementations failed despite technical excellence; benefits did not appear as promised) and the new paradigm that emerged.
Common mistake: Treating "paradigm" as a casual synonym for "perspective" or "theory." Kuhn means something specific: a community-wide shift in fundamental assumptions about what counts as a research problem, what methods are legitimate, and what answers look like. Using Kuhn loosely weakens your argument.
Philosophy of Science Popper, 1959

Popper: Falsification and What Counts as Science

Science distinguished by falsifiability, not verification

Core Concept: Scientific theories must be falsifiable. Science advances through bold conjectures and rigorous attempts at refutation, not through verification.

🎯 Falsifiability: Theories must make testable predictions
🔍 Critical Testing: Seek evidence that could disprove theories
📊 Demarcation: Separates science from pseudoscience
🔄 Conjecture & Refutation: Science progresses through bold hypotheses and rigorous attempts to refute them
IS Relevance: Designing research that makes testable predictions and can be empirically refuted
📚 Falsifiability in IS Research Design

Popper's key insight for IS is that falsifiability distinguishes science from pseudoscience. Many IS theories make vague claims that are hard to refute: "Organizations benefit from digital transformation" cannot fail if you can always find some benefit somewhere. A Popperian critique would demand: What specific outcome would prove this wrong? What boundary conditions would cause the relationship to reverse? In exams, use Popper when critiquing weak research design.

🔗 Connections to Other Theories
Popper and Kuhn: Popper believed individual refutations drive science forward; Kuhn showed that paradigms persist despite refutations. In IS comps, when defending a theory against contradictory evidence, you can argue either way: Popper would say "the theory is falsified, move on"; Kuhn would say "anomalies don't immediately topple paradigms." Use Popper for stronger critiques of underdeveloped theories.
Popper and Positivism: Popper rejected the positivist idea that science confirms theories through verification. Instead, he argued science works by bold conjectures and critical testing. Both share commitment to empiricism and objective reality, but differ on method. In IS, this matters: positivist papers often claim to "confirm" hypotheses; Popperian papers would frame it as "this hypothesis survived attempts to refute it."
📖 Key People and Seminal Papers
Citation foundation: Popper, K. R. (1959). The logic of scientific discovery. Basic Books. (Originally published in German in 1934 as Logik der Forschung.) In IS, you rarely cite Popper directly, but his concepts (falsifiability, refutation) show up in how IS researchers frame hypothesis testing and research rigor. For exam purposes, cite him when defending strong empirical standards or critiquing unfalsifiable claims. Critical point: Popper is about the logic of testing, not about who is right; even if you have good data, ask: what would change your mind?
🎯 Exam Strategy
When to use Popper: Deploy this lens when critiquing weak research design or defending strong empirical standards. Example exam answer: "The study claims AI improves decision quality, but the outcome measure (any improvement, however small) is unfalsifiable. A Popperian approach would require specifying conditions under which AI would fail to improve decisions, then testing those." Shows you understand the demarcation between science and non-science.
Boundary condition: Falsifiability works best for causal or predictive claims. For meaning-focused research (phenomenology, critical analysis), demanding falsifiability becomes less relevant. In exams, acknowledge this: "Popper's standard applies strongly to variance-based research but less so to interpretive research that seeks understanding rather than prediction."
Philosophy of Science Lakatos, 1970

Lakatos: Research Programmes as the Middle Ground

Science as evolving research programmes with hard cores and protective belts

Core Concept: Scientific theories have a hard core (central assumptions) protected by auxiliary hypotheses. Programs are progressive or degenerating based on novel predictions.

🛡️ Hard Core: Central assumptions protected from falsification
🔄 Protective Belt: Auxiliary hypotheses that can be modified
📈 Progressive vs Degenerating: Quality of research program evolution
⚖️ Sophisticated Falsification: Theories rejected only when a better alternative exists, not from single refutations
IS Relevance: Evaluating IS research programs (e.g., TAM extensions) as progressive or degenerating
📚 Research Programmes in IS: TAM as a Case Study

Lakatos's framework is powerful for IS because many IS "theories" are actually evolving research programmes. The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) has a hard core (perceived usefulness and ease of use drive adoption) protected by auxiliary hypotheses and moderators added over decades. Lakatos would ask: Are TAM extensions progressive (predicting novel phenomena in new contexts) or degenerating (just saving the core with ad-hoc moderators)? In exams, use this lens to evaluate whether a research tradition is advancing or stagnating.

🔗 Connections to Other Theories
Lakatos as a bridge: Lakatos tried to reconcile Kuhn and Popper. Unlike Kuhn, Lakatos allows rational evaluation of competing programmes: a progressive programme gains novel predictions, while a degenerating one does not. Unlike Popper, Lakatos recognizes that a single refutation doesn't kill a theory - you can defend the hard core through protective-belt adjustments. In IS exams, use Lakatos when you want to evaluate theories without being naively falsificationist or purely relativistic.
Lakatos and institutional theory: Both examine how research traditions evolve, but Lakatos is about scientific rationality while institutional theory is about legitimacy and social pressures. A programme might be degenerating scientifically but still thrive institutionally because reviewers expect it. In IS comps, you can argue: "TAM remains influential institutionally, but Lakatosian analysis shows whether it is still scientifically progressive."
📖 Key People and Seminal Papers
Citation foundation: Lakatos, I. (1970). Falsification and the methodology of scientific research programmes. In I. Lakatos & A. Musgrave (Eds.), Criticism and the growth of knowledge (pp. 91–196). Cambridge University Press. In IS, Lakatos is rarely cited directly, but his language (hard core, protective belt, progressive vs. degenerating) is valuable for analyzing research traditions. Critical point: Use Lakatos as a diagnostic tool for your field, not as a prescriptive framework. You are not saying "this research should be abandoned"; you are saying "by Lakatosian standards, this programme has these characteristics."
🎯 Exam Strategy
When to use Lakatos: Deploy this lens when analyzing research traditions or when responding to questions about research evaluation and evolution. Example: "Evaluate the Technology Acceptance Model's contribution to IS." Answer: "TAM's hard core is that perceived usefulness and ease of use drive adoption. Early extensions added moderators (experience, voluntariness) and contexts (different technologies). By Lakatosian standards, is this progressive (novel predictions in new domains) or degenerating (just saving the core)? Evidence suggests it is increasingly degenerating - newer TAM papers add moderators without predicting genuinely novel phenomena." This shows sophisticated evaluation.
Caution on determinism: Lakatos's framework is an ex-post analysis. You cannot always tell in advance whether a programme is progressive. Be modest: "Current evidence suggests..." rather than claiming certainty about a programme's trajectory.
Comparison Framework Multiple Authors

Kuhn vs Popper vs Lakatos: Comparative Analysis

Understanding different philosophies of scientific progress

Purpose: Compare and contrast three major philosophies of science to understand how scientific knowledge develops and what counts as good research.

📊 Comparison Table: Side-by-side analysis of key dimensions
🎓 Exam Critical: Essential for comprehensive exam discussions
🔗 Integration: How each applies to IS research evaluation
🔑 Key Distinction: Revolution (Kuhn) vs Refutation (Popper) vs Progressive Programs (Lakatos)
IS Relevance: Framing your research approach and defending methodological choices in comprehensive exams
📊 Comparison Framework: Three Lenses on Scientific Progress
Popper's view: Science advances through bold conjectures and critical testing. A theory is scientific if falsifiable; it gains credibility by surviving rigorous attempts at refutation. Kuhn's view: Science advances through paradigm shifts. Normal science solves puzzles within a paradigm; revolutions occur when anomalies accumulate and a new paradigm emerges. Lakatos's view: Science advances through progressive research programmes with hard cores and protective belts. Programmes are evaluated by whether they generate novel predictions (progressive) or just defend the core (degenerating). In IS exams, knowing all three lets you choose the most powerful lens for your argument.
🎯 Choosing the Right Lens for Your Answer
Use Popper when: You need to critique weak research design (unfalsifiable claims, circular logic). Example: "The theory claims digital transformation increases innovation, but defines innovation so broadly that any outcome counts as innovation. By Popperian standards, this is pseudoscience."
Use Kuhn when: You are analyzing how IS research paradigms have shifted (from technical to sociotechnical, from variance to process models). Example: "Anomalies in technical implementation theories (system excellence didn't guarantee adoption) accumulated until a new paradigm (sociotechnical) emerged."
Use Lakatos when: You are evaluating whether a research tradition (like TAM or diffusion theory) is scientifically healthy. Example: "Is the research tradition around user adoption still progressive, or is it degenerating into increasingly ad-hoc moderators?"
🔗 Integration Strategy for Exams
The meta-move: In strong comps answers, you can use all three together. Example: "Positivist reviewers will demand falsifiable predictions (Popper). But we also recognize that IS research operates within paradigms (Kuhn) where some anomalies are absorbed through protective-belt adjustments (Lakatos). A mature research programme navigates all three: it makes falsifiable claims (Popper), acknowledges it operates within a paradigm (Kuhn), and produces novel predictions rather than just defending the core (Lakatos)." This shows sophisticated integration.
Avoid false dichotomies: Do not treat these as mutually exclusive (either Kuhn is right or Popper is right). They illuminate different aspects of how science works. Use them as complementary diagnostic tools.
Philosophy of Science Comte, 1830s

Positivism

Scientific knowledge through empirical observation and objective measurement

Core Concept: True knowledge comes from empirical observation and scientific method. Reality exists objectively and can be measured through systematic observation, leading to laws and predictions.

🔬 Empiricism: Knowledge through observation and measurement
📊 Objectivity: Reality exists independent of observer
⚖️ Causality: Identifying cause-effect relationships through testing
📈 Prediction: Scientific laws enable forecasting
IS Relevance: Foundation for quantitative IS research using surveys, experiments, and statistical analysis to test causal relationships and build predictive models
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Positivism is a philosophy-of-science tradition rather than one paper. In IS, it is usually discussed as a paradigm that supports hypothesis testing and causal explanation. A common IS framing is the paradigms discussion in Goles and Hirschheim (2000).
Discipline of origin Philosophy of science.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Meta (not a theory type). Positivism is a stance about knowledge, not a theory of a phenomenon.
Core constructs Objectivity, empiricism, measurement, causal explanation, replication.
Mechanism (plain) You treat reality as external and measurable, then you test claims about relationships using data and formal logic.
Boundary conditions Most useful when constructs can be operationalized with reasonable validity and when causal claims are supported by design (experiments, quasi-experiments, strong controls). Less useful for meaning-making questions where "what the thing is" depends on interpretation.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Positivist logic remains the default in many IS empirical papers (especially in security, analytics, and economics-style work), but it is rarely cited as "positivism" directly. It shows up in research design choices and review criteria. (Not typically listed as a theory lens in citations.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Positivism grew out of the idea that science should be grounded in observable facts and that good explanations should connect causes to effects in a way that can be tested. In practice, what matters for comps is how positivism shapes the "rules of the game": clear constructs, testable hypotheses, measurement validity, and attention to alternative explanations.

💎 Theoretical Core

The core claim is not about any single social phenomenon. It is about what kind of knowledge counts as credible. A positivist paper usually aims to explain and predict: it models relationships among constructs, tests them with data, and tries to separate signal from noise. The causal mechanism in positivist work is often expressed as a nomological network. The exam move is to connect it to design. If your causal story is strong but your design is weak (endogeneity, common method bias, selection), positivist reviewers will push back hard. Boundary conditions matter because positivism can look "clean" but be wrong if the constructs do not travel well across contexts. For example, a measure that works in one country or platform might not mean the same thing elsewhere.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS has long debated paradigms, especially when interpretive and critical traditions challenged a single dominant view of "rigor." Goles and Hirschheim (2000) is a useful anchor for comps because it shows how paradigms shape assumptions, methods, and what reviewers reward. More recently, IS also debates whether purely variable-based models are enough for digital phenomena that change fast (platforms, AI, ecosystems). This does not mean positivism disappears. It means positivist work increasingly borrows designs and data sources (field experiments, trace data, audits) to strengthen causal claims.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Positivism gives clear standards. It pushes you to define constructs, design tests, and take alternative explanations seriously. This is why it remains dominant in many top-journal review processes. Weaknesses: It can underplay meaning, context, and power. It can also create "measurement confidence" where we trust a scale too much even when the underlying phenomenon is shifting (example: new AI work practices that older scales do not capture well). Common misapplications in IS: Treating statistical significance as substantive truth, using weak proxies for complex constructs, and claiming causality from cross-sectional survey correlations. Alternatives and competitors: Interpretivist research (often grounded in phenomenology) focuses more on meaning and lived experience. Critical research (radical humanism/structuralism) foregrounds power and ideology. Process theories emphasize sequences and mechanisms over static variable relationships.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use a positivism discussion when the question is about "what counts as evidence" or when you need to defend a causal claim and your identification strategy. Example research question: "Do mandatory breach notification laws reduce breach incidents, and through what mechanisms?" Example hypothesis: "Stronger regulatory pressure is associated with a lower rate of breaches in the affected firms." What I would measure (simple): I would define a clear outcome (breach incidents), measure regulatory change as a policy shock, and use a quasi-experimental design (difference-in-differences) with robustness checks for pre-trends and selection. Seminal APA citation: Goles, T., & Hirschheim, R. (2000). The paradigm is dead, the paradigm is dead... long live the paradigm. Omega, 28(3), 249-268.

🔗 Connections to Other Paradigms
Positivism and Interpretivism: Positivism assumes reality is objective and knowable through empirical observation. Interpretivism (grounded in phenomenology) assumes meaning is constructed and contextual. In IS comps, Burrell and Morgan (1979) framework positions these as opposite paradigms on the subjective-objective axis. The critical exam move is not to claim one is "right," but to acknowledge when each applies: use positivism for causal prediction, interpretivism for meaning and experience.
Positivism and Critical Research: Positivism emphasizes objectivity and neutrality. Critical research (radical humanism and structuralism) argues that neutrality is impossible - knowledge always reflects power. In IS exams, you can argue: "While positivism aims for objectivity, critical research rightly points out that what we choose to study and measure reflects values. A mature positivist approach acknowledges this while maintaining rigor."
📖 Seminal References and Citations
Core reference: Goles, T., & Hirschheim, R. (2000). The paradigm is dead, the paradigm is dead... long live the paradigm: The legacy of Burrell and Morgan. Omega, 28(3), 249-268. This is the canonical IS reference for positivism and paradigm debates. Critical point: In IS, positivism is often discussed NOT as a specific "theory" but as a research paradigm that shapes assumptions about knowledge, methods, and rigor. Lee and Baskerville (2003, MIS Quarterly) offer an updated discussion of positivist vs. qualitative research in IS, useful for showing how positivism remains dominant despite paradigm debates.
🎯 Boundary Conditions and Common Mistakes
When positivism shines: Use positivism when you have stable, measurable constructs and want to estimate causal effects or make predictions. Example: "Does system quality affect adoption?" Measure quality (system response time, data accuracy), measure adoption (system usage, satisfaction), test the relationship.
When positivism struggles: Avoid positivism when constructs are meaning-dependent or constantly changing. Example: "What does 'digital transformation' mean to organizations?" This needs interpretive or critical lenses, not measurement scales.
Common exam mistake: Treating positivism as synonymous with "good research." Positivism is one paradigm; it is powerful for certain questions (prediction, causal effects) but limited for others (meaning, lived experience, power dynamics). Show sophistication by defending positivist choices when appropriate and acknowledging when other paradigms are better.
Philosophy Husserl, 1913

Phenomenology

Understanding how people experience and make meaning from phenomena in their lived worlds

Core Concept: Phenomenology privileges subjective lived experience and intentionality as the foundation for understanding human phenomena. Rather than reducing experience to variables too early, it asks: "How is this phenomenon experienced and made meaningful by people?"

🧠 Lived Experience: The focus on how people actually encounter the world in their daily lives, not abstract concepts
🔍 Intentionality: Consciousness is always directed toward something; meaning emerges through this directedness
📍 Context & Interpretation: Meaning is contextual and interpretive, not universal or predetermined
🎯 Sensemaking: Understanding requires describing how people make sense of phenomena, especially when constructs are unstable or new
IS Relevance: Phenomenology is foundational for interpretive IS research that examines digital experience, meaning-making, and identity in new work contexts. It guards against forcing emerging phenomena into pre-existing theoretical boxes.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Husserl's early work in phenomenology (often associated with Ideas and related writings) established the focus on lived experience and consciousness. In IS, phenomenology is usually a foundation for interpretive research rather than a cited causal theory.
Discipline of origin Philosophy.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Meta (not a theory type). Phenomenology shapes how we study phenomena, not a predictive model.
Core constructs Lived experience, meaning, intentionality, context, interpretation.
Mechanism (plain) You try to understand a phenomenon by describing how it is experienced and made meaningful by people, rather than reducing it to variables too early.
Boundary conditions Best for questions about sensemaking, identity, experience, and meaning, especially when constructs are not stable or not well-defined. Less useful when the goal is prediction or estimating causal effects.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Phenomenology shows up indirectly in interpretive IS traditions (experience-centric studies of work, digital practice, and transformation), but it is rarely named as the explicit "theory" in top-journal empirical papers. (More a philosophical grounding than a citation lens.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Phenomenology begins with the idea that we should take subjective experience seriously. Instead of assuming we already know what a concept means (like "trust" or "control"), phenomenology asks how people actually experience these ideas in their daily lives. That matters in IS because many digital phenomena are experienced differently across roles, contexts, and histories.

💎 Theoretical Core

Phenomenology is not built around propositions like a variable model. Its "core" is a commitment: meaning comes from how people encounter the world. For IS, the exam move is to connect this to research questions that are about interpretation, not just behavior. Boundary conditions are important. If the exam question is asking for prediction ("what increases adoption?"), phenomenology is not the best primary answer. But if the question is "how do users experience algorithmic control?" or "what does dignity mean in AI use?", phenomenology is a strong grounding.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS researchers often use phenomenology as background for interpretive methods (interviews, ethnography) and for analysis that stays close to experience. In a comps answer, you can position phenomenology as a counterweight to purely instrumental models: it helps you keep the phenomenon human.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: It helps you avoid forcing new digital phenomena into old boxes. It encourages deep description and careful interpretation, which is valuable when technologies change fast. Weaknesses: It can be criticized for limited generalizability and for being hard to evaluate with the same criteria as positivist work. It also requires strong methodological discipline to avoid turning into storytelling. Common misapplications in IS: Using "phenomenology" as a label without doing the hard work of experience-based analysis, or making causal claims without a design that supports them. Alternatives and competitors: Positivist approaches for causal estimation, critical approaches for power and ideology, and process theorizing when you want sequences of events plus mechanism.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use phenomenology when the exam question is about meaning, experience, or identity, especially in new digital work settings. Example research question: "How do experienced professionals experience the introduction of substitutive AI tools in their daily work?" Example hypothesis: Phenomenology usually does not produce classic hypotheses. A better exam move is to state an expected pattern: "Professionals will describe AI as threatening when it challenges what they see as the core of their expertise." What I would measure (simple): I would not start with a survey scale. I would collect interview or diary data about work episodes, then code for recurring meanings (threat, loss of autonomy, pride, relief) and link them to specific AI features and organizational practices. Seminal APA citation: Husserl, E. (1931). Ideas: General introduction to pure phenomenology. George Allen & Unwin.

🔗 Connections to Other Traditions
Phenomenology and Interpretivism: Phenomenology is foundational for interpretive research, but they are not synonymous. Interpretivism is broader: it includes any research that focuses on meaning and understanding. Phenomenology specifically privileges lived experience and how consciousness encounters phenomena. In IS exams, show the distinction: you can do interpretive research (e.g., discourse analysis) without being explicitly phenomenological, but phenomenology requires attention to first-person experience.
Phenomenology and Positivism: These are opposite on the Burrell & Morgan paradigm map. Positivism assumes objective reality and universal laws; phenomenology assumes meaning is contextual and experience-dependent. In IS comps, you might argue: "Phenomenology is essential for studying emerging digital phenomena where constructs are unstable. Positivism works once we have stable, agreed-upon constructs." This shows you understand when each applies.
📖 Key References and Interpretive Grounding
Foundational texts: Husserl, E. (1931). Ideas: General introduction to pure phenomenology. George Allen & Unwin. In IS, phenomenology is rarely cited as the main "theory" but rather as philosophical grounding for interpretive methods. Van Manen, M. (1990). Researching lived experience. State University of New York Press is more practical for IS researchers. Critical point: Heidegger, M. (1962). Being and time emphasizes how technology is experienced as present-at-hand vs. ready-to-hand, which is valuable for understanding digital artifacts and work practices.
🎯 When Phenomenology Shines vs. Struggles in Exams
Shines when: The exam asks about meaning-making, lived experience, or how people encounter and make sense of new technologies. Example: "How do knowledge workers experience algorithmic management?" Deploy phenomenology: "I would conduct interviews focusing on how workers describe their daily work, what they feel the algorithms value, and how this shapes their sense of competence and autonomy."
Struggles when: The exam asks for prediction, causal effects, or generalizable patterns across large populations. Example: "What factors increase AI adoption?" Phenomenology is weak here; positivism or behavioral economics is stronger.
Exam trap: Do not claim phenomenology "generates no hypotheses." Phenomenology generates expected patterns of meaning, not statistical hypotheses. In your exam answer, articulate what you expect people will report experiencing, then show how you would systematically observe and analyze that experience.
Philosophy Burrell & Morgan (1979)

Radical Humanism (Burrell and Morgan)

How ideology, consciousness, and domination shape digital life and opportunities for emancipatory change

Core Concept: People are often trapped not just by material structures but by ideology and distorted consciousness about what is normal. Digital systems reinforce these ideological traps by shaping what users see, believe is possible, and accept as inevitable.

🧠 Ideology & Consciousness: Digital systems encode beliefs about what is "normal" or "necessary," limiting what people perceive as possible
⚖️ Domination via Meaning: Power operates through shared but contested interpretations, not just objective constraints
✊ Emancipation through Critique: Understanding ideological structures is the first step toward collective action for change
🔍 Radical Change Imperative: The goal is not adaptation or fit but transformation of oppressive arrangements
IS Relevance: Critical and emancipatory IS research uses this lens to study surveillance, algorithmic management, digital labor, and workplace control. It reveals how digital systems can reinforce domination through ideology.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Burrell and Morgan (1979), Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Key contribution: a map of paradigms based on subjective vs objective assumptions and regulation vs radical change.
Discipline of origin Sociology / organization studies (paradigm theory).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Meta (not a theory type). This is a paradigm lens.
Core constructs Subjective reality, ideology, emancipation, consciousness, critique.
Mechanism (plain) You explain social outcomes by looking at how beliefs and ideologies shape what people see as normal, and how that limits freedom.
Boundary conditions Best for research questions about domination, ideology, and emancipation in digital life (surveillance, algorithmic control, workplace resistance). Less useful for narrow prediction questions.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Critical and emancipatory IS work continues (especially around digital responsibility, ethics, and AI harms), but it is not always labeled as "radical humanism." It often appears as critical theory or emancipatory critique.
📚 Foundational Origins

Burrell and Morgan's framework is about paradigms, not about one phenomenon. Radical humanism sits on the "subjective" side and the "radical change" side. The key idea is that people can be dominated not only by material structures but also by ideology and taken-for-granted meanings. That is why radical humanism cares about consciousness and emancipation.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that social arrangements can trap people through ideology and distorted consciousness. Digital systems can participate in that trap by shaping what users see, what they believe is possible, and what they accept as normal. The mechanism is critique. You look for how digital practices reproduce domination (for example, workers internalizing metric pressure) and how alternative interpretations and collective action can create emancipation. Boundary conditions are practical. This lens is powerful when there is a clear power issue, but it can be rejected as "too normative" if you do not connect the critique to concrete practices and evidence.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, this paradigm shows up through critical approaches that study surveillance, digital labor, algorithmic management, and harm. It connects naturally to dignity and digital responsibility conversations.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: It helps you see power in places where mainstream models treat technology as neutral. It gives you language for harms, dignity, and emancipation. Weaknesses: It can drift into moral statements without clear empirical grounding. Reviewers may push back if the paper does not show "so what" beyond critique. Common misapplications in IS: Using critical language without connecting it to real organizational practices, or presenting a critique but offering no plausible path to change. Alternatives and competitors: Radical structuralism focuses more on objective structures (institutions, class, material constraints). Interpretive research focuses on meaning without necessarily taking an emancipatory stance.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use radical humanism when the exam question involves ideology, meaning, and emancipation in digital systems (surveillance, AI control, platform labor). Example research question: "How do algorithmic performance systems shape how workers define 'good work' and 'self-worth' over time?" Example hypothesis: This lens often avoids classic hypotheses. A good exam move is a claim: "Algorithmic metrics can become internalized as moral judgments, which can reduce workers' sense of autonomy and dignity." What I would measure (simple): Interviews and ethnographic observation of work practices, plus artifacts (dashboards, ranking screens, policy documents). Then analyze how meanings and justifications change. Seminal APA citation: Burrell, G., & Morgan, G. (1979). Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Heinemann.

🔗 Connections to Other Critical Perspectives
Radical Humanism vs. Radical Structuralism: Both are "radical change" perspectives, but they differ on how domination works. Radical humanism focuses on ideology and consciousness - people are trapped by false beliefs about what is normal. Radical structuralism focuses on objective power structures - people are constrained by material and institutional forces, regardless of their beliefs. In IS exams, you might argue: "Algorithmic management systems combine both: they constrain workers through objective metrics (radical structuralism) AND shape how workers internalize those constraints as moral values (radical humanism)."
Radical Humanism and Phenomenology: Both privilege lived experience and meaning. Phenomenology asks: "How do people experience phenomena?" Radical humanism adds: "How does ideology distort that experience?" In IS research, you can combine them: use phenomenological methods (interviews) to uncover lived experience, then apply critical analysis to show how that experience is shaped by ideological forces (what workers accept as normal or inevitable).
📖 Key Citations and Critical Theory Grounding
Foundational reference: Burrell, G., & Morgan, G. (1979). Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Heinemann. This is the canonical source for the Burrell & Morgan framework that positions radical humanism. In IS, critical point: Zuboff, S. (2019). The age of surveillance capitalism. Public Affairs is a contemporary application showing how digital systems encode ideology. Also cite Braverman, H. (1974). Labor and monopoly capital for structural analysis of deskilling and workplace control.
🎯 Exam Strategy: Using Radical Humanism to Strengthen Arguments
Deploy when: The exam question asks about power, ideology, harm, or emancipation in digital systems. Example: "How do platform algorithms affect worker agency and dignity?" Answer with radical humanism: "Platforms use algorithms to measure and rank worker performance, but workers internalize these metrics as measures of their self-worth. This ideological trap - accepting platform rankings as objective truth - reduces their sense of autonomy even when they technically have choices. Emancipatory approaches require helping workers recognize this ideology."
Strengthen with evidence: Radical humanism requires empirical grounding. Show: (1) What ideology workers accept (e.g., "metrics measure quality"), (2) Evidence it is ideology (e.g., workers acknowledge metrics are incomplete but still internalize them), (3) Consequences (reduced autonomy, stress), (4) Paths to emancipation (collective bargaining, transparency, alternative measures).
Avoid the normative trap: Radical humanism can sound preachy. In exams, ground your critique in concrete IS practices. Do not just claim "this system oppresses workers" - show the mechanism: how the ideology gets constructed, transmitted, and internalized. This makes your argument stronger and harder to dismiss as merely moral.
Sociology Burrell & Morgan (1979)

Radical Structuralism (Burrell and Morgan)

How structural power relations and material constraints drive conflict and radical transformation

Core Concept: Social outcomes are shaped primarily by objective structural power relations, not individual beliefs. Real change comes through conflict when structural pressures and contradictions force transformation, not through adaptation or consensus.

🏗️ Objective Structures: Material and institutional constraints are real and operate independently of individual awareness
⚔️ Conflict as Change Driver: Transformation happens through structural pressures and conflicts, not voluntary adoption
💰 Power & Inequality: Structural arrangements benefit some groups and disadvantage others; ask "who wins and who loses?"
🔄 Structural Contradictions: Systems contain internal contradictions that force change when pressures become unbearable
IS Relevance: Useful for understanding platform power dynamics, labor control, regulatory capture, and why digital reforms often fail despite good intentions. Structural incentives override individual motivation.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Burrell and Morgan (1979), Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Radical structuralism is the objective + radical change quadrant.
Discipline of origin Sociology / organization studies (paradigm theory).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Meta (not a theory type).
Core constructs Objective structures, power, conflict, material constraints, transformation.
Mechanism (plain) You explain outcomes by focusing on structural power relations (not only individual beliefs), and you ask how conflicts drive change.
Boundary conditions Best for research questions where structures clearly matter (labor markets, regulation, platform power, inequality). Less useful for micro-level experience questions.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Shows up indirectly in work on platform power, data governance, and digital responsibility, but the label "radical structuralism" is not common in IS papers.
📚 Foundational Origins

Radical structuralism shares the "radical change" goal, but it is more objective than radical humanism. It treats structures as real constraints that shape behavior, conflict, and inequality. Digital systems can reinforce or shift these structures (think of platform monopolies, labor control, and regulatory capture).

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that social outcomes are shaped by structural power relations, and real change often comes through conflict. The mechanism is not "people change their minds." It is "structures create pressures and conflicts, and these conflicts force transformation." In IS, this can be used to explain why some digital reforms fail: because structural incentives and power relations block them, even if individuals have good intentions.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS research uses related structural lenses (institutional theory, political economy, critical realism) more often than explicitly calling it radical structuralism. But the exam value is the contrast: if institutional theory explains legitimacy pressures, radical structuralism pushes you to ask: who benefits from the institutional arrangement, and who loses?

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: It keeps you honest about power and constraints. It is a good antidote to "managerial optimism" stories about digital transformation. Weaknesses: It can become deterministic if you ignore agency and local practice. It can also be hard to operationalize if you stay at a very abstract level. Common misapplications in IS: Treating every outcome as oppression without careful evidence, or ignoring the micro-level processes through which structures are enacted. Alternatives and competitors: Institutional theory for legitimacy and isomorphism, sociomateriality for practice-level entanglement, and radical humanism for ideology and meaning.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use radical structuralism when the question is about power structures, inequality, and conflict in digital systems (platform dominance, surveillance capitalism, labor control). Example research question: "How do platform governance rules shift bargaining power between platforms and complementors over time?" Example hypothesis: "When a platform tightens control over APIs and data access, complementors' dependence increases and their strategic options narrow." What I would measure (simple): Platform policy changes, API access restrictions, complementor entry/exit, revenue dependence, and qualitative evidence of conflict (public statements, lawsuits, policy debates). Seminal APA citation: Burrell, G., & Morgan, G. (1979). Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Heinemann.

🔗 Connections to Institutional Theory and Political Economy
Radical Structuralism vs. Institutional Theory: Both examine how structures shape behavior, but with different lenses. Institutional theory focuses on legitimacy and isomorphism - why organizations become similar through conforming to institutional pressures. Radical structuralism focuses on power and conflict - whose interests are served and who loses. In IS exams: "Institutional theory explains why many companies adopt similar data governance policies (legitimacy pressures). But radical structuralism asks: who benefits from those policies? Do they serve workers or primarily protect firms?"
Radical Structuralism and Radical Humanism: Both seek radical change, but structuralism emphasizes objective material constraints (capital, labor markets, platform architectures) while humanism emphasizes ideology and consciousness. Example: "Platform dependency on gig workers is a structural fact (radical structuralism). But workers' acceptance of low pay as 'flexibility' is ideological (radical humanism). Real change requires addressing both."
📖 Key References: Political Economy and Structural Analysis
Core citations: Burrell, G., & Morgan, G. (1979). Sociological paradigms and organisational analysis. Heinemann. For IS-specific applications: Srnicek, N. (2017). Platform capitalism. Polity Press shows how platform structures concentrate capital and power. Critical point: Zuboff, S. (2019). The age of surveillance capitalism demonstrates structural mechanisms through which platforms extract and monetize data - good evidence for radical structuralist arguments about architecture and power.
🎯 When to Deploy Radical Structuralism in Exams
Deploy when: The exam asks about structural power, inequality, or why well-intentioned reforms fail. Example: "Why do many companies struggle to implement ethical AI despite genuine commitment?" Radical structuralism answer: "Because structural incentives (shareholder pressure, competitive dynamics, data advantages) override individual commitment. Firms benefit from using data intensively and opaquely; ethical constraints reduce profitability. Real change requires structural reform (regulation, antitrust) not just individual conscience."
Strengthen with specificity: Do not make vague claims about "capitalist structures." Show the mechanism: (1) How is profit extracted? (2) Who controls the assets and rules? (3) What conflicts arise? (4) What would change the structure? Example: "Platform architectures (centralized control, opaque algorithms, network effects) create structural advantages for platforms over workers. Workers lack information, alternatives, and collective power. Structural change requires interoperability, transparency mandates, or labor organization."
Avoid determinism: Radical structuralism is NOT fatalism. Structures shape possibilities, but people navigate and resist within those constraints. In exams, show: (1) What the structure enables/constrains, (2) How people resist, (3) What transformations are possible. This avoids sounding like "the system always wins."
Philosophy Freire (1970)

Emancipatory Pedagogy

How dialogue, critical reflection, and collective action enable people to resist domination and control their lives

Core Concept: Liberation requires praxis - critical reflection on one's situation combined with collective action to change it. The "banking" model of passive knowledge transfer reproduces domination, while dialogue and co-creation support conscientization (critical consciousness) and emancipation.

💬 Dialogue & Co-creation: Knowledge is created through dialogue between equals, not deposited from expert to passive learner
🧠 Conscientization: Critical reflection on one's world that reveals domination and opens possibilities for change
⚙️ Praxis: Reflection plus action - not just thinking about problems, but taking steps to solve them
🔓 Literacy & Agency: Understanding how systems work (algorithmic literacy, digital literacy) is necessary for resistance and meaningful participation
IS Relevance: Used in ICT4D, digital inclusion, algorithmic literacy initiatives, and participatory design research. Guides interventions that build critical understanding rather than passive adoption of technology.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Freire (1970), Pedagogy of the Oppressed. Key contribution: education should be dialogical and liberating, not a "banking" model of depositing knowledge.
Discipline of origin Education / critical theory.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type V (design and action) when used as a guide for intervention, and Type II when used as a critique of domination.
Core constructs Oppression, dialogue, conscientization, praxis (reflection + action), liberation.
Mechanism (plain) People become able to change their situation when they critically reflect on it and act together, instead of passively accepting imposed meanings.
Boundary conditions Best for contexts where learning, empowerment, and inequality are central (digital inclusion, ICT4D, worker training, algorithmic literacy). Less useful for narrow predictive questions.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Most often appears indirectly in ICT4D and critical digital inclusion research. In top IS outlets, it is more common as an underlying stance than a named theory. (Use as a critical lens, and be explicit about the intervention.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Freire wrote for contexts of oppression and unequal power, where education can either reproduce domination or support liberation. The "banking" model treats learners as empty containers. Freire argues for dialogue where learners and teachers co-create knowledge and build critical consciousness.

💎 Theoretical Core

The core proposition is that liberation requires praxis: reflection plus action. In digital contexts, this translates to questions about who controls technology, who understands it, and who has the ability to contest it. A simple example is algorithmic literacy: if people cannot understand how a system shapes them, it is hard to resist harm. Boundary conditions matter. This lens is not a plug-in variable model. It is a critical and action-oriented stance. If you use it, you should be ready to talk about participation, dialogue, and change, not only description.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, emancipatory ideas show up in digital inclusion work, participatory design, and critical perspectives on technology. It also connects to dignity: emancipation is partly about protecting and restoring human dignity in systems that can dehumanize.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: It gives a strong ethical and action orientation. It helps you link research to real change, not only explanation. Weaknesses: It can be criticized for being normative and hard to evaluate with standard positivist criteria. It also requires careful attention to whose voice is included and whose is missing. Common misapplications in IS: Using emancipatory language while still designing top-down interventions, or treating "participation" as a checkbox without real power sharing. Alternatives and competitors: Critical theory traditions, participatory design, and dignity-focused approaches like CARE when the focus is on affronts and responses.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use emancipatory pedagogy when the exam question involves empowerment, digital inclusion, or designing interventions that reduce harm and inequality. Example research question: "How can organizations design AI training so employees can question and reshape AI work practices instead of only learning compliance?" Example hypothesis: "Training programs that include dialogue and reflection will increase employees' perceived agency and reduce blind reliance on AI outputs." What I would measure (simple): Pre/post measures of perceived agency and critical awareness, qualitative evidence from workshops, and behavioral indicators (raising concerns, proposing changes, contesting AI decisions). Seminal APA citation: Freire, P. (1970). Pedagogy of the oppressed. Herder and Herder.

🔗 Connections to Critical Pedagogy and Participatory Design
Emancipatory Pedagogy and Radical Humanism/Structuralism: All three are critical perspectives, but with different focuses. Radical humanism and structuralism are about analyzing domination. Emancipatory pedagogy is about designing and facilitating change through education and dialogue. In IS exams: "I could diagnose surveillance capitalism as a structural problem (radical structuralism), but to address it requires helping users understand algorithmic systems and collectively decide how to use them - that is where emancipatory pedagogy comes in."
Emancipatory Pedagogy and Participatory Design: Both emphasize dialogue and co-creation, but participatory design is often more about involving users in design decisions, while emancipatory pedagogy emphasizes critical consciousness and collective action. In IS research: "Participatory design gets users involved. Emancipatory pedagogy asks: Are users critically reflecting on power and alternatives, or just providing input within predetermined options?"
📖 Key Citations and Emancipatory Theory
Core reference: Freire, P. (1970). Pedagogy of the oppressed. Herder and Herder. In IS, critical point: The distinction between "banking" education (expert deposits knowledge in passive learners) and dialogue-based education matters. Also cite Freire, P. (1973). Education for critical consciousness and hooks, b. (1994). Teaching to transgress: Education as the practice of freedom for feminist perspectives on emancipatory teaching. These show how emancipatory pedagogy has evolved beyond Freire's original formulation.
🎯 Exam Strategy: Deploying Emancipatory Pedagogy
Deploy when: The exam asks about education, digital literacy, user empowerment, or designing interventions for digital inclusion. Example: "How should organizations teach workers about algorithmic decision-making?" Emancipatory answer: "Not through lectures (banking model). Instead, create opportunities for workers to: (1) Examine actual algorithms and decisions affecting them, (2) Reflect critically on whose interests are served, (3) Propose alternatives and test them collectively. This builds conscientization and agency."
Strengthen through praxis: Do not just describe pedagogy - show the action component. Show: (1) What is being taught/reflected on, (2) How dialogue and co-creation happen, (3) What collective action or change results. Example: "Training employees to understand algorithmic systems is incomplete without supporting them to contest harmful outputs. Praxis means reflection AND action (raising concerns, proposing changes)."
Common mistake in exams: Treating "participation" as sufficient for emancipation. True emancipatory pedagogy requires critical consciousness - people understanding WHY systems work as they do and WHAT alternatives exist. Merely involving users in decisions without building their critical understanding falls short. Show in your exam answer that you understand this distinction.
⚠️ IS Application: From Theory to Practice
Emancipatory pedagogy in IS education: How IS teachers and IS researchers support critical understanding. Example: Instead of teaching "how to implement AI," teach "how AI systems encode choices and whose interests they serve." This helps students and practitioners become critical users, not passive adopters.
Emancipatory pedagogy in digital inclusion: ICT4D initiatives that build critical digital literacy help people understand and contest surveillance, algorithmic bias, and platform control. In exams, connect pedagogy to concrete outcomes: Does the intervention increase perceived agency? Does it lead to collective action or change?

🧠 Part 0C: Meta-Theory & Research Craft

Frameworks about how to build, evaluate, and write theory

7 Frameworks

📖 What This Section Covers

این بخش درباره «متاتئوری» است - یعنی تئوری درباره خودِ تئوری‌ها. قبل از اینکه بری سراغ TAM یا Trust Theory یا هر تئوری دیگه، باید بدونی اصلاً یک تئوری خوب چه شکلیه و چطور نقدش کنی.

چهار عنصر Whetten (1989): هر تئوری کامل باید چهار چیز داشته باشه: What (مفاهیم و متغیرها چی هستن؟)، How (روابط بینشون چیه؟)، Why (مکانیزم زیرینش چیه؟ چرا این اتفاق می‌افته؟)، و Who/Where/When (شرایط مرزی - کجا کار می‌کنه و کجا نه؟). خیلی از مقالات ضعیف فقط What و How دارن ولی Why رو جا می‌ندازن.

پنج نوع تئوری Gregor (2006): Type I (تحلیل و توصیف)، Type II (توضیح - چرا اتفاق افتاد؟)، Type III (پیش‌بینی - چه اتفاقی می‌افته؟)، Type IV (توضیح + پیش‌بینی - هم چرا و هم چه خواهد شد)، Type V (طراحی - DSR). برای امتحان مهمه بتونی بگی هر مقاله‌ای که می‌خونی کدوم Type هست.

Variance vs. Process: تئوری‌های Variance با متغیرها و رگرسیون کار می‌کنن (مثل TAM)، ولی تئوری‌های Process داستان و توالی رویدادها رو توضیح میدن. این تفاوت در امتحان جامع زیاد پرسیده میشه.

چرا برای comps مهمه؟ در امتحان جامع مستقیماً ازت می‌پرسن: «آیا این مقاله تئوری خوبی ارائه میده؟ عناصرش کامله؟ چه نوع تئوری‌ای هست؟» بدون متاتئوری فقط می‌تونی تئوری‌ها رو حفظ کنی ولی نمی‌تونی نقدشون کنی - و دقیقاً نقد کردن چیزیه که امتحان دکتری انتظار داره.

Meta-theory literally means "theory about theories" - a higher level of thinking where instead of studying real-world phenomena, we examine the theories themselves. This section gives you the analytical tools and frameworks to evaluate existing theories, build new ones, and provide scholarly critiques. It covers the foundational works of Whetten (complete theory elements), Gregor (five types of theory in IS), Markus & Robey (Variance vs. Process theories), and frameworks for evaluating research quality (novelty, rigor, and relevance).

Why this is critical for the comprehensive exam: Many comp exam questions directly ask you to evaluate a study from a theoretical perspective - for example, does this study present a "good theory"? Are the theory elements complete? What type of theory is it (explanatory, predictive, design)? Without meta-theory, you can memorize theories but cannot analyze and critique them - which is exactly what the PhD exam expects.
Key concepts you should master:
  • Complete theory elements (Whetten): What (concepts and variables), How (relationships), Why (underlying mechanisms), Who/Where/When (boundary conditions)
  • Gregor's five theory types: Analysis & Description (Type I), Explanation (Type II), Prediction (Type III), Explanation & Prediction (Type IV), Design & Action (Type V - DSR)
  • Variance vs. Process: Variance theories examine variables and statistical relationships (e.g., regression), while Process theories explain narratives and event sequences
  • Research quality evaluation: The triad of novelty (originality), rigor (methodological soundness), and relevance (practical applicability)
  • Review and critique frameworks: How to review papers, identify theoretical strengths and weaknesses, and suggest scholarly improvements

🗺️ Meta-Theory Connection Map

How meta-frameworks relate to each other in building and evaluating theory

Theory Structure
  • Whetten (1989) - What, How, Why, Boundaries
  • Gregor (2006) - 5 Types of Theory
  • Markus & Robey (1988) - Causal Structure
Modern Challenges
  • Burton-Jones (2021) - Next-Gen IS Theorizing
  • Sun et al. (2025) - Novelty-Rigor-Relevance
Practical Writing
  • Baird (2021) - Writing IS Papers
  • Sarker et al. (2023) - Reviewing Framework

🔗 Key Relationships

Whetten → Gregor Whetten's elements map to Gregor's types - a Type IV (Explanation) needs strong "Why" (causal logic); Type V (Design) emphasizes "How" (principles)
Markus & Robey → Gregor Causal structure differentiates Type III vs IV: variance models vs process models require different temporal reasoning
Burton-Jones ⚡ Whetten Updates "Why" for mechanism clarity - modern reviewers expect explicit, testable mechanisms, not just correlations
Sarker + Baird q-r framework (questions-responses) operationalizes Baird's writing advice into reviewer dialogue simulation
Sun et al. ↔ Whetten Novelty-Rigor-Relevance triad complements Whetten by asking "So what?" alongside "What/How/Why"

🎯 Theory Application Examples

Diagnosing an existing theory: Use Whetten to check completeness. Example: "Evaluating Technology Acceptance Model - What (constructs)? ✓ Has PU, PEOU. How (relationships)? ✓ Direct effects specified. Why (mechanism)? ⚠️ Weak - lacks psychological explanation for why ease matters. Boundaries? ⚠️ Originally voluntary adoption only."
Classifying your contribution: Use Gregor. Example: "I'm proposing that blockchain governance reduces agency costs through immutability. This is Type IV (Explanation) - testable predictions about what happens and why, but not yet prescriptive design principles."
Defending causal logic: Use Markus & Robey. Example: "My theory assumes variance logic (more transparency → more trust), but I should consider process alternatives: Does transparency enable trust-building events, or does it set boundary conditions for trust calibration over time?"
Justifying novelty: Use Burton-Jones or Sun et al. Example: "My contribution is novel because it specifies a new mechanism (XYZ) that prior work assumed but never tested. This addresses Sun et al.'s novelty dimension while maintaining rigor through falsifiable predictions."

🔗 Cross-References to Substantive Theories

Whetten → Trust Theory: Check if trust research specifies "What" (ability, benevolence, integrity), "How" (multiplicative? additive?), "Why" (vulnerability reduction), and "Boundaries" (does it apply to AI trust?).

Gregor → TAM: TAM is Type III (Prediction) but aspires to be Type IV (Explanation). The "Why" (cognitive ease reduces effort) is present but often underspecified.

Markus & Robey → Structuration: Structuration Theory uses process causality - structures emerge from recursive interaction, not linear cause-effect.

📝 Exam Usage Guide & Scenarios

Quick diagnosis toolkit: Whetten for completeness → Gregor for type → Markus & Robey for causal structure → Burton-Jones for mechanism quality → Sun et al. for contribution strength.

Scenario 1: "Evaluate this theory's contribution"
Answer structure: (1) Use Whetten to check What/How/Why/Boundaries. (2) Use Gregor to classify type. (3) Use Sun et al. to assess novelty-rigor-relevance. (4) Identify gaps and suggest improvements. ~100 words total.
Scenario 2: "How would you improve TAM for AI contexts?"
Answer structure: (1) Use Burton-Jones to argue TAM's "Why" lacks mechanism detail. (2) Use Gregor to note TAM is Type III (prediction) but needs Type IV (explanation) for AI's unique properties. (3) Use Whetten boundaries to argue original context (voluntary, simple tools) doesn't transfer to AI. (4) Propose AI-specific mechanisms.
Scenario 3: "Distinguish process vs variance theories"
Answer structure: (1) Use Markus & Robey's causal structure framework. (2) Variance = correlational, cross-sectional, "more X → more Y". (3) Process = temporal, sequential, "X enables Y which triggers Z". (4) Example: Variance TAM vs Process Innovation Diffusion. (5) Note both are valid but require different methods.
Meta-Framework Whetten (1989) Core for comps

What Constitutes a Theory (Whetten, 1989)

A diagnostic framework for evaluating whether theoretical contributions are complete and well-specified

Core Concept: Complete theories answer four interconnected questions: What (constructs), How (relationships), Why (causal logic), and Who/Where/When (boundary conditions). Missing or weak elements indicate incomplete theorizing.

🔍 WHAT: Which constructs and variables belong in the theory? Are any missing or redundant?
🔗 HOW: What are the relationships among constructs? How do they connect?
❓ WHY: What is the underlying causal logic? Why should these relationships exist?
📍 BOUNDARIES: When does the theory apply, and when does it break down? For whom, where, and when?
IS Relevance: Essential for exam preparation and evaluating theory contributions in IS research. Provides a fast diagnostic checklist for completeness, helping you think like a reviewer.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Whetten (1989), "What Constitutes a Theoretical Contribution?" Academy of Management Review. Key contribution: defines what makes a theoretical contribution complete (what, how, why, who/where/when).
Discipline of origin Management/organizational theory (meta-theory).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed, but universally used in IS for evaluating theory contributions.
Gregor (2006) type Meta-framework (Type I analysis). Not a theory of a phenomenon, but guidance for theory building.
Core constructs What (constructs/variables), How (relationships), Why (causal logic), Who/Where/When (boundary conditions), comprehensiveness vs parsimony.
Mechanism (plain) A complete theory needs: what factors, how they relate, why those relationships exist, and under what conditions. Missing any element weakens contribution.
Boundary conditions This is a framework for evaluating contributions, not a model to test empirically.
📚 Foundational Origins

Whetten wrote this as an AMR guide for reviewers and authors. The problem he addressed is simple: many papers claim theoretical contribution but do not clearly articulate what makes the contribution theoretical. His solution is to break theory into building blocks (what, how, why, boundaries) and show what "adding value" looks like at each level.

💎 Theoretical Core
Whetten's framework provides four essential questions every theory must answer

1️⃣ WHAT

Which constructs and variables should be included? Are any missing or redundant?

2️⃣ HOW

What are the relationships among constructs? Arrows, correlations, processes?

3️⃣ WHY

What is the underlying causal logic? Why should we expect these relationships?

4️⃣ WHO/WHERE/WHEN

What are the boundary conditions? When does the theory apply, and when does it break down?

If a comps question asks "evaluate this theory," run through these four elements systematically. If any are weak or missing, you have a structured critique.

Extended from BCIS 6670 StudyGuide:

Whetten's 7 Reviewer Questions are exam-gold:
  • "What's new?" - Novelty of contribution
  • "So what?" - Significance and impact
  • "Why so?" - Theoretical justification
  • "Well done?" - Design quality
  • "Done well?" - Execution quality
  • "Why now?" - Timeliness and relevance
  • "Who cares?" - Target audience

Incremental vs Radical Contributions: Whetten distinguishes incremental (improving existing theory) from radical (challenging foundational assumptions). This helps frame novelty precisely, especially alongside Sun et al. (2025).

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Whetten is widely cited in IS for the same reason it is cited in management: it gives a fast checklist for completeness. In BCIS 6670, Whetten pairs with Gregor (2006) for type of theory, Markus & Robey (1988) for causal structure, and Burton-Jones et al. (2021) for digital-specific theorizing moves.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

✅ Strengths:

  • Simple, memorable, fast diagnostic tool
  • Works across paradigms
  • Helps avoid incomplete theorizing

❌ Weaknesses:

  • Can feel like a checklist rather than real theorizing if applied mechanically
  • Boundary conditions ("who/where/when") often weakest in published work

🔄 Common Misapplications:

  • Treating it as a template to fill in rather than critical thinking tool
  • Weak "why" sections that only restate relationships without causal mechanisms

🔗 Alternatives/Competitors:

  • Gregor (2006) for theory types
  • Sutton & Staw for "what theory is not"
  • Burton-Jones et al. for digital theorizing shifts
🎯 Practical Application for Exam
When a question asks "critique this theory" or "what is missing," apply Whetten's four elements systematically.

Example Research Question: "What theoretical elements are needed to explain AI delegation as a sociotechnical governance mechanism?"

What I would measure in comps:
  1. WHAT constructs: delegation willingness, AI capability, accountability structure
  2. HOW they relate: feedback loops, appraisal-driven updates
  3. WHY: delegation requires authority + trust + capability assessment
  4. BOUNDARIES: works for agentic AI in enterprise settings, less clear for fully autonomous systems

Seminal APA citation: Whetten, D. A. (1989). What constitutes a theoretical contribution? Academy of Management Review, 14(4), 490-495.

IS-Native Gregor (2006) Core for comps

Theory Types (Gregor, 2006)

A taxonomy organizing theories by primary goal and components, helping authors communicate their theoretical contribution

Core Concept: Different research purposes require different theory types, each with distinct primary goals and components. Matching your theory type to your research goal improves clarity and helps reviewers apply appropriate evaluation criteria.

📋 Type I (Analysis): Describes and classifies what exists; no causal claims or predictions
🔍 Type II (Explanation): Explains why and how things work; offers causal logic but no formal predictions
🎯 Type IV (Explanation + Prediction): The most common in positivist IS; combines causal story with testable hypotheses
🛠️ Type V (Design & Action): Prescriptive; offers design principles and interventions to accomplish specific goals
IS Relevance: Essential for exam preparation and positioning theory contributions in IS. Helps you communicate what kind of theory you are building and why your criteria for success differ from other types.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Gregor (2006), "The Nature of Theory in Information Systems," MIS Quarterly, 30(3), 611-642. Key contribution: taxonomy of five theory types in IS (Analysis, Explanation, Prediction, EP, Design/Action).
Discipline of origin IS (meta-theory about IS theory).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Type I (analysis/classification) by her own framework.
Core constructs Five theory types (I-V), primary goals, components (constructs, relationships, scope, causal explanations, testable propositions, prescriptive statements).
Mechanism (plain) Different research goals require different theory types. Matching type to goal improves clarity and evaluation.
Boundary conditions This is a classification framework, not a phenomenon theory. Use it to position and evaluate theory, not to test hypotheses.
📚 Foundational Origins

Gregor argues that IS borrows theories from many reference disciplines, which creates confusion about what counts as "theory." Her solution is a taxonomy that organizes theories by primary goal and components. This helps authors communicate what kind of contribution they are making and helps reviewers apply appropriate criteria.

💎 Theoretical Core
Gregor's Five Theory Types - each serves a different research purpose

Type I: Analysis

Describes what is, classifies phenomena. No causal claims. Example: taxonomies, frameworks.

Type II: Explanation

Explains what, why, how. Causal explanations but no predictions. Example: process theories.

Type III: Prediction

Predicts what will happen, not why. Testable propositions without causal explanation. Rare.

Type IV: EP

Both explains and predicts. Most common in positivist IS research. Causal story + testable hypotheses.

Type V: Design & Action

Prescriptive. Says how to do something. Design principles and guidelines. Common in DSR.

📋 Gregor Table 3: Seven Theory Components

  1. Means of representation
  2. Primary constructs
  3. Statements of relationship
  4. Scope
  5. Causal explanations
  6. Testable propositions
  7. Prescriptive statements

Not every type includes every component: Type I = 1-4; Type II adds 5; Type III adds 6; Type IV = 1-6; Type V = all 7.

Variance vs Process: Variance theories ask "how much variance in Y is explained by X?" - often cross-sectional. Process theories focus on sequences and need longitudinal evidence. A comps trap is papers that claim process but only test variance.
Matching Theory Type to Research Question: Use this decision tree on exams. If your question is "what exists?" or "how to classify?", you're likely Type I. If "why does this happen?", aim for Type II. If "what will happen?", Type III (rare) or Type IV (more common with causal story). If "how should we design?", Type V. On comps, examiners often ask "what theory type is this paper?" - use Gregor's seven components (Table 3) to show which are present/absent. Connect to Whetten (1989) completeness criteria: good theories have clear constructs, relationships, scope, and assumptions - Gregor tells you which components each type needs.
Type IV (Explanation + Prediction) is the IS Default: Most IS empirical papers claim Type IV - they explain causal logic AND generate testable hypotheses. On exams, watch for the difference between causal explanation (theoretical story) and testable propositions (hypotheses you can measure). A common miss is papers that explain well but offer vague predictions, or predict outcomes without explaining the mechanism. Strong answers connect to Markus & Robey (1988) causal structure: specify whether change is driven by technology, organization, or their interaction; whether you use variance or process logic; and at what level mechanisms operate.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Gregor's taxonomy is widely used in IS for positioning contributions. In BCIS 6670, it pairs with Whetten for completeness checking and Markus & Robey for causal structure. Together they give a comprehensive lens for evaluating theory quality.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Clear taxonomy; helps authors position contributions; reduces confusion about what "theory" means; widely accepted in IS. - Weaknesses: Some theories blend types (e.g., Type II + Type V); strict classification can oversimplify complex contributions. - Common misapplications: Claiming Type IV (EP) but only providing correlation evidence; mixing levels without cross-level mechanisms; ignoring boundary conditions. - Alternatives/competitors: Whetten for theory completeness; Sutton & Staw for "what theory is not"; Burton-Jones for digital-specific theorizing.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: When asked "what type of theory is this," use Gregor's five types. When asked "what's missing," check which components (Table 3) are present vs absent. Example research question: "What type of theory is needed to guide the design of human-AI delegation interfaces?" Example hypothesis: Not applicable - this is a classification framework. What I would measure: In comps, I would classify the theory type (likely Type V: Design/Action for interface design) and then show which components are present: constructs (delegation affordances, feedback mechanisms), relationships (how affordances enable delegation), scope (enterprise AI contexts), and prescriptive statements (design principles for delegation interfaces). Seminal APA citation: Gregor, S. (2006). The nature of theory in information systems. MIS Quarterly, 30(3), 611-642.

IS-Native Markus & Robey (1988) Important

3 Dimensions of Causal Structure (Markus & Robey, 1988)

A framework for making causal assumptions explicit in IT and organizational change research

Core Concept: Much confusion in IT research comes from making different causal assumptions without stating them. This framework makes three causal dimensions explicit: who/what drives change, how causality works (variance vs process), and at what level.

🔧 Causal Agency: Is change driven by technology, organization, or emergent interaction between them?
📊 Logical Structure: Variance theorizing (explaining outcome variation) or process theorizing (explaining sequences of events)?
📍 Level of Analysis: Individual, group, organization, or societal level - and do mechanisms connect across levels?
⚙️ Research Design Implications: Choices about agency, logic, and level determine what evidence counts and what methods are needed
IS Relevance: Essential meta-framework for exam answers about IT and organizational change. Helps you articulate whether your theory is technological imperative, organizational imperative, or emergent; and whether you are building a variance or process explanation.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Markus & Robey (1988), "Information Technology and Organizational Change: Causal Structure in Theory and Research," Management Science. Key contribution: framework for analyzing causal structure across causal agency, logical structure (variance vs process), and level of analysis.
Discipline of origin IS/organizational change (Management Science).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed into IS, but IS-adjacent because it is directly about IT and organizational change.
Gregor (2006) type Meta-framework (closest to Type I: analysis/classification). It is about making causal assumptions explicit, not predicting a specific outcome.
Core constructs Causal agency (technological imperative / organizational imperative / emergent), variance vs process, level of analysis (individual/group/org/society).
