Mechanism (plain) Many disagreements come from being unclear about "what causes what" and "how causality works." If we make these dimensions explicit, theorizing and research design become cleaner.
Boundary conditions Best for critiquing and designing theorizing, not as a substitute for a phenomenon theory.
📚 Foundational Origins

Markus and Robey start from a practical problem: IT and organizational change research often makes contradictory claims, which makes generalization difficult. A big part of the problem is that papers assume different kinds of causality. Their move is to critique the causal structure underneath theories rather than only debating theory content.

💎 Theoretical Core
The framework asks three questions that are very usable in comps answers, making causal assumptions explicit

1️⃣ Causal Agency

What drives change - technology, organization, or emergent interaction?

  • Technological imperative: Technology determines outcomes
  • Organizational imperative: Organization/context determines outcomes
  • Emergent perspective: Outcomes emerge from interaction

2️⃣ Logical Structure

Variance theorizing or process theorizing?

  • Variance: Focus on explaining variation in outcomes (correlation, regression)
  • Process: Focus on sequences of events and mechanisms over time

3️⃣ Level of Analysis

Individual, group, organization, or society?

Mismatch between theory level and data level creates mis-specification
These choices change your method needs. Variance theorizing can often tolerate cross-sectional evidence, while process theorizing usually needs longitudinal evidence. If level of analysis is mixed without cross-level mechanisms, the model is mis-specified from the start.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In BCIS 6670, this connects naturally to Sarker et al. (2019) because the six types in the sociotechnical axis also imply different causal stories about the social and the technical. That cross-mapping is a good comps move because it shows you can compare theories structurally.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Fast tool for reviewer-like thinking; makes variance vs process explicit; helps explain contradictions as "structural" not personal.
  • Weaknesses: Not a phenomenon theory; some phenomena are truly multi-level and the framework warns more than it solves.
  • Common misapplications: Using process language but only cross-sectional tests; mixing levels without cross-level mechanisms; claiming "emergent" while modeling technology deterministically.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Whetten for completeness and Gregor for theory types. This framework is a complement.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
When asked "is my theorizing framed correctly," apply Markus & Robey's three dimensions.

Example research question: "Is my theorizing about AI delegation framed as technological imperative, organizational imperative, or emergent, and do my methods match that causal structure?" Example hypothesis: "In contexts with high task uncertainty, emergent human-AI interaction processes explain delegation outcomes better than a pure technological imperative model."

What I would measure:
  • For variance: IV/DV in a cross-sectional model with controls
  • For process: sequences of events (adoption, failures, overrides, redesign) and longitudinal trace data
  • In both cases, make the level of analysis explicit

Seminal APA citation: Markus, M. L., & Robey, D. (1988). Information technology and organizational change: Causal structure in theory and research. Management Science, 34(5), 583-598. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 3 & 4/Papers/Information_Technology_and_Org.pdf (scan/OCR) and BCIS 6670/StudyGuide/session3.html.

IS-Native Burton-Jones et al. (2021) Important

Next-Generation IS Theorizing (Burton-Jones et al., 2021)

A call to move beyond theoretical templates toward theorizing that fits digital phenomena

Core Concept: IS theorizing often relies on narrow templates borrowed from reference disciplines. Next-generation theorizing requires moving beyond templates, developing better mechanisms, and specifying tighter boundary conditions that actually fit digital phenomena.

🎯 Theorizing Shifts: Named moves in how you theorize (e.g., from technology-centric to sociotechnical, from adoption to practice change)
🔧 Mechanism Building: Going beyond listing variables to explaining how and why things work in specific contexts
📍 Boundary Conditions: Specifying when theory applies, not assuming universal applicability across all contexts
📝 Digital-Native Theorizing: Building theory that fits the phenomenon rather than forcing phenomena into pre-existing templates
IS Relevance: Essential for evaluating and constructing theory contributions in exam answers. Helps you name the theorizing shift you are making and justify why it fits the digital phenomenon you are studying.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Burton-Jones et al. (2021), "Next-Generation Information Systems Theorizing: A Call to Action," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: argues IS needs "next-generation theorizing" and proposes shifts/strategies for how we theorize digital phenomena.
Discipline of origin IS (meta-theorizing about theorizing).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Meta-framework. It is not a phenomenon theory, it is a framework about how theorizing should change.
Core constructs "Next-generation" theorizing shifts and strategies; Motivate/Create/Mobilize logic in the paper.
Mechanism (plain) The paper argues that how we theorize shapes what we can see and explain. New digital phenomena require moving beyond narrow theory templates and improving how we build mechanisms and boundaries.
Boundary conditions It is guidance for theorizing, not a causal model for an outcome. It helps you evaluate and craft theory sections, not estimate coefficients.
📚 Foundational Origins

The paper is written as a call to action. Its starting point is that IS faces fast-moving digital phenomena, but our theorizing practices can lag behind. The "origin" problem is not one empirical puzzle, but how we build explanations and what we treat as theoretical contribution.

💎 Theoretical Core
A meta-framework that helps diagnose whether theory sections are stuck in templates versus doing real digital theorizing
A practical way to use it is to show that you can name the shift you are making (what changes in theorizing) and then justify why that shift fits the digital phenomenon.
Template thinking: "copy a reference discipline model and add moderators" versus real digital theorizing that fits the phenomenon
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In BCIS 6670, the paper is used alongside Whetten, Gregor, and Markus & Robey. The combined exam move is: Whetten checks completeness, Gregor positions the type of theory, Markus & Robey checks causal structure (variance vs process, level), and Burton-Jones pushes you to update the theorizing itself for digital contexts.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Useful to break "template thinking"; good language for explaining why new digital phenomena need new theorizing moves.
  • Weaknesses: Because it is meta, it can stay abstract if you do not translate it into concrete theoretical propositions and boundaries.
  • Common misapplications: Treating it as motivational text but not changing actual theorizing choices (constructs, mechanisms, boundaries).
  • Alternatives/competitors: Whetten and Gregor are the closest complements; this paper does not replace them, it pushes the field toward stronger digital theorizing.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "What shift in IS theorizing is needed to explain delegation and accountability in human-AI systems?"

This is a meta-framework, so an exam-appropriate move is to state propositions about how a theorizing shift changes what can be seen (lens) rather than a statistical hypothesis.
What I would measure: In comps, I would show which strategy the paper follows, what it Motivates, what it Creates, and how it Mobilizes. This is conceptual evidence rather than statistical measurement.

Seminal APA citation: Burton-Jones, A., Butler, B. S., Scott, S. V., & Xu, S. X. (2021). Next-generation information systems theorizing: A call to action. MIS Quarterly, 45(1), 301-314. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 3 & 4/Papers/burton_jones_full.txt and BCIS 6670/StudyGuide/session3.html.

IS-Native Baird (2021) Important

Writing IS Papers Framework (Baird, 2021)

Five elements for writing strong IS papers that clearly communicate tension and present credible resolution

Core Concept: A publishable IS paper is not just "a topic plus a method." It must have a clear tension or puzzle that the paper resolves for a specific audience. Writing matters, and the process of "middle-out" writing - starting with theory, then evidence - is more effective than linear writing.

🎯 Focus: A clear area of focus - what the paper addresses and why it matters
📚 Background: Existing knowledge and context that sets up the puzzle
⚡ Theoretical Tension: The conflict or assumption that breaks down in a new digital context
✅ Resolution: How the paper credibly resolves the tension with evidence and argumentation
IS Relevance: Practical guidance for writing exam answers and research papers. Helps you frame contributions using "assumption challenging" rather than "gap spotting" - a stronger rhetorical move.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Baird (2021), "On Writing Research Articles Well: A Guide for Writing IS Papers," Journal of the Association for Information Systems. Key contribution: five elements for writing strong IS papers (focus, background, theoretical tension, resolution, guidelines).
Discipline of origin IS (research writing craft).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Practical framework (closest to Type V design/action for writing).
Core constructs Area of focus, background, theoretical tension, resolution, guidelines for future application; assumption challenging vs gap spotting; "middle-out" writing.
Mechanism (plain) A paper becomes publishable when it clearly communicates tension and resolves it for a specific audience. The writing process matters (outline first, write middle-out, intro last).
Boundary conditions Guidance for writing, not a phenomenon theory. Works best when paired with a real theoretical idea and evidence.
📚 Foundational Origins

Baird wrote this as practical guidance for IS researchers (especially theory papers). In Week 1 notes, the key message is that a strong paper is not just "a topic plus a method." It is an argument with a clear tension and a credible resolution that matters for a specific audience.

💎 Theoretical Core
The five elements are the backbone for writing strong IS papers

1️⃣ Focus

Area of focus - what the paper addresses

2️⃣ Background

Context and existing knowledge

3️⃣ Theoretical Tension

The conflict or puzzle that needs resolution

4️⃣ Resolution

How the paper resolves the tension

5️⃣ Guidelines

Guidelines for future application

For comps, the useful move is "assumption challenging" instead of "gap spotting". Gap spotting is "nobody studied X." Assumption challenging is "the field assumes X, but that assumption fails in this new digital context." That frames contribution better.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

As a meta tool, this pairs naturally with Whetten and Gregor. Whetten helps check completeness (what/how/why/boundaries), Gregor helps position the type of theory, and Baird helps you present the tension and audience clearly so a reviewer can see the value.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Very practical; centers tension and audience; helps a PhD student write a coherent paper.
  • Weaknesses: If used as a rigid template, it can make writing feel robotic.
  • Common misapplications: Re-labeling gap spotting as assumption challenging; using the five elements but still having a weak resolution; forgetting to include real guidelines for application.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Whetten for theory completeness; Sun et al. for novelty/rigor/relevance lens in editorial context.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "What theoretical tension am I resolving, and which taken-for-granted assumption should I challenge to resolve it?"

In comps, show the tension clearly and tie the resolution directly to it.

Example hypothesis: "Assumption-challenging introductions lead reviewers to perceive higher novelty than pure gap-spotting introductions, holding execution constant."

What I would measure: Coding of introductions (assumption challenging vs gap spotting) and reviewer assessments if data exists.

Seminal APA citation: Baird, A. (2021). On writing research articles well: A guide for writing IS papers. Journal of the Association for Information Systems, 22(5), 1197-1211. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 1/Notes/Handout_2_Required_Readings.md and BCIS 6670/Week 1/Papers/Baird_WritingResearchPaper_JAIS2021.pdf.

IS-Native Sarker et al. (2023) Important

Reviewing Framework (q-r) (Sarker et al., 2023)

A framework for developmental and paradigm-aware reviewing that separates idea quality from execution quality

Core Concept: The q-r framework separates quality of idea (q) from quality of execution (r). A weak idea is rarely saved by good methods (weak q, strong r is still weak). Conversely, a great idea with weak execution can often be improved. Developmental reviewing coupled with paradigm awareness reduces unfair cross-tradition criticism.

💡 q = Quality of Idea: Is the idea important, interesting, and novel for the target audience?
⚙️ r = Quality of Execution: Is the design, evidence, and writing strong enough to support the idea?
🎯 Developmental Stance: Help authors improve even if rejecting; distinguish fatal flaws from fixable issues
🌐 Paradigm Awareness: Different research traditions have different standards; evaluate within paradigm, not across
IS Relevance: Helps exam writers position their answers and theory contributions with reviewer awareness. Teaches how to distinguish idea-level contributions from execution improvements.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Sarker et al. (2023), "Editorial: Some Thoughts on Reviewing for Information Systems Research and Other Leading Information Systems Journals," Information Systems Research. Key contribution: q-r framework (q = quality of idea, r = quality of execution) and practical guidance for developmental, paradigm-aware, civil reviewing.
Discipline of origin IS reviewing/editorial practice.
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native (ISR editorial).
Gregor (2006) type Practical guidance (closest to Type V design/action for reviewing).
Core constructs q-r framework, developmental reviewing, paradigm awareness, reviewer role metaphors, fatal flaws vs fixable issues.
Mechanism (plain) Separating q and r improves feedback: r is often improvable, q is harder to fix. Paradigm awareness reduces unfair reviews across research traditions.
Boundary conditions Framework for reviewing and positioning, not a phenomenon theory. In comps, it helps me write reviewer-aware answers.
📚 Foundational Origins

This editorial comes from the ISR reviewing process and tries to make evaluation standards explicit. The BCIS 6670 Week 1 notes emphasize the developmental goal: reviews should help authors improve even when the paper is rejected.

💎 Theoretical Core
The q-r framework separates idea quality from execution quality, improving feedback and evaluation

q = Quality of Idea

"Is the idea important and interesting?"

Weak q is rarely saved by fancy methods

r = Quality of Execution

"Is the execution solid?"

r can often be improved through revision
For comps, the direct implication is: fix "So what?" first, then defend design and execution.
The editorial emphasizes paradigm-aware reviewing. Criteria differ across positivist, interpretive, design science, and other traditions, and reviewers should not apply one paradigm's criteria to another.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In practice, this sits naturally next to Whetten and Sun et al.. Whetten checks theory completeness. Sun helps frame novelty with rigor and relevance. Sarker makes "quality of idea" vs "quality of execution" explicit. In comps answers, using these together sounds reviewer-aware.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Fast evaluation tool; normalizes developmental and civil reviewing; makes paradigm awareness explicit.
  • Weaknesses: If q is treated as only novelty, it can undervalue replication and consolidation; if r becomes method fetishization, it can reward complexity over clarity.
  • Common misapplications: Mixing q and r (criticizing method when the real issue is weak question); ignoring paradigm; unprofessional tone.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Sun et al. for more nuance on novelty; Whetten for theory completeness; Gregor for theory types.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
When a question asks "evaluate this study," separate q from r explicitly.

Example research question: "Does this study have high q (important idea) and high r (rigorous execution), and which dimension is the bottleneck?"

Example hypothesis: "Reviewer recommendations are more positive when q is high, even if r is initially low, because r is perceived as improvable."

What I would measure: Coding of reviewer comments into q vs r themes, decision outcomes, and revision trajectories. In comps, I write the answer to show q clearly and then defend r.

Seminal APA citation: Sarker, S., Whitley, E. A., Goh, K.-Y., Hong, Y., Mahring, M., Sanyal, P., Su, N., Xu, H., Xu, J. D., Zhang, J., & Zhao, H. (2023). Editorial: Some thoughts on reviewing for information systems research and other leading information systems journals. Information Systems Research, 34(4), 1321-1338. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 1/Notes/Handout_2_Required_Readings.md and the ISR editorial PDF in BCIS 6670/Week 1/Papers/.

IS-Native Sun et al. (2025) Important

Novelty-Rigor-Relevance Triad (Sun et al., 2025)

A balanced evaluation framework that redefines novelty in relation to rigor and relevance for IS research

Core Concept: Evaluating novelty alone creates inconsistency and leads to "interesting but unrigorous" work or "rigorous but who cares" contributions. The triad requires balancing novelty with rigorous logic and clear relevance for the intended audience. Novelty can take multiple forms: new mechanisms, new boundary conditions, new theory integration, or improved conceptual clarity.

✨ Novelty (Redefined): New mechanisms, boundary conditions, theory integration, or conceptual clarity - not just new variables
🎯 Rigor: Why the logic and evidence are credible and convincing, regardless of paradigm
📢 Relevance: Who cares and why now? What audience does this matter for?
⚖️ Balanced Contribution: All three elements must be present and defended; tradeoffs must be explicit
IS Relevance: The latest (2025) editorial guidance for contribution evaluation. Helps exam writers frame contributions that are simultaneously novel, rigorous, and relevant. Clarifies that novelty looks different across paradigms.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Sun et al. (2025), "Rebalancing Novelty with Rigor and Relevance in Information Systems Research," MIS Quarterly (Editor's Comments). Key contribution: argues novelty must be evaluated together with rigor and relevance; novelty looks different across paradigms.
Discipline of origin IS editorial/reviewing philosophy (MISQ).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Meta-framework (Type I + Type V flavor): classification + prescriptive guidance for authors/reviewers.
Core constructs Novelty, rigor, relevance; tradeoffs and tensions; novelty across paradigms; pitfalls (post-hoc theorizing, construct proliferation).
Mechanism (plain) Evaluating novelty alone creates inconsistency and "interesting but weak" work. The triad pushes the field to define novelty as substantively necessary and balanced with rigor and relevance.
Boundary conditions Framework for evaluating contribution, not a phenomenon theory. In comps, it helps me defend why my contribution matters now.
📚 Foundational Origins

Sun et al. argue that novelty has become a dominant criterion in IS, but it is often used inconsistently. That ambiguity confuses authors and leads to unbalanced work (either novelty without rigor, or rigor without a real "why care"). The triad is meant to rebalance evaluation and clarify what novelty means across paradigms.

💎 Theoretical Core
The triad is a contribution-evaluation lens: novelty must be balanced with rigor and relevance

1️⃣ Novelty

Not just "new variables" - can be new mechanisms, new boundary conditions, new theory integration, or new conceptual clarity

2️⃣ Rigor

Why the logic and evidence are credible

3️⃣ Relevance

Must matter for an audience - who cares and why now

In comps answers, use it to frame contribution: novelty must be supported by rigor and must matter for an audience (relevance).
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

This pairs naturally with Sarker's q-r framework. q is not only novelty, but novelty is one major ingredient of q. Whetten helps check completeness of the theoretical argument. Together, they give a reviewer-like way to position a paper's contribution.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Helps avoid novelty obsession; makes contribution debates more consistent; fits multiple paradigms.
  • Weaknesses: Still requires judgment, and authors can game it by claiming novelty post-hoc without actually changing the theory.
  • Common misapplications: Equating novelty with construct proliferation; using novelty language to hide weak mechanisms; ignoring relevance (who cares).
  • Alternatives/competitors: q-r (Sarker) for simpler evaluation; Whetten for completeness; Gregor for theory types.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
When positioning contribution, explicitly state novelty, rigor, and relevance.

Example research question: "How do I justify that my contribution is novel, rigorous, and relevant, without overstating novelty?"

What I would measure: In comps, I would explicitly state:
  • Novelty (what is new)
  • Rigor (why the logic and evidence are credible)
  • Relevance (who cares and why now)

Seminal APA citation: Sun, H. (2025). Rebalancing novelty with rigor and relevance in information systems research. MIS Quarterly. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 1/Notes/Handout_2_Required_Readings.md and the MISQ editorial PDF in BCIS 6670/Week 1/Papers/.

IS-Native Davis, 1989

Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)

Why people accept or reject information technology based on usefulness and ease of use

Main Model: TAM (Davis, 1989)

Core Concept: User acceptance is determined by two specific beliefs: Perceived Usefulness and Perceived Ease of Use. Ease of use also influences usefulness.

⚡ Perceived Usefulness (PU): The degree to which a person believes using a system would enhance his or her job performance.
🎯 Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU): The degree to which a person believes using a system would be free of effort.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Davis (1989), "Perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, and user acceptance of information technology," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: a parsimonious belief based model of IT acceptance with validated PU and PEOU measures.
Discipline of origin IS (built on social psychology logic from TRA/TPB).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native, but strongly borrowed in its causal logic (beliefs -> intention -> behavior).
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): TAM explains why adoption happens and predicts intention/use.
Core constructs Perceived usefulness (PU), perceived ease of use (PEOU), attitude (in early versions), behavioral intention, and use.
Mechanism (plain) People adopt a system when they believe it helps them perform better (PU) and does not feel hard to use (PEOU). Ease of use also increases usefulness because "easy" systems are easier to see as helpful.
Boundary conditions Strongest in voluntary contexts, early adoption stages, and work productivity tools. Weaker in mandatory use settings, habit-driven use, and settings where social influence, identity, or risk dominates.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Direct "pure TAM" tests in MISQ/ISR/JAIS/JMIS are less common, but TAM still shows up as a baseline in adoption work (including analytics adoption) and in newer conversations about why AI is not "just another IT" (trust, explainability, algorithm aversion).
📚 Foundational Origins

TAM started with a practical IS problem: organizations were investing in new systems, but many systems were underused or used in a shallow way. Davis's move was to translate social psychology into a small set of beliefs that fit workplace IT. Instead of modeling many attitudes and norms, he focused on two beliefs that are easy to recognize in real projects: "Will this help me do my job better?" (perceived usefulness) and "Will this be hard?" (perceived ease of use). The intellectual roots matter in comps answers. In the Theory of Reasoned Action, Fishbein and Ajzen argue that behavior is driven by intention, and intention is shaped by beliefs and attitudes (and, in later work, norms). Ajzen's Theory of Planned Behavior extends this logic by adding perceived behavioral control. TAM keeps the belief -> intention -> behavior logic, but it narrows the belief set to IT-relevant beliefs. That is why TAM became a cumulative research programme quickly: it was simple, it was measurable, and it traveled across systems and settings.

💎 Theoretical Core

The core claim is that PU and PEOU explain a large part of why people form intentions to use a system, and why they later use it. PEOU also influences PU because when a system feels difficult, people are less likely to see it as helpful. In the earliest formulation, attitude toward using the system plays a role, but many later TAM studies drop the attitude construct and connect beliefs directly to intention. The exam move is to be explicit about which version you are using, because reviewers often expect that clarity. The mechanism is belief-based and mostly cognitive. People do not adopt because a system is "good" in some abstract sense. They adopt when they expect personal performance gains and low effort costs. That expectation then drives intention and, when conditions allow, actual use. In other words, TAM is an efficiency story about why rational-looking adoption happens, not a deep theory of identity or power. Boundary conditions are where TAM gets tested. TAM fits best when individuals have discretion (voluntary use) and when the core value of the system is productivity. It struggles more in mandatory settings (where "use" is required), in long-term use where habit dominates, and in settings where risk and uncertainty are central (privacy, cybersecurity, AI decisions). In those settings, usefulness can still matter, but trust, accountability, and perceived risk often become primary mechanisms.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

A major extension is TAM2. Venkatesh and Davis (2000) add social influence and more detailed drivers of usefulness. This is important because it answers a question that basic TAM leaves vague: where does usefulness come from? In TAM2, PU can be shaped by things like job relevance, output quality, result demonstrability, and social influence (subjective norm, image). This also makes TAM more dynamic, because these drivers can change as people gain experience.

Evolution: TAM 2 (Venkatesh & Davis, 2000)

Another big step is UTAUT, which integrates several acceptance models. It keeps the core acceptance logic, but reframes the constructs as performance expectancy and effort expectancy, and it adds social influence and facilitating conditions. In comps, UTAUT is useful for showing you know the evolution, but it also sets up a critique: adding more predictors is not always better theory if the mechanism becomes a long list of antecedents without a clear "why" story. The critique phase is also part of TAM's evolution. Benbasat and Barki (2007) argue that TAM became a default template in IS and sometimes replaced more interesting theorizing about systems, tasks, and contexts. Bagozzi (2007) pushes further and argues for richer models of decision making and self-regulation, not only belief-intention links. These debate papers are very exam-friendly because they let you answer "when is TAM appropriate" and "when is TAM lazy" without sounding dismissive. In 2020-2025, TAM often appears as a baseline that newer work builds beyond. Black-box AI systems, for example, raise adoption questions that PU and PEOU do not fully cover because the key issue is uncertainty about how the system behaves and how to be accountable for it. Asatiani et al. (2020) highlight the challenge of explaining black-box AI behavior. Jussupow et al. (2024) connect adoption to algorithm aversion and appreciation, which links naturally to trust and perceived control. Min and Lea (2023) show that even in analytics adoption, motivators and inhibitors go beyond simple usefulness, especially across cultures.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: TAM is popular because it is simple and cumulative. PU and PEOU are intuitive, they are easy to measure, and they often explain a meaningful share of variance in intention. This makes TAM a useful baseline when you need a clean adoption story and your goal is explanation and prediction. Weaknesses: TAM can become shallow if it is treated as the whole theory of adoption. It under-theorizes social influence, identity, power, and organizational constraints unless you extend it. It can also encourage incremental papers where the contribution is just adding another antecedent to PU or PEOU. The biggest conceptual weakness is that it can turn into a checklist rather than a mechanism-driven explanation. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is applying TAM in mandatory settings and treating reported "use" as success. Another misuse is measuring only intention and treating it as behavior. A third is treating PEOU and PU as generic survey items without grounding them in a specific task and system, which weakens construct validity. Alternatives and competitors: If your setting is about risk and vulnerability, trust theory is often a better core mechanism. If your setting is about human-AI authority allocation, delegation provides a more direct dependent variable than "intention to use". If your setting is about ongoing work practices, sociomateriality or affordance theory often give richer mechanisms than belief-intention models. UTAUT and TPB are closer cousins when you still want an acceptance-style model but need a broader belief set.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use TAM when the question is about user adoption of a specific system and you want a simple baseline mechanism (usefulness and ease of use). Example research question: "When employees are offered a generative AI assistant for report writing, what beliefs drive their intention to use it, and when does trust matter more than usefulness?" Example hypothesis: "Perceived usefulness of the AI assistant is positively associated with behavioral intention to use the assistant." What I would measure (simple): I would measure PU and PEOU using Davis-style Likert items adapted to the specific system ("Using the assistant improves my performance", "Using the assistant is easy to learn"), then measure intention and, if possible, actual use from logs rather than only self-reports. If the setting involves privacy or high-stakes decisions, I would add trust and perceived risk measures to avoid forcing everything into PU/PEOU. Seminal APA citation: Davis, F. D. (1989). Perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, and user acceptance of information technology. MIS Quarterly, 13(3), 319-340.

IS-Native Venkatesh et al., 2003 Core for comps

Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT/UTAUT2)

Why people adopt and keep using IT - unified through beliefs, conditions, and moderators

Main Model: UTAUT (Venkatesh et al., 2003)

Core Concept: People form intentions to use technology when they expect performance gains, low effort, social support, and real enabling conditions. Over time, habit and facilitating conditions matter more than initial beliefs.

⚡ Performance Expectancy: "Will this help me do my job better?"
🎯 Effort Expectancy: "Is it easy enough to use?"
👥 Social Influence: "Do important others think I should use it?"
🔧 Facilitating Conditions: "Is the support infrastructure there?"
IS Relevance: The dominant integrative model for technology acceptance research - synthesizes TAM, TPB, and DOI into one framework with explicit moderators (age, gender, experience, voluntariness)
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Venkatesh, Morris, Davis, and Davis (2003), "User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: a synthesis of major acceptance models into a small set of core determinants plus key moderators.
Key extension Venkatesh, Thong, and Xu (2012), "Consumer acceptance and use of information technology," MIS Quarterly. Adds hedonic motivation, price value, and habit (UTAUT2) and targets consumer settings more directly.
Discipline of origin IS (synthesis of social psychology and prior IS acceptance models).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native (a unifying model built for IS).
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): UTAUT explains and predicts intention and use with a stronger "why this set of beliefs" logic than many one-off acceptance models.
Core constructs Performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, behavioral intention, and use; with moderators like age, gender, experience, and voluntariness (UTAUT), plus habit, hedonic motivation, and price value (UTAUT2).
Mechanism (plain) People form intentions when they expect performance gains, low effort, social support, and real support resources (training, infrastructure). Over time, habit and enabling conditions matter more for actual use.
Boundary conditions Strong for explaining "will people adopt and keep using this IT?" but weaker for explaining deep work practice change, power, or ethics. It can also become a checklist if used without a clear theoretical focus.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" In top IS outlets, pure "test UTAUT" papers are less common today, but UTAUT remains a living research programme through synthesis and critique. One good recent anchor is the JAIS meta-analysis by Blut et al. (2022).
📚 Foundational Origins

UTAUT was created because the acceptance literature became crowded. By the early 2000s, IS had many overlapping models (TAM, TPB, innovation diffusion variants, social cognitive ideas), and papers often looked like small incremental tweaks. Venkatesh and colleagues tried to bring order by asking: across models, what keeps showing up, and when do effects change across groups and contexts? Their answer was a unified view with four core determinants and a set of moderators that explain why results differ. UTAUT2 comes from a similar motivation, but with a different context. Many acceptance models were workplace-focused. UTAUT2 shifts toward consumer and everyday technologies where enjoyment, habit, and "value for money" can matter as much as usefulness.

💎 Theoretical Core
Intention and use can be explained by a small set of beliefs and conditions, with effects varying by context

1️⃣ Performance Expectancy

The main "value" belief (close to usefulness)

2️⃣ Effort Expectancy

The ease belief (close to ease of use)

3️⃣ Social Influence

Captures normative pressure

4️⃣ Facilitating Conditions

Whether the environment makes use feasible (resources, compatibility, training)

UTAUT also insists that these effects are not constant: they vary by age, gender, experience, and whether use is voluntary. The mechanism is still belief based, but it is more explicit about context.
In UTAUT2, habit becomes a key mechanism because repeated use can shift the driver from "I intend to use" to "I just do it." Boundary conditions are where comps answers get stronger. UTAUT is not the right tool when the dependent variable is not "use" but something like delegation, accountability, or dignity outcomes.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, UTAUT often serves as a baseline or a "starting model" that researchers adapt to a domain (health IT, mobile services, AI assistants). That pattern is both a strength and a weakness. It makes studies comparable, but it can also encourage papers that only add context-specific antecedents without sharpening the mechanism.

Evolution: UTAUT 2 (Venkatesh et al., 2012)

Recent work also revisits UTAUT critically. Blut et al. (2022) provide a large meta-analytic view and highlight where effects are stable vs fragile, which helps you answer "when does UTAUT work?" more honestly. Integration is common with trust and risk, especially in privacy and AI settings.

The exam move is to justify the integration: UTAUT explains intention via performance/effort/social/support, while trust explains intention via perceived vulnerability and reliance. They overlap but are not the same mechanism.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: UTAUT is useful because it is integrative and comparative. It gives you a common language across adoption studies and it explains why results vary (moderators). It is a good way to write an exam answer that feels cumulative rather than ad hoc.
  • Weaknesses: It can become a kitchen-sink model. If you throw in every construct without a story, you get statistical fit but weak explanation. Another weakness is that moderators are often ignored or poorly theorized, which defeats the purpose of UTAUT.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Treating UTAUT as universal across contexts without checking meaning (measurement invariance), dropping moderators because they are inconvenient, and using UTAUT for outcomes that are not really "acceptance" outcomes.
  • Alternatives and competitors: TAM (simpler baseline), TPB (more explicit about control and norms), diffusion of innovations (population spread over time), and delegation/sociomateriality when the phenomenon is about changing work arrangements rather than "will people use it."
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use UTAUT when the exam question is clearly about adoption or continued use, and you need a richer baseline than TAM (especially if social influence and facilitating conditions matter).

Example research question: "In a hybrid workplace, what drives sustained use of an AI meeting assistant, and how does prior experience shape those drivers?"

Example hypothesis: "Performance expectancy is positively associated with behavioral intention to use the AI meeting assistant."

What I would measure (simple): I would measure performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating conditions using UTAUT-style Likert items adapted to the specific tool. I would measure use with logs if possible and model experience (time since first use) as a moderator. If the setting is high risk (privacy), I would add trust and perceived risk as parallel mechanisms.

Seminal APA citation: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425-478.

🔄 Theory Contrast: TAM vs UTAUT

When to use TAM vs UTAUT:

Dimension TAM UTAUT
Complexity Simpler baseline - 2 core beliefs Richer model - 4 determinants + moderators
Core Constructs PU, PEOU PE, EE, SI, FC
Social Influence Not explicit (added in TAM2) Explicit construct
Moderators Not built-in Age, gender, experience, voluntariness
Best For Clean adoption story; simple baseline When social influence and facilitating conditions matter
🎯 Exam-ready guidance: Use TAM when you need a parsimonious baseline. Use UTAUT when results vary by groups and conditions, OR when social norms and organizational support are theoretically relevant. Avoid "kitchen-sink" models - justify every construct you include.
Sociology Rogers, 1962 Core for comps

Diffusion of Innovations (DOI)

How innovations spread through populations via social communication and perceived attributes

Core Concept: Innovations spread unevenly through populations based on perceived attributes (relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, observability) combined with social communication, network effects, and adopter categories over time.

📊 Perceived Attributes: Relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, and observability drive adoption decisions
🌐 Social Diffusion: Adoption follows an S-curve with innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, and laggards
🔄 Communication Channels: Networks and interpersonal channels amplify innovation awareness and legitimacy
⏱️ Time Dimension: Diffusion is fundamentally a temporal process with distinct phases and decision points
IS Relevance: DOI explains technology adoption patterns at the population level, especially valuable for understanding how IT innovations spread across organizations, professional networks, and user communities when social learning and network effects matter.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Rogers (1962; later editions), Diffusion of innovations. Key contribution: explains how innovations spread through communication over time in a social system, and why adoption is uneven across groups.
Discipline of origin Sociology / communication.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type II/IV (depends on use): DOI can explain diffusion mechanisms (Type II) and can support prediction of adoption patterns when operationalized (Type IV).
Core constructs Innovation attributes (relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, observability), adopter categories, communication channels, time, social system.
Mechanism (plain) People adopt when an innovation looks better than what they have, fits their context, is not too complex, and can be tried and observed. Social communication and network position shape who adopts first and who follows.
Boundary conditions Best for explaining spread over time and across populations. Less precise for explaining deep post-adoption use, local work practices, or power and politics.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" DOI logic still appears in IS adoption work (especially where timing, categories, and social diffusion matter), but it is often used indirectly or combined with acceptance models. In top IS outlets, DOI is more visible as a foundation for constructs like perceived innovation attributes than as a full diffusion model.
📚 Foundational Origins

Rogers built DOI to explain a recurring pattern: new ideas and technologies rarely spread evenly. Instead, a small group adopts early, then adoption accelerates, then it slows. DOI explains this by combining innovation attributes with social communication. People do not adopt in isolation. They learn about innovations through networks and they watch what happens to early adopters. In IS, DOI matters because many technologies are networked. The value of adoption can depend on others adopting (platforms, standards, security practices). DOI gives you language for the social spread process, not only individual intention.

💎 Theoretical Core
Diffusion is a communication process over time within a social system - adoption depends on perceived innovation attributes and social learning

Relative Advantage

Innovation looks better than what they have - pushes adoption

Compatibility

Fits their context - pushes adoption

Complexity

Not too complex - slows adoption if too high

Trialability & Observability

Can be tried and observed - supports learning and reduces uncertainty

Mechanism: Uncertainty reduction plus social proof. Early adopters take risks. Later adopters follow when uncertainty drops and the innovation becomes socially legitimate.
Boundary conditions are key. DOI is strong for questions like "Why did this technology spread slowly in this industry?" It is weaker for questions like "How does using this AI tool change work identity?" where practice and meaning become central.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS researchers often use DOI to measure perceived innovation characteristics. Moore and Benbasat (1991) is a classic IS adaptation that operationalizes DOI-style perceptions for IT innovations, which made DOI easier to test in IS settings. DOI also gets combined with TAM/UTAUT.

The combo can be clean if you are explicit: DOI explains innovation perceptions and spread, while TAM/UTAUT explain intention and use. The combo becomes messy if you include everything without a story.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: DOI helps you explain adoption at the population level and it gives you clear levers (compatibility, trialability, observability). It is also exam-friendly because it gives you a process story over time.
  • Weaknesses: It can treat adoption as too smooth and rational. It often underplays power, institutional pressure, and strategic manipulation (especially in platforms). It also does not give a deep account of ongoing use and reinvention.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Treating DOI as a list of attributes without studying diffusion over time, and claiming "diffusion" from a single cross-sectional survey.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Institutional theory for legitimacy pressures, TAM/UTAUT for individual intention, and sociomateriality/structuration for deeper practice change.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use DOI when the exam question is about how an innovation spreads across a population (firms, users, hospitals), not only why one person adopts.

Example research question: "Why did some hospitals adopt patient-facing AI triage faster than others, and how did adoption spread through professional networks?"

Example hypothesis: "Perceived compatibility of the AI triage system with existing clinical workflows is positively associated with adoption."

What I would measure (simple): I would measure perceived innovation attributes (compatibility, complexity, relative advantage), network exposure (who talks to whom), and time of adoption. A good design is a longitudinal diffusion model or event history analysis rather than a one-time survey.

Seminal APA citation: Rogers, E. M. (1962). Diffusion of innovations. Free Press.

IS-Native Goodhue & Thompson, 1995 Core for comps

Task-Technology Fit (TTF)

Performance improves when technology capabilities align with task requirements and characteristics

Core Concept: The alignment (fit) between technology features and task demands directly explains performance outcomes. Utilization alone is insufficient; the critical mechanism is whether the tool actually supports what the task requires.

🎯 Task Characteristics: Task complexity, information requirements, and interdependence define what capabilities are needed
⚙️ Technology Characteristics: Functionality, data quality, ease of use, and support capabilities determine what a tool can deliver
🔗 Fit as Mechanism: Alignment between task needs and technology capabilities drives performance, not just adoption or usage
📈 Performance Outcomes: Better fit leads to improved task performance through enabling users to meet task demands more effectively
IS Relevance: TTF bridges acceptance models and performance outcomes, explaining when and why technology investments deliver actual performance improvements. Essential for evaluating IS effectiveness beyond intention-to-use.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Goodhue and Thompson (1995), "Task-technology fit and individual performance," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: links technology characteristics and task characteristics to performance through fit, not just intention.
Discipline of origin IS (influenced by contingency logic in organization theory).
IS-native or borrowed? Mostly IS-native (fit logic is broader, but the model is built for IT).
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): explains why performance improves when technology matches task needs and supports prediction of performance impacts.
Core constructs Task characteristics, technology characteristics, task-technology fit, utilization, and performance impacts.
Mechanism (plain) If the tool supports the actual task requirements, people can do the task better. Fit is the key channel between features and performance, and use alone is not enough.
Boundary conditions Best when tasks are clear enough to specify requirements and when technology features are stable. Harder when tasks are emergent, exploratory, or when value comes from experimentation.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" TTF remains common in applied IS work, but in top IS outlets it is often used as a secondary lens (for performance impacts) rather than the central framing. It is especially useful when the exam question is "does the tool improve performance, and for which tasks?" (If you want, I can add 2020-2025 top-outlet examples with a targeted search.)
📚 Foundational Origins
TTF was proposed because acceptance models were not enough for performance questions - people can use a system and still perform poorly if it doesn't match task demands.

TTF was proposed because acceptance models were not enough for performance questions. People can use a system and still perform poorly if the system does not match what the task demands. Goodhue and Thompson argued that the key is fit: the alignment between task needs (information requirements, interdependence, complexity) and technology capabilities (data quality, functionality, ease, support).

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Fit drives performance. Utilization matters, but fit is the mechanism that explains why use translates into performance impacts.

The main proposition is: fit drives performance. Utilization matters, but fit is the mechanism that explains why use translates into performance impacts. This is why TTF is powerful in comps answers when you want to move beyond "intention" to "outcomes."

Use TTF when you want to move beyond "intention" to "outcomes" in exam answers.
Boundary conditions: If tasks are changing fast, it can be hard to specify what "fit" means. Also, fit can be subjective: two people can see different fits depending on skill and experience.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS research, TTF is often used in combination with TAM/UTAUT. The combination can be coherent: acceptance beliefs explain use, while TTF explains whether use leads to performance. The risk is redundancy if you treat "usefulness" as the same thing as "fit." They are related, but usefulness is an expectation, while fit is an alignment claim that can be evaluated against task requirements.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: TTF gives a clear mechanism for performance impacts. It is also very practical: it connects design choices to outcomes through task needs.
  • Weaknesses: Fit can be hard to measure well. Many studies measure it as a perception scale, which can collapse fit back into "usefulness." Another weakness is that fit can be dynamic: the task and the technology co-evolve.
  • Common misapplications: Treating fit as a vague positive feeling, or using cross-sectional self-reports for both fit and performance (common method bias). Another misuse is ignoring the task analysis and only measuring technology features.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Affordance theory and sociomateriality can explain use and outcomes as emergent from practice, not only from a pre-specified task-feature alignment. RBV and human capital can explain performance outcomes at the firm level rather than task level.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use TTF when the exam question is about performance impacts and "which tool for which task", not just intention to use.

Example research question: "When does a generative AI writing assistant improve analyst performance, and which tasks see the biggest gains?"

Example hypothesis: "Task-technology fit is positively associated with individual performance impacts of the AI writing assistant."

What I would measure (simple): I would classify tasks (summarization, drafting, brainstorming, compliance writing), measure perceived fit for each task, and measure performance with objective outcomes when possible (time, quality ratings, error rates). I would also measure experience to see whether fit changes with learning.

Seminal APA citation: Goodhue, D. L., & Thompson, R. L. (1995). Task-technology fit and individual performance. MIS Quarterly, 19(2), 213-236.

IS-Native DeLone & McLean, 1992 Core for comps

DeLone and McLean IS Success Model

A multidimensional framework organizing IS success across quality, usage, satisfaction, and organizational benefits

Core Concept: IS success is multidimensional and evolves over time. Quality dimensions (system, information, and service) drive usage and user satisfaction, which together produce organizational net benefits. Success is context-dependent and must be evaluated at the appropriate stakeholder and organizational level.

⚙️ Quality Dimensions: System quality, information quality, and service quality determine whether the IS can deliver value
👥 User Response: Quality drives both usage intensity and user satisfaction as intermediate outcomes
💰 Net Benefits: Usage and satisfaction translate into organizational benefits (improved decisions, faster processes, better outcomes)
🎯 Context-Dependent: Success indicators and stakeholder perspectives vary by system type, industry, and organizational level
IS Relevance: Provides a foundational framework for IS evaluation, ensuring researchers move beyond individual measures to understand how technical quality translates into organizational value. A diagnostic tool for understanding what "success" means in context.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work DeLone and McLean (1992), "Information systems success: The quest for the dependent variable," Information Systems Research. Key contribution: a unifying model that organizes what IS researchers mean by "success".
Key update DeLone and McLean (2003), "The DeLone and McLean model of information systems success: A ten-year update," Journal of Management Information Systems. Adds service quality and clarifies "net benefits".
Discipline of origin IS (built as a synthesis of prior IS measures and outcomes).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): it explains pathways from IS quality to outcomes and supports prediction of success indicators.
Core constructs System quality, information quality, service quality (update), use / intention to use, user satisfaction, net benefits.
Mechanism (plain) Better quality systems and outputs lead to more use and higher satisfaction. Use and satisfaction are the channels through which the IS produces net benefits.
Boundary conditions Works best as an evaluation framework for a specific IS in a defined context. Causal direction between use and satisfaction can be reciprocal, and "net benefits" depends on stakeholder and level of analysis.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" In 2020-2025, top IS journals often study value and impacts using more specific lenses (digital resources, resilience, privacy, trust) rather than the full DeLone and McLean chain. The model remains common in applied evaluation work, but direct use in MISQ/ISR/JAIS/JMIS is less visible compared to the 1990s-2000s.
📚 Foundational Origins
DeLone and McLean built their model because IS research had a basic problem: everyone cared about "IS success", but the field did not agree on what to measure.

DeLone and McLean built their model because IS research had a basic problem: everyone cared about "IS success", but the field did not agree on what to measure. Some papers treated usage as success, others used satisfaction, and others used impacts. Their 1992 paper is a synthesis move. It organizes prior dependent variables into a coherent structure and argues that success is multidimensional and unfolds over time. The 2003 update matters because it shows how the model evolved under critique. By then, researchers had debated whether "use" is a cause or an effect, whether quality should include service quality, and how to define impacts. The update reframes impacts as "net benefits" to emphasize that benefits are context-specific and can differ by stakeholder.

💎 Theoretical Core
Core proposition: Quality (system, information, and service) drives use and satisfaction, and these drive net benefits.

The core proposition is a pathway model: quality (system, information, and service) drives use and satisfaction, and these drive net benefits. Many empirical studies test a subset of the chain, but the theoretical claim is that the pieces belong together. The causal mechanism is mostly about capability and perception. System quality and information quality capture whether the system works well and provides good outputs. Service quality captures the support context. If these are strong, people are more willing and able to use the system, and they are more satisfied. Use and satisfaction then translate into tangible benefits like better decisions, faster processes, or improved outcomes.

Boundary conditions are central: Use can be mandatory, so use may not equal success. Satisfaction can be high even when net benefits are low (for example, if the system is pleasant but not productive). Net benefits can also conflict across stakeholders. A system can benefit management while creating extra work for frontline employees. This is why the model often works best as a diagnostic framework rather than as a deep causal theory of organizational value.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, the model became a common backbone for evaluation studies, especially for enterprise systems, e-government services, and online platforms. Over time, researchers refined what "use" means (frequency vs depth vs feature use), and they clarified the level of benefits they are claiming (individual vs organizational). A major debate paper is Seddon (1997), which challenged parts of the original causal structure and argued for clearer separation between use and usefulness and for more careful thinking about the dependent variable.

For comps, the key point is that the model is influential but contested, and you should be able to explain what the debate is really about (measurement and causal direction, not "do we like success models").

For 2020-2025, one pattern is that the "IS success" conversation in top outlets often appears through adjacent concepts like digital resource value, resilience, and performance impacts, rather than by directly using the DeLone and McLean chain. That does not make the model obsolete. It means you usually need a more specific kernel theory to explain why quality translates into benefits in your context.

Petter, DeLone, & McLean (2013) Meta-Analysis: Week 5 This meta-analysis systematically examines which antecedents drive the six IS success dimensions. They analyzed 140+ empirical studies and identified 43 independent variables across 5 categories, organized using Leavitt's Diamond framework (task, people, structure, technology + an added social layer):
  • Task characteristics — task compatibility, variability, difficulty, interdependence
  • User characteristics — attitude toward technology, trust, technology self-efficacy, computer anxiety, user expectations, enjoyment
  • Social characteristics — subjective norms, peer support, computer playfulness, social influence
  • Project characteristics — user involvement, top management support, relationship with developers, project management quality
  • Organizational characteristics — management processes, IT investments, organizational competence, inertia, domain expertise

Key finding: User characteristics (especially attitude and self-efficacy), task-technology fit, and user involvement had the most consistent support across studies. Many organizational and project-level antecedents had weaker or insufficient evidence, showing where the field still needs more work.

Methodology note: The authors used a vote-counting approach with inter-coder reliability of Cohen's kappa = 0.81. Each relationship was coded as supported, not supported, or mixed based on statistical significance across studies.

For comps, this meta-analysis is useful because it shows which factors have strong vs. weak empirical support. A significant takeaway from class discussion is the concept of inertia as an organizational antecedent - most organizational activities are habit-driven (System 1), not goal-driven (System 2), which means IS success can be undermined by routines that resist change. Also note: the paper highlights that IS success is not just about the technology - task, people, and organizational factors matter just as much. The Leavitt's Diamond mapping is a clever move because it connects IS success factors to a well-known organizational theory, making it easier to draw from other disciplines.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: The model is popular because it gives a shared vocabulary for talking about success. It forces researchers to be explicit about what kind of "success" they mean, and it helps connect technical qualities to user outcomes and organizational value. It is also easy to communicate to practitioners who want evaluation metrics.
  • Weaknesses: The biggest weakness is that it can be used without real theorizing. If you treat it as "include these variables and run SEM", you may produce a diagram but not a mechanism. Another weakness is causal ambiguity: use and satisfaction can influence each other, and net benefits can reshape future use (feedback loops). The model is more of a map than a deep explanation of value creation.
  • Common misapplications: A common mistake is to claim organizational net benefits based on individual survey responses without appropriate data and level alignment. Another is to treat "use" as success even when use is mandatory. A third is to ignore time: success is often a trajectory, not a snapshot.
  • Alternatives/competitors: For focused questions, researchers often use more specific theories of value, capability, or behavior (for example, RBV for strategic value of resources, or trust/privacy theories for platform outcomes). These can complement DeLone and McLean by supplying a stronger "why" behind the success pathway.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use DeLone and McLean when the question is about evaluating "IS success" and you need a clear chain from quality to use and satisfaction to net benefits.

Example research question: "What explains the net benefits of an enterprise collaboration platform in a hybrid work organization, and which quality dimensions matter most for sustained use?"

Example hypothesis: "Higher system quality is positively associated with user satisfaction, which is positively associated with net benefits of the platform."

What I would measure (simple): System quality and information quality can be measured via validated survey items (reliability, ease, timeliness, relevance). Use should be measured with logs if possible (active days, feature breadth), and net benefits should be defined at the right level (individual productivity, team coordination, or organizational performance), ideally with outcome data rather than only perceptions.

Seminal APA citation: DeLone, W. H., & McLean, E. R. (1992). Information systems success: The quest for the dependent variable. Information Systems Research, 3(1), 60-95.

Psychology Gibson, 1979 Core for comps

Technology Affordance Theory

Technology offers action possibilities that emerge from the relationship between artifact, user goals, and context

Core Concept: Affordances are action possibilities that exist in the relationship between a goal-oriented actor and their environment. Technology doesn't determine outcomes; rather, it offers potentials that users perceive and actualize based on their goals, capabilities, and context.

🎯 Relational Property: Affordances exist between artifact and actor, not in the artifact alone or the person alone
🔧 Functional Affordances: What actions the technology makes possible (e.g., anonymity, persistence, traceability)
💭 Symbolic Expressions: Values, meanings, and suggested ways of use communicated by the technology
⚡ Actualization: Outcomes emerge through user perception and appropriation of affordances over time, not automatically
IS Relevance: Explains IT effects as mediated by affordances, avoiding technological determinism while accounting for how design shapes possibilities. Particularly valuable for understanding platform features, digital transformation, and heterogeneous user adaptations.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Gibson (1979), The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception. Key contribution: affordances as action possibilities that exist in the relationship between an actor and an environment, not just "features" of an object.
Key IS adaptation Markus and Silver (2008) reframe IT effects using "technical objects", "functional affordances", and "symbolic expressions" to get beyond feature lists and designer intent.
Discipline of origin Psychology (ecological psychology).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (imported into IS).
Gregor (2006) type Mostly Type II (explanation): it explains how IT enables and constrains action through relational possibilities, without always aiming for precise prediction.
Core constructs Technical object; functional affordances; symbolic expressions; affordance actualization; outcomes of use.
Mechanism (plain) A technology does not cause outcomes by itself. It offers possibilities for goal-oriented action to specific users in a context. Outcomes happen when users perceive and actualize those affordances over time.
Boundary conditions Affordances depend on actor goals/capabilities and on context. Same feature can enable one group and constrain another. Affordances can exist but never be actualized.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Affordances remain central in top IS work on societal change and platform features, for example, an affordance-based institutional logics perspective (MISQ) and research on ephemerality features in social media (MISQ).
📚 Foundational Origins

Affordance theory starts in ecological psychology. Gibson (1979) argued that perception is deeply tied to action. We do not just see objects, we see what we can do with them. A chair affords sitting, but not for everyone (a toddler may not be able to sit on a tall chair). The key is relational: affordances are not "inside the object" alone, and they are not "inside the person" alone. They sit in the relationship between a goal-oriented actor and the environment. IS picked up affordances because it solves a classic problem: how to talk about the IT artifact without turning the theory into either technological determinism ("tech causes outcomes") or social determinism ("everything is social"). In IS, affordances became a way to speak about what technology makes possible, while still leaving room for human agency and context.

💎 Theoretical Core
IT effects flow through affordances - they are potentials, not automatic outcomes

The core proposition is that IT effects flow through affordances. A "technical object" (the IT artifact and its components) offers functional affordances to a specified user group, and it also communicates symbolic expressions (values, meanings, and suggested ways of use). These together shape what people can do and what they believe the technology is for.

Outcomes are not automatic. They emerge through appropriation and through the actualization of affordances over time. Affordances are potentials, not outcomes. A system may afford transparency, but if no one uses the dashboard, transparency is not actualized.
This is why affordance research often uses process thinking and longitudinal designs. Boundary conditions are built into the definition: affordances depend on goals and capabilities, so heterogeneity is expected. The same feature can be an enabler for one group and a constraint for another.
It is important to distinguish between "perceived affordances" (what users think they can do) and "existing affordances" (what is actually possible given the artifact and context). Misalignment between the two is a common source of failure in implementations.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, affordance theorizing evolved in a few connected moves. One move was conceptual clarity about what an affordance is (and is not). Markus and Silver (2008) are important because they pushed the field away from treating affordances as a fancy word for features. They also added the symbolic side, which fits well with IS settings where values and norms are part of technology use. Another move was a stronger process view. Volkoff and Strong (2013) link affordances to critical realism and argue that affordances work like generative mechanisms: they exist in the real domain, they are actualized through action, and they lead to observable effects. Strong et al. (2014) then build an explicit theory of organization-level affordance actualization in the EHR context. These papers are useful because they give you an exam-ready vocabulary for how affordances become outcomes. The third move is application in contemporary digital contexts. Faik et al. (2020) show how IT affordances can shift institutional logics in societal change (affordances become a route to explain "how IT matters"). Lehrer et al. (2023) use a feature-specific approach (ephemerality) that is consistent with affordance thinking because it links design properties to patterns of engagement. Together, these illustrate how affordances connect design and social outcomes in IS.

Integration with other theories is common. Affordances often pair well with institutional logics (to explain how affordances shift power and legitimacy) and with sociomateriality (to explain entanglement of technology and practice). The exam trap is to combine them without clarifying ontology. If you treat affordances as properties that exist independent of practice, you need to be clear how that fits with sociomateriality's stance on inseparability.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Affordance theory is popular because it gives a balanced way to theorize the IT artifact. It supports richer explanations than feature lists, but it avoids deterministic claims. It also travels well across contexts because the unit is not "the system", but "what actions the system makes possible for which users".
  • Weaknesses: Affordance language can be vague if researchers do not specify actor, goal, and context. Another weakness is that affordance claims can become unfalsifiable if everything is labeled an affordance after the fact. The solution is to define affordances clearly and trace actualization empirically over time.
  • Common misapplications in IS: The most common misuse is to treat affordances as synonyms for features ("the affordance of a like button"). Another is to skip actualization and jump straight from affordances to outcomes without showing how action produced the outcome. A third is to ignore constraints and unintended effects. Affordances are not only enabling; they also highlight what is not possible.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Adaptive Structuration Theory (AST) is a close cousin in spirit (technology and social structure in use). Sociomateriality is another competitor, but it rests on different ontological assumptions. Actor-network theory and structuration theory are also common alternatives for explaining sociotechnical effects.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use affordance theory when the question is about what actions a technology enables or constrains for a user group in a specific context, not only whether people adopt it.

Example research question: "How do ephemerality features on social media change user engagement behaviors, and what affordances do users actualize when content disappears?"

Example hypothesis: "Greater perceived ephemerality affords more self-disclosure by reducing perceived long-term social risk, which increases posting frequency."

What I would measure (simple): I would define the focal affordance explicitly (for example, "low persistence self-disclosure"), measure user perceptions and goals (risk, impression management), and then use behavioral logs to capture actualization (posting frequency, content type, deletion behavior). The key exam point is to separate potential (affordance) from realized outcomes (actualization and engagement).

Seminal APA citation: Gibson, J. J. (1979). The ecological approach to visual perception. Boston, MA: Houghton Mifflin.

Organizational Studies Orlikowski, 2007 Important

Sociomateriality

Technology and social practice are inseparable and co-produced together through everyday work practices

Core Concept: Technology and social practice are ontologically entangled, not separate layers that interact. Work outcomes emerge through sociomaterial practices where human intentions, organizational norms, and material features are performed and produced together, making them inseparable in analysis.

🔗 Inseparability: Technology and social practice cannot be meaningfully separated; they are produced together
🔄 Performativity: Reality is produced through ongoing practices, not pre-existing and separate
📋 Routines as Unit: Study emerges through organizational routines where material features and social norms become entangled
⚙️ Material Details Matter: Data, interfaces, algorithms, and configuration are integral to understanding practice, not backdrop to human action
IS Relevance: Provides a lens for studying digital work practices, avoiding both technological determinism and pure social constructivism. Particularly valuable for understanding how technology and organizations co-evolve, digital transformation processes, and emerging platform dynamics.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Orlikowski (2007), "Sociomaterial practices: Exploring technology at work," Organization Studies. Key contribution: technology and social practice are inseparable in everyday work, so we should study them as entangled.
Discipline of origin Organization studies / science and technology studies (practice theory, materiality turn).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed foundations, but developed into a core IS lens through IS scholars (especially Orlikowski and colleagues).
Gregor (2006) type Mostly Type II (explanation), but it is also ontological (it changes what we think the phenomenon is). It is not designed for precise prediction.
Core constructs Sociomaterial practices; entanglement/inseparability; performativity; materiality; routines; "technology-in-practice".
Mechanism (plain) Outcomes come from ongoing practices where human intentions, organizational norms, and material features are performed together. Technology does not sit outside practice, and practice is not purely social.
Boundary conditions Best for studying situated work practices and ongoing change. Hard to translate into simple variable models. Often fits interpretive and process methods more than cross-sectional surveys.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Sociomaterial lenses continue to show up in top outlets for studying social media and responsibility in digital contexts, for example, a sociomaterial examination of social protest cycles and recent work on digital responsibility.
📚 Foundational Origins

Sociomateriality became influential in IS because it challenged a common habit in our field: treating "technology" as a thing you add into a social setting, and then measuring its "impact". Orlikowski's 2007 paper argues that in real organizational life, social and material are not separate layers that interact. They are entangled in practice. People do not just use technology. They enact practices that are simultaneously social (norms, meanings, power) and material (devices, software, data, interfaces).

For comps, it helps to say explicitly what problem this solves. Sociomateriality gives you a language to study digital work without either blaming outcomes only on human choice or claiming the artifact determines everything. It also pushes you to pay attention to routines and to how technology is enacted differently across situations.
💎 Theoretical Core
The core proposition is inseparability - technology and social practice are produced together as people work

If we separate "the social" and "the material" as two independent variables, we miss how work actually happens. Sociomateriality claims that the unit of analysis should often be the practice itself, not isolated actors or isolated artifacts. This is why many sociomaterial studies focus on routines, coordination, and ongoing change rather than on one-time adoption decisions.

Sociomateriality is different from "technology affects behavior" theories because it is not mainly a linear causal story. It is a constitutive story. It says that what the technology is, and what the social practice is, are produced together as people work. That is why mechanism language is often about performativity (how actions produce realities) rather than about X causing Y.
Boundary conditions matter. Sociomateriality is strong for explaining how digital and social become intertwined over time, but it is not designed for precise prediction. It can be hard to operationalize because it pushes against clean separation of constructs. If your research design requires clear independent and dependent variables, you need to be explicit about how you translate sociomaterial ideas without breaking the basic ontological point.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, sociomateriality grew into a broad lens with internal debates. One debate is about how strong the ontological claim should be. Some work treats sociomateriality as a reminder to include materiality and practices (a softer version). Other work treats inseparability as a strict ontological commitment (a stronger version). That difference matters because it affects what counts as a good explanation and what research methods make sense. Sociomateriality has been used to study infrastructures, platforms, and social media, where the boundary between "tool" and "social system" is blurry. Tarafdar and Ray (2021) is a good example: they use a sociomaterial perspective to study social protest cycles, where content, platform mechanics, and collective action become entangled. A related 2020-2025 stream is digital responsibility. Recker et al. (2025) is not a sociomateriality paper, but it captures the broader top-journal shift toward theorizing responsibility and harms in digital settings. Sociomateriality is often a good companion lens in this space because responsibility is enacted through practices and artifacts (logs, interfaces, controls), not only through attitudes.

Integration with other theories is common but risky. Sociomateriality pairs naturally with practice theory and with process views of change. It can also be combined with affordance theory, but only if you are clear whether you treat affordances as stable potentials of an artifact or as something that only exists in practice. The exam move is to name that tension instead of ignoring it.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
