  • Strengths: Sociomateriality helps IS researchers avoid shallow "IT impact" stories. It forces you to take the artifact seriously while also taking organizational practices seriously. It is especially strong for studying ongoing transformation and emergent use, where outcomes are not planned in advance.
  • Weaknesses: It can be hard to translate into testable hypotheses because it resists separating constructs. It can also become a label that researchers use without actually doing sociomaterial analysis (for example, they mention "entanglement" but still run a standard variable model). Conceptual clarity is crucial because different scholars use the term in different ways.
  • Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is to use sociomateriality as decoration, while still treating technology as an independent variable and treating the social as context. Another is to claim inseparability but then only study human narratives and ignore the material details (data, interfaces, algorithms, permissions) that shape practice.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Actor-network theory is a close alternative (it also treats human and nonhuman together). Structuration theory and adaptive structuration theory are also common, but they have different assumptions about structure and agency. Affordance theory is another alternative when you want an action-possibility mechanism rather than a constitutive ontology.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Use sociomateriality when the question is about technology and work practices becoming entangled over time, and splitting "tech" from "social" would hide the mechanism.

Example research question: "How do employees and an AI-based decision support tool co-produce new accountability practices, and how do those practices reshape what the tool is used for over time?"

Example hypothesis (framed as a proposition, since this lens is often qualitative): "As organizations introduce AI decision tools, accountability will shift from individual explanation to sociomaterial practices of documentation and auditability, and this shift will change both work routines and how the tool is configured." What I would measure (simple): In a qualitative design, I would trace routines and artifacts: how people document decisions, how audit logs are used, what parts of the AI output are treated as authoritative, and how tool configuration changes after incidents. If a quantitative component is needed, I would operationalize specific sociomaterial patterns (for example, frequency of audit log use, number of workflow changes, or changes in documentation practices) and connect them to outcomes like perceived accountability. Seminal APA citation: Orlikowski, W. J. (2007). Sociomaterial practices: Exploring technology at work. Organization Studies, 28(9), 1435-1448.

🔄 Theory Contrast: Sociomateriality vs Affordance

Two lenses for studying technology-in-practice:

Dimension Sociomateriality Affordance Theory
Ontology Constitutive entanglement - inseparable Relational - separate but connected
Core Focus Practice as co-production of social + material Action possibilities from artifact + user goals
Key Constructs Entanglement, Performativity Affordance, Actualization
Causal Style Emergent, process-based More amenable to variable models
Best For Practice evolution, digital transformation Feature-outcome links, design implications
🎯 Exam integration: Use sociomateriality when practices and artifacts evolve together inseparably. Use affordances when you need clearer causal stories about what technology enables. Markus & Silver (2008) offer a more operational affordance view.

📝 Practice Questions: Part 0C (Meta-Theory & Research Craft)

✅ Reusable Answer Template (90-100 words)

  1. Definition: "X is defined as..." (1-2 sentences, cite source)
  2. Core Mechanism: "This occurs because..." (explain HOW it works, 2-3 sentences)
  3. Boundary Conditions: "This relationship holds when..." or "Limitations include..." (1-2 sentences)
  4. IS Example: "For example, in [context]..." (concrete illustration, 1-2 sentences)
  5. How to Measure: "Researchers operationalize this as..." (constructs, scales, methods, 1-2 sentences)
  6. Common Critique: "A key criticism is..." (show awareness of limitations, 1 sentence)

Target: 90-100 words total. Always cite 2-3 papers with (Author, Year).

Question 1: Explain Whetten's (1989) four essential elements of complete theory (What, How, Why, Who/Where/When). Provide an IS example showing how missing one element weakens theoretical contribution.

Model Answer (98 words):

Whetten (1989) argues complete theories specify four elements: What (which constructs matter), How (relationships between constructs), Why (underlying theoretical logic), and Who/Where/When (boundary conditions). Weak theories omit elements. For example, early Technology Acceptance Model (Davis, 1989) specified What (perceived usefulness, ease of use) and How (both predict intention), but weak on Why (cognitive mechanisms). Venkatesh et al. (2003) strengthened TAM by adding Why (effort expectancy reduces uncertainty) and boundary conditions (experience, voluntariness moderate effects). Critique: Whetten's framework emphasizes variance theory; process theories require different evaluation criteria (Markus & Robey, 1988).

Question 2: Describe Gregor's (2006) five theory types and explain why categorizing a theory's type matters for research design and evaluation.

Model Answer (99 words):

Gregor (2006) identifies five theory types: Type I (Analysis/Description - defines phenomena), Type II (Explanation - why phenomena occur), Type III (Prediction - forecasts without explaining), Type IV (Explanation & Prediction - full causal models), Type V (Design & Action - prescriptive artifacts). Knowing a theory's type clarifies evaluation criteria. For instance, Activity Theory (Type I) describes sociotechnical systems but doesn't predict outcomes - evaluating it for predictive validity would be inappropriate. Conversely, Technology Acceptance Model (Type IV) must demonstrate both explanation and prediction. Design Science theories (Type V) require utility evaluation, not just explanatory power (Hevner et al., 2004). Mismatching theory type and evaluation method yields invalid conclusions.

🔬 Part RM: Research Methods & Study Design

Essential methodological foundations for PhD comprehensive exams

8 Topics

📖 What This Section Covers

این بخش روش‌شناسی تحقیق رو پوشش میده. قبل از شروع، مهمه بدونی که در امتحان جامع فقط نمی‌پرسن «چه روشی استفاده شده» - بلکه می‌خوان بدونی چرا اون روش مناسب بوده و چه تهدیدهایی validity رو تحت تأثیر قرار میده.

چهار نوع Validity: Internal validity (آیا رابطه علّی واقعیه؟)، External validity (آیا تعمیم‌پذیره؟)، Construct validity (آیا واقعاً همون چیزی رو اندازه می‌گیری که ادعا می‌کنی؟)، Statistical conclusion validity (آیا نتایج آماری قابل اعتماده؟). در هر مقاله‌ای که نقد می‌کنی، باید بتونی بگی کدوم validity ضعیفه.

Common Method Bias (CMB): وقتی همه متغیرها رو با یک پرسشنامه از یک نفر می‌پرسی، واریانس مشترک ایجاد میشه که رابطه‌ها رو بزرگ‌تر نشون میده. این یکی از رایج‌ترین انتقادات در IS هست.

SEM، Mediation، Moderation: SEM برای مدل‌های پیچیده با روابط همزمان استفاده میشه. Mediation جواب میده «از چه مسیری اثر می‌ذاره؟» (مثلاً X از طریق M روی Y اثر می‌ذاره). Moderation جواب میده «کی اثر قوی‌تر یا ضعیف‌تره؟» (مثلاً اثر X روی Y برای زنان قوی‌تره).

کمّی vs. کیفی: روش‌های کمّی (survey، experiment) برای تست فرضیه و تعمیم‌پذیری خوبن. روش‌های کیفی (case study، grounded theory، interviews) برای عمق و کشف مفاهیم جدید. Mixed methods هر دو رو ترکیب می‌کنه. برای comps باید بدونی چه زمانی هر کدوم مناسبه.

Research Methods are the tools, techniques, and principles that help us conduct accurate, reliable, and valid research. This section covers the methodological foundations every PhD researcher must know: validity (are we really measuring what we claim?), reliability (are our results reproducible?), causality (how can we prove X causes Y?), different research designs (quantitative, qualitative, and mixed methods), and common validity threats like Common Method Bias, Endogeneity, and selection bias. It also covers specialized methods like Systematic Literature Review, Grounded Theory, and basic statistical techniques like regression, mediation, moderation, and SEM.

Why this is essential for the comprehensive exam: The PhD exam has "foundation questions" that directly test your knowledge of research methods. You may be asked when to use mixed methods (and why), how to conduct an SLR, or how Grounded Theory differs from quantitative research. You also need to identify validity threats and explain how to control them when critiquing papers or writing research proposals.
Key concepts you must know deeply:
  • Four types of validity: Internal (did X cause Y?), External (generalizability), Construct (does the instrument measure the theoretical concept?), Nomological (do relationships with other constructs make sense?)
  • Three conditions for causality: Temporal precedence, covariation, and ruling out alternative explanations
  • When to use Mixed Methods: When you need both deep understanding (qual) and statistical generalization (quant), or when one method alone is insufficient
  • Common threats to identify: Common Method Bias, Endogeneity, Selection bias, Measurement error
  • Advanced methods: SEM for complex relationships, Mediation/Moderation for mechanisms, Case study for deep context, Grounded theory for theory building

🗺️ Research Methods Connection Map

How methods relate to research questions and theories

Quantitative
  • Survey Research - Testing theories at scale
  • Experiments - Establishing causality with control
  • Secondary Data Analysis - Large-scale patterns
  • Archival Research - Historical patterns
Qualitative
  • Case Study - Deep context understanding
  • Ethnography - Cultural immersion
  • Grounded Theory - Theory building from data
  • Interview Studies - Rich narratives
Mixed Methods
  • Sequential - Qual → Quant or vice versa
  • Concurrent - Both simultaneously
  • Transformative - Social justice focus
IS-Specific
  • Design Science - Build and evaluate artifacts
  • Action Research - Change while studying
  • Systematic Literature Review - Synthesize evidence

🔗 Method-Theory Pairings

Survey → Variance Theories Use surveys to test variance models like UTAUT, TPB, Trust Theory - large sample, validated scales, SEM analysis
Case Study → Process Theories Use case studies to understand how things unfold over time - Structuration, Sociomateriality, Institutional change processes
Experiment → Causal Claims Use experiments when you need strong internal validity - manipulation + random assignment = causal inference (e.g., testing Prospect Theory framing effects)
Grounded Theory → Theory Building Use grounded theory when existing theories don't explain your phenomenon - iterative coding, theoretical sampling, emerge new constructs
Design Science → Artifact Theories Use design science for Digital Object Theory, Platform architecture - build artifact, evaluate utility, contribute design principles (Gregor Type V)
Mixed Methods → Complex Phenomena Use mixed when you need both depth (qual) and breadth (quant) - e.g., interviews to identify trust dimensions → survey to test trust model

🎯 Research Question → Method Decision Tree

Step 1: What type of question?

↳ "What is happening?" (Exploratory) → Use Case Study or Grounded Theory

↳ "Why/How does X happen?" (Explanatory) → Use Case Study (process) or Experiment (causality)

↳ "How much/many?" (Descriptive) → Use Survey or Secondary Data

↳ "Does X cause Y?" (Causal) → Use Experiment (or quasi-experiment, natural experiment)

↳ "Does theory T hold?" (Testing) → Use Survey with SEM, or Archival Data

Step 2: What's your epistemology?

↳ Positivist (objective reality, testable) → Quantitative methods

↳ Interpretivist (socially constructed) → Qualitative methods

↳ Pragmatist (whatever works) → Mixed methods

Step 3: Practical constraints?

↳ Need large sample? → Survey (but watch for response rates)

↳ Limited access? → Case study (single or few cases can be valuable)

↳ Need fast results? → Experiment or secondary data (qual takes time)

⚠️ Common Method Selection Mistakes

Mistake 1: Using survey when you need causality. Surveys show correlation, not causation. Fix: Use longitudinal surveys (panel data) or add instrumental variables for quasi-experimental design.
Mistake 2: Case study with n=1 claiming generalizability. Single cases have low external validity. Fix: Be honest about boundaries - use analytical generalization (theory extension), not statistical generalization.
Mistake 3: Mixing methods without integration. Just doing qual + quant separately isn't mixed methods. Fix: Show how one method informs the other - e.g., "interviews identified new constructs, which we then validated via survey."
Mistake 4: Grounded Theory but starting with a priori theory. Grounded theory requires theoretical sensitivity, not hypothesis testing. Fix: If you have existing theory, use thematic analysis or case study instead.

📝 Exam Scenarios: Method Selection

Scenario 1: "Design a study on AI adoption in healthcare"
Answer structure: (1) RQ: "What factors drive AI adoption?" → variance model → Survey. (2) Sample: Healthcare professionals, n=300+, validated scales (UTAUT + Trust + Task-Technology Fit). (3) Analysis: SEM to test moderation (age, experience). (4) Validity threats: Common method bias (temporal separation), selection bias (random sampling across hospitals), measurement error (pilot test scales).
Scenario 2: "Study how organizations implement blockchain"
Answer structure: (1) RQ: "How does blockchain change organizational processes?" → process understanding → Case Study. (2) Design: Multiple case study (3-5 firms), purposive sampling (early adopters), longitudinal (12-month observation). (3) Data: Interviews, documents, observation. (4) Analysis: Process tracing, pattern matching. (5) Validity: Triangulation (multiple sources), member checking, thick description.
Scenario 3: "Test if privacy warnings reduce data disclosure"
Answer structure: (1) RQ: "Does X cause Y?" → Causal claim → Experiment. (2) Design: 2x2 between-subjects (warning presence × warning severity). (3) DV: Amount of personal data disclosed. (4) Random assignment to conditions. (5) Control: Demographic covariates. (6) Theory: Privacy Calculus + Dual-Process Theory (warnings trigger System 2 processing).
Research Methods Core for comps

Validity and Reliability

Ensuring research measures what it claims to measure

Core Concept: Validity = measuring the right thing; Reliability = measuring consistently. All rigorous research must establish both.

🎯 Internal Validity: Confidence that X causes Y (control confounds)
🌍 External Validity: Generalizability to other contexts
📏 Construct Validity: Measuring theoretical constructs accurately
🔁 Reliability: Consistency across time, items, raters
📚 When to Use Each Type
  • Internal validity threats: selection bias, history effects, maturation, testing effects, instrumentation drift. Critical for experimental and quasi-experimental designs.
  • External validity threats: interaction of selection and treatment, interaction of setting and treatment. Ask: "Can we generalize from college students to working professionals?"
  • Construct validity types: convergent (different measures of same construct correlate), discriminant (measures of different constructs don't correlate), nomological (relationships with other constructs as theorized).
  • Reliability measures: Cronbach's alpha (internal consistency, aim for α > 0.70), test-retest (temporal stability), inter-rater (Cohen's kappa for agreement).
Exam Strategy: When critiquing a study, systematically check all four validity types. Common mistake: cross-sectional surveys cannot establish internal validity (causality) because they violate temporal precedence.
Common Pitfall: High reliability ≠ high validity. You can reliably measure the wrong thing (e.g., measuring attitude instead of behavior consistently).

📊 How to Operationalize

Construct Validity Assessment:

  1. Conduct confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to verify item loadings
  2. Check convergent validity: AVE > 0.50
  3. Check discriminant validity: √AVE > inter-construct correlations
  4. Assess nomological validity: test theorized relationships

Example in IS: Measuring "Trust in AI Systems" - convergent validity requires items about AI ability, benevolence, integrity to load on same factor; discriminant validity requires trust to differ from satisfaction or continuance intention.

Research Methods Core for comps

Common Method Bias (CMB)

Systematic variance from measurement method rather than constructs

Core Concept: When both independent and dependent variables come from the same data source (e.g., self-report survey), artificial correlations may arise from response styles, social desirability, or consistency motives rather than true relationships.

📋 Common Source: Same respondent provides all data
🔄 Common Measure: Same question format throughout
🕒 Measurement Context: All data collected at same time/place
⚠️ Effect: Inflated correlations, Type I errors
🛠️ Remedies for CMB
  1. Procedural remedies (ex-ante): Temporal separation (measure IV and DV at different times), spatial separation (different survey sections), anonymity assurance, reverse-coded items, different response formats.
  2. Statistical remedies (ex-post): Harman's single-factor test (if single factor accounts for majority of variance, CMB present), CFA marker technique (add theoretically unrelated marker variable), common latent factor approach (add method factor to structural model).
  3. Design remedies: Multi-source data (manager rates performance, employee rates attitudes), objective measures where possible (actual usage logs vs self-reported usage).
Exam Strategy: When evaluating survey research, always check: (1) Are IV and DV from same source? (2) Were they measured at same time? (3) Did authors test for or address CMB? If answers are yes, yes, no - flag as limitation.
When CMB Matters Most: Self-reported perceptual constructs (attitudes, intentions) measured cross-sectionally. Less concern with objective/archival data or longitudinal designs with temporal lag.
Research Methods Core for comps

Causality & Causal Inference

Establishing that X causes Y requires three necessary conditions

Core Concept: To claim causality, researchers must demonstrate three conditions. Most IS research uses correlational designs that fail to meet all three, limiting causal claims.

⏰ Temporal Precedence: Cause must precede effect in time
📊 Covariation: Cause and effect must systematically vary together
🚫 Rule Out Alternatives: Eliminate confounds and rival explanations
⚠️ Cross-Sectional Limits: Cannot establish causality without temporal ordering
🔍 Endogeneity Sources

Endogeneity occurs when predictors correlate with error term, violating causal inference assumptions:

  • Omitted variables: Unmeasured factors affecting both X and Y (e.g., firm capability affects both IT investment and performance)
  • Simultaneity/Reverse causality: Y causes X instead of (or in addition to) X causing Y (e.g., performance enables IT investment)
  • Measurement error: Systematic error in independent variables biases coefficients

Common fixes: Instrumental variables (2SLS), fixed effects models, difference-in-differences, propensity score matching, natural experiments.

Exam Strategy: When asked to evaluate causal claims, check all three conditions systematically. Common exam trap: "The survey shows that perceived usefulness causes adoption." Incorrect if cross-sectional (violates temporal precedence).
Gold Standard: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) meet all three conditions by design - random assignment rules out confounds, treatment precedes outcome, and manipulation creates covariation.
Research Methods Core for comps

Mixed Methods Research

Intentional integration of qualitative and quantitative approaches

Core Concept: Mixed methods research combines QUAL and QUAN approaches in a single study to gain complementary insights. Design choice determines how and when integration occurs (Venkatesh et al., 2013).

🔀 Convergent Parallel: QUAL + QUAN simultaneous, compare/triangulate findings
➡️ Exploratory Sequential: QUAL → QUAN (interviews build survey items)
⬅️ Explanatory Sequential: QUAN → QUAL (interviews explain survey patterns)
✨ Advantages: Completeness, complementarity, corroboration
📊 When to Use Each Design
  • Convergent parallel: When seeking triangulation and completeness. Example: Survey measures security compliance rates (QUAN), interviews reveal why violations occur (QUAL). Results converge on same phenomenon from different angles.
  • Exploratory sequential (QUAL→QUAN): When exploring new phenomena to build measurement instruments. Example: Interviews identify trust dimensions in AI systems, then develop and validate survey scales.
  • Explanatory sequential (QUAN→QUAL): When statistical results need deeper understanding. Example: Survey shows 60% violate security policies, follow-up interviews explain policies conflict with productivity goals.
Exam Strategy (98 words): Mixed methods research intentionally integrates qualitative and quantitative approaches in a single study. Venkatesh et al. (2013) identify three main designs: convergent parallel (simultaneous QUAL+QUAN for triangulation), exploratory sequential (QUAL→QUAN, using interviews to build surveys), and explanatory sequential (QUAN→QUAL, using interviews to explain survey patterns). Key advantages include completeness (breadth and depth), complementarity (offsetting each method's weaknesses), and corroboration (confirming findings across methods). For example, studying security policy violations: quantitative surveys reveal 60% violate policies, while qualitative interviews explain violations occur because policies conflict with productivity goals.
Research Methods Core for comps

Systematic Literature Review (SLR)

Rigorous, transparent, and reproducible method for reviewing research

Core Concept: SLR follows explicit protocol to identify, evaluate, and synthesize research on a question. Unlike narrative reviews, SLR is systematic and replicable (Templier & Paré, 2015).

📋 Six Stages: Problem formulation → Search → Screening → Quality assessment → Data extraction → Synthesis
🔍 Transparency: Explicit criteria, documented decisions, reproducible process
✅ Quality Filter: Assess methodological rigor before including studies
📊 vs Meta-Analysis: SLR is method; meta-analysis is statistical technique
🔄 SLR Procedure (6 Stages)
  1. Problem formulation: Define research question, inclusion/exclusion criteria, scope
  2. Literature search: Systematic search across databases (AIS eLibrary, Web of Science, etc.) using defined keywords
  3. Screening: Review titles/abstracts against criteria, then full-text review
  4. Quality assessment: Evaluate methodological rigor (sampling, analysis, validity)
  5. Data extraction: Code studies using standardized form (author, year, method, findings, constructs)
  6. Synthesis: Identify patterns, gaps, contradictions. Narrative or framework-based synthesis.
Exam Strategy (97 words): Systematic Literature Review (SLR) is a rigorous, transparent, and reproducible method for reviewing research. Following Templier & Paré (2015), the general procedure includes: (1) problem formulation with clear criteria, (2) systematic literature search across databases, (3) screening titles/abstracts then full-texts, (4) quality assessment, (5) data extraction using standardized forms, and (6) synthesis identifying patterns and gaps. SLR differs from meta-analysis in scope and method: SLR can include qualitative and quantitative studies using narrative synthesis, while meta-analysis requires quantitative studies and uses statistical pooling of effect sizes.
Research Methods Core for comps

Grounded Theory

Building theory from data through constant comparison and theoretical sampling

Core Concept: Grounded theory (Glaser & Strauss, 1967) builds theory inductively from data rather than testing existing theory. Two key mechanisms distinguish it: constant comparison and theoretical sampling.

🔄 Constant Comparison: Iteratively compare new data to existing categories
🎯 Theoretical Sampling: Let emerging theory guide who/what to study next
📊 Theoretical Saturation: Stop when no new insights emerge
⚙️ Coding Stages: Open → Axial → Selective coding
🔍 Key Concepts Explained

Constant Comparison: Each new data piece is compared to previously collected data and emerging categories, refining concepts through comparison. For example, comparing Interview 2 to Interview 1 to see if themes match or new categories emerge.

Theoretical Sampling: Sampling decisions are guided by emerging theory rather than predetermined. You purposefully sample cases that illuminate developing concepts until reaching theoretical saturation (no new insights). Unlike statistical sampling (representativeness), theoretical sampling seeks concept development.

Example in IS: Studying shadow IT adoption. Interview 1 reveals "policy resistance." Interview 2 confirms and adds "peer influence." Theoretical sampling directs you to interview power users (not random sample) to understand resistance mechanisms. Continue until new interviews add no new dimensions.

Exam Strategy (99 words): Grounded Theory (Glaser & Strauss, 1967) is a qualitative method for building theory from data rather than testing existing theory. Constant comparison means iteratively comparing each new data piece to previously collected data and emerging categories, refining concepts through these comparisons. For example, comparing Interview 2 to Interview 1 to see if themes match or new categories emerge. Theoretical sampling means letting emerging theory guide who/what to study next, rather than deciding all sampling upfront. You purposefully sample cases that illuminate developing concepts until reaching "theoretical saturation" (no new insights).
Research Methods Important

Quantitative Methods: Regression, Mediation, Moderation, SEM

Core statistical techniques for testing IS theories

Core Concept: Quantitative IS research uses regression-based techniques to test theoretical relationships. Understanding when to use each approach is critical for comps.

📈 Regression: Test direct effects (Y = β₀ + β₁X)
🔗 Mediation: Test indirect effects (X → M → Y)
⚡ Moderation: Test interaction effects (effect of X on Y depends on Z)
🔄 SEM: Test complex models with multiple relationships and latent variables
📊 When to Use Each Method
  • Mediation (X → M → Y): Tests why or how X affects Y. Example: IT capability (X) → Process efficiency (M) → Firm performance (Y). Use Sobel test or bootstrap confidence intervals for indirect effect. Partial mediation if direct effect remains significant; full mediation if not.
  • Moderation (X×Z → Y): Tests when or for whom X affects Y. Example: Security training effectiveness depends on user experience. Test interaction term (X×Z). Plot simple slopes at high/low moderator values to interpret.
  • SEM vs Regression: Use SEM when: (1) multiple DVs, (2) latent variables with measurement error, (3) testing mediating chains, (4) need model fit indices. Regression sufficient for simple direct effects with observed variables.
Exam Strategy: When proposing quantitative research, match method to theory. Mediation answers "how does X affect Y?", moderation answers "when/for whom?", SEM handles complex models with latent constructs. Always specify measurement (how constructs operationalized) and analysis (which technique and why).
Research Methods Important

Qualitative Methods: Case Study, Interpretive Research, Saturation

Core approaches for understanding meaning and context in IS

Core Concept: Qualitative IS research seeks deep understanding of phenomena in context. Quality judged by trustworthiness (credibility, dependability, confirmability) rather than validity/reliability.

📚 Case Study: In-depth investigation of phenomenon in real-world context
🔍 Interpretive: Understanding meaning from participants' perspectives
📊 Saturation: Continue data collection until no new themes emerge
✅ Trustworthiness: Credibility, dependability, confirmability, transferability
🔍 Core Qualitative Approaches
  • Case study: Intensive examination of single case or multiple cases. Useful when boundaries between phenomenon and context unclear. Example: How did Organization X appropriate new collaboration technology? Data: interviews, observations, documents. Analysis: pattern matching, explanation building.
  • Interpretive research: Seeks to understand meaning through participants' lived experience. Assumes reality is socially constructed. Example: How do security professionals interpret their organizational role? Data: semi-structured interviews. Analysis: hermeneutic interpretation, identifying themes.
  • Data saturation: Continue collecting data until new data no longer generate new insights or themes. Not about sample size - about concept completeness. Achieved through iterative coding and theoretical sampling.
✅ Trustworthiness Criteria

Four criteria replace positivist validity/reliability:

  • Credibility (internal validity): Findings represent reality? Techniques: prolonged engagement, member checking, triangulation
  • Transferability (external validity): Findings apply elsewhere? Provide thick description for readers to judge
  • Dependability (reliability): Process documented and consistent? Audit trail of decisions
  • Confirmability (objectivity): Findings rooted in data not researcher bias? Document interpretation process
Exam Strategy: When proposing qualitative research, specify: (1) paradigm (interpretive, critical, positivist qualitative), (2) data collection method (interviews, observations, documents), (3) analysis approach (thematic, grounded theory, narrative), (4) how you'll establish trustworthiness (member checking, triangulation, audit trail).

📝 Practice Questions: Part RM (Research Methods & Study Design)

Note: Mixed Methods, SLR, and Grounded Theory already have model answers embedded in their theory entries above.

Question 1: Explain the four types of validity (internal, external, construct, nomological) and provide an example of a threat to each in IS research. Why can't cross-sectional surveys establish internal validity?

Model Answer (97 words):

Internal validity: confidence X causes Y; threatened by selection bias (non-random assignment). External validity: generalizability; threatened by sample-treatment interaction (college students ≠ working professionals). Construct validity: measuring intended constructs; threatened by mono-method bias (only self-report). Nomological validity: theorized relationships hold; threatened when proposed correlations don't materialize. Cross-sectional surveys can't establish internal validity because they violate temporal precedence - measuring X and Y simultaneously can't prove X preceded Y. Only experimental (random assignment) or longitudinal designs (temporal ordering) establish causality (Shadish et al., 2002; Venkatesh et al., 2013).

Question 2: Define Common Method Bias (CMB) and explain three remedies - one procedural and two statistical. When is CMB most problematic?

Model Answer (99 words):

Common Method Bias (CMB) occurs when variance stems from measurement method rather than constructs (Podsakoff et al., 2003). Procedural remedy: temporal or spatial separation (measure predictor and outcome at different times/contexts). Statistical remedies: (1) Harman's single-factor test (if one factor explains >50% variance, CMB suspected), (2) CFA marker technique (adding theoretically-unrelated marker variable to detect method effects). CMB is most problematic when: single respondent provides all data, same survey instrument, Likert scales create acquiescence bias, and social desirability affects responses (MacKenzie & Podsakoff, 2012). Multi-source data mitigates CMB.

Question 3: What are the three necessary conditions for establishing causality? Explain why most IS studies can't claim causal relationships and what research designs can.

Model Answer (98 words):

Three conditions for causality: (1) Temporal precedence - cause precedes effect, (2) Covariation - X and Y statistically associated, (3) Rule out alternative explanations - no confounds. Most IS studies use cross-sectional surveys, which satisfy covariation but fail temporal precedence (simultaneous measurement) and can't rule out confounds (observational data). Only randomized controlled trials (RCTs) establish causality via random assignment, which controls confounds. Longitudinal designs (panel data, time-series) can establish temporal ordering. Quasi-experiments with strong controls approximate causality (Shadish et al., 2002). Observational studies show associations, not causation.

💻 Part A: Core IS Theories

Information Systems-native theories and frameworks

15 Theories

📖 What This Section Covers

اینجا قلب رشته IS هست - تئوری‌هایی که خودِ رشته سیستم‌های اطلاعاتی ساخته (IS-native)، نه از روانشناسی یا جامعه‌شناسی قرض گرفته. اینا پرکاربردترین و پراستنادترین تئوری‌ها در ژورنال‌های MISQ، ISR، JAIS و JMIS هستن.

TAM و خانواده‌ش: شروع می‌کنیم با Technology Acceptance Model (Davis, 1989) - ساده‌ترین و پراستنادترین مدل IS. دو متغیر: Perceived Usefulness و Perceived Ease of Use. بعدش UTAUT (Venkatesh et al., 2003) که ۸ مدل رو یکی کرد و moderator اضافه کرد (سن، جنسیت، تجربه). نقطه قوتشون سادگیه، نقطه ضعفشون اینه که فقط «use» رو پیش‌بینی می‌کنن - نه delegation، نه trust، نه accountability.

IS Success (DeLone & McLean): موفقیت IS چند بُعدیه: System Quality → Information Quality → Service Quality → Use → Satisfaction → Net Benefits. مهم: «use» توی mandatory systems معنی نداره، و «net benefits» بسته به stakeholder فرق می‌کنه. Petter et al. (2013) نشون داد ۶ دسته antecedent (task، people، social، project، organizational، technology) روی موفقیت اثر میذارن.

Affordance Theory: فناوری خودش نتیجه تولید نمی‌کنه - امکانات عمل (action possibilities) رو فراهم می‌کنه که بسته به هدف و توانایی کاربر فعال میشن. این تئوری از determinism فناوری فرار می‌کنه و می‌گه «بستگی داره».

TTF (Task-Technology Fit): فناوری وقتی عملکرد رو بهبود میده که با نیازهای task همخوانی داشته باشه. ساده ولی قدرتمند - و synergy خوبی با TAM داره.

برای comps: باید بتونی هر کدوم از این تئوری‌ها رو مقایسه کنی، بگی کی از کدوم استفاده کنی، و مهم‌تر از همه بگی boundary condition هر کدوم چیه (کجا کار نمی‌کنه).

Core IS Theories are the native, home-grown frameworks and models of the Information Systems discipline - theories that originated within IS itself, not borrowed from other fields. These theories address IS-specific questions: What exactly is an IT artifact and how should we conceptualize it? Why do some people and organizations adopt new technologies while others do not? After adoption, why do some continue using and others abandon? How do we measure the success of an IS? How does IT create (or fail to create) value? This section includes classic and widely-used models like TAM, UTAUT, TTF, DeLone & McLean IS Success Model, and more contemporary perspectives like Technology Affordances, Sociomateriality, and Digital Objects Theory.

Why this is critical for the comprehensive exam: These are the most cited and widely-used theories in IS research - TAM alone has thousands of citations. Exam questions frequently ask you to compare two IS-native theories (e.g., TAM vs. TTF - which has more explanatory power in what conditions?) or explain how these IS-native theories differ from borrowed theories from psychology, sociology, or economics.
Key concepts and theory families:
  • Technology adoption and continuance: TAM (usefulness and ease of use), UTAUT (integration of 8 theories), DOI (Rogers), ECT (why users continue or stop - Bhattacherjee)
  • Task-technology fit: TTF (Goodhue & Thompson), Technology Affordances (relational capabilities between user and technology)
  • IS success and value creation: DeLone & McLean IS Success Model (6 dimensions), IT Value
  • IT Artifact conceptualization: Sociomateriality (Orlikowski), Digital Objects (Programmability, Addressability, Traceability), Sociotechnical Systems
  • Exam tips: IS-native vs. borrowed theories difference, initial adoption vs. continuance, tool perspective vs. affordance perspective

🗺️ Core IS Theories Connection Map

How IS-native theories relate across artifact conceptualization, adoption, success, and AI/delegation

IT Artifact Nature
  • Digital Object Theory - Ontological nature
  • 5 Views of IT Artifact - Conceptualizations
  • Sociotechnical Axis - Social ↔ Technical
  • Sociomateriality - Entanglement view
  • Affordance Theory - Action possibilities
Adoption & Use
  • UTAUT/UTAUT2 - Acceptance predictors
  • DOI - Innovation diffusion
  • TTF - Technology-task match
  • Media Richness - Channel selection
IS Success
  • DeLone & McLean - Success dimensions
  • ARM (Zhang) - Affective responses
AI & Delegation
  • IS Delegation - Human→IT delegation
  • Human-AI Dynamics - Trust calibration
  • Human-AI Hybrid - Problem-solving modes

🔗 Key Relationships

Affordance ↔ Sociomateriality Both reject tool view and emphasize relational emergence, but Affordance maintains analytical separation (technology offers, users perceive) while Sociomateriality sees inseparable entanglement
UTAUT → D&M Success Adoption (UTAUT) precedes use; continued use (D&M) feeds back to perceived success and user satisfaction - this is the classic adoption-to-value chain
TTF + UTAUT TTF-TAM integration is common: fit (TTF) explains performance, while attitudes (UTAUT/TAM) explain intention - both needed for full model
IS Delegation → Human-AI IS Delegation provides conceptual foundation (what it means to delegate to IT); Human-AI Dynamics adds trust calibration and learning mechanisms specific to AI
5 Views ⊃ All Artifact Orlikowski & Iacono's taxonomy encompasses all artifact theories - each theory implicitly adopts one view (tool, proxy, ensemble, computational, nominal)

🎯 The IS Adoption Journey (Layered Process)

Stage 1: Artifact Conceptualization → Start with 5 Views of IT Artifact to position your study. Choose Affordance if you want analytical separation, or Sociomateriality if technology-practice are inseparable. Use Digital Object Theory for ontological grounding.

Stage 2: Adoption Decision → Apply UTAUT for behavioral intention, DOI for diffusion across time/population, TTF if task-fit matters. Combine TTF+UTAUT for full cognitive model.

Stage 3: Use & Success → Measure with DeLone & McLean (system quality → use → impact), add ARM if affective responses drive engagement.

Stage 4: AI-Era Extension → For advanced AI systems, integrate Human-AI Delegation Dynamics for trust calibration + reliance decisions, grounded in IS Delegation framework.

🔬 Research Design Pairings

Survey research (variance model): UTAUT + TTF + Trust Theory. Example: "Predicting chatbot adoption - UTAUT for intention, TTF for fit, Trust for perceived reliability."
Qualitative/interpretive study: Sociomateriality + Affordance (if you want some ontological separation). Example: "How do remote workers enact video conferencing affordances? Use Affordance Theory to code material features → perceived action possibilities → actualized use."
Longitudinal/process study: DOI (diffusion over time) + Structuration (how use changes structure). Example: "How does blockchain adoption spread and reshape governance? DOI for diffusion patterns, Structuration for governance evolution."
Design science research: Digital Object Theory (what you're building) + D&M Success (how you evaluate). Example: "Building a decision support system - Digital Object Theory to specify artifact, D&M to measure user satisfaction and impact."

⚠️ Common Mistakes & How to Avoid Them

Mistake 1: Treating UTAUT as a complete adoption theory. Fix: UTAUT predicts intention, not actual use or sustained value. Add D&M Success or ECT for post-adoption.

Mistake 2: Using Sociomateriality without committing to its ontology. Fix: If you still want to talk about "the technology" separately from practice, use Affordance Theory instead - don't claim sociomateriality then violate its core assumption.

Mistake 3: Ignoring the artifact view in your study. Fix: Every IS study implicitly takes a stance on the IT artifact. Make it explicit using Orlikowski & Iacono's 5 Views.

🌐 Cross-Domain Bridges (Part A ↔ Other Parts)

A → B (Organizational): UTAUT at individual level, Institutional Theory at organizational level. Example: "Why do firms adopt blockchain? Institutional pressures (mimetic, coercive) drive organizational adoption, then UTAUT explains individual user acceptance."

A → C (Behavioral): UTAUT overlaps with behavioral theories. UTAUT's effort expectancy ≈ self-efficacy (SCT), social influence ≈ social norms (TPB). Use together when multilevel.

A → D (Security): Human-AI trust calibration connects to security. Example: "Trust in AI decision support (Part A: Human-AI Dynamics) relates to insider threat (Part D) when users over-rely on flawed AI recommendations."

📝 Exam Usage Guide & Scenarios

Quick decision: AI adoption? UTAUT + Human-AI Dynamics. System success? D&M + TTF. Ontological positioning? 5 Views → Affordance or Sociomateriality.

Scenario 1: "How would you study AI chatbot adoption in customer service?"
Answer structure: (1) Artifact view: Ensemble (chatbot + workflow + training). (2) Adoption: UTAUT for intention + TTF for task-fit. (3) AI-specific: Human-AI Dynamics for trust calibration. (4) Success: D&M to measure service quality improvement. (5) Method: Survey for UTAUT, interviews for trust calibration process.
Scenario 2: "Explain why Affordance Theory and Sociomateriality are incompatible"
Answer structure: (1) Affordance assumes separable technology and users (relational but distinct). (2) Sociomateriality rejects ontological separation - technology and practice are constitutively entangled. (3) Affordance allows us to say "the technology affords X"; Sociomateriality says this misleadingly reifies technology. (4) Choose based on research question: if you need analytical leverage, use Affordance; if emphasizing inseparability, use Sociomateriality.
Scenario 3: "Design a study on IS success for a new enterprise system"
Answer structure: (1) Use D&M as foundation: system quality, information quality, service quality → use → user satisfaction + net benefits. (2) Add ARM for affective dimension (frustration, enjoyment). (3) Consider TTF to explain why quality matters (fit with task needs). (4) Longitudinal: track how use → satisfaction feedback loop evolves post-implementation.

🕰️ Theory Evolution Timeline (Historical Genealogy)

1980s Foundation: TAM (1989) emerges from TRA → establishes technology acceptance research stream

1990s Expansion: DOI applied to IT (Rogers 1995) + TTF (1995) adds task dimension → TAM-TTF integration begins

2000s Consolidation: UTAUT (2003) synthesizes 8 models → becomes dominant. DeLone & McLean updated (2003) for e-commerce era

2010s Sociotechnical Turn: Sociomateriality (Orlikowski 2007) challenges tool view → Affordance Theory (Leonardi 2011) offers middle ground. Digital Object Theory emerges for ontological clarity

2020s AI Era: IS Delegation → Human-AI Dynamics & Trust Calibration → addresses agentic AI systems beyond tools

Key insight: Early theories assumed passive tools; modern theories (Affordance, Sociomateriality, AI Delegation) emphasize relational/agentic technology

🔬 Methodological Fit Table

Theory Best Method Why
UTAUT Survey (SEM) Designed for variance testing; validated scales exist
Sociomateriality Ethnography, Case Study Requires observing practice-technology entanglement; not measurable with surveys
Affordance Mixed methods Qual to identify affordances, Quant to test actualization patterns
DOI Longitudinal (Panel/Time series) Diffusion unfolds over time; requires temporal data
Human-AI Dynamics Experiments + Interviews Trust calibration needs controlled AI performance; qualitative explains reasoning

📚 Theory-in-Practice (Real Published Examples)

UTAUT + TTF integration: "Examining the Factors Affecting Mobile Learning" (Al-Emran et al., Education and Information Technologies). Combined UTAUT's behavioral intention with TTF's task-technology fit to predict m-learning adoption. Found TTF moderates UTAUT effects.
Affordance Theory: "Enterprise Social Media Affordances" (Treem & Leonardi, Communication Theory). Identified 4 affordances (visibility, persistence, editability, association) as middle-ground between determinism and social construction. Widely cited framework.
Sociomateriality: "Sociomateriality of Algorithmic Work" (Orlikowski & Scott, Organization Science). Showed how algorithms and human judgment are constitutively entangled in credit-scoring - can't separate "the algorithm's decision" from "the human's decision."
DOI + Institutional Theory: "Cloud Computing Adoption" (various MISQ/ISR papers). DOI explains individual firm adoption; Institutional Theory explains industry-level mimetic diffusion. Multi-level complementarity.

⚖️ Boundary Conditions (When Theories Don't Apply)

UTAUT fails when: Mandatory adoption (no choice → behavioral intention irrelevant), post-adoption behavior (only predicts initial adoption)

TTF fails when: Technology is highly malleable (users adapt tech to task, not vice versa), social/hedonic value dominates (fit doesn't matter for entertainment IT)

Sociomateriality fails when: Technology is truly passive (e.g., static database), clear boundaries needed for policy/design (its ontology resists evaluation)

Affordance fails when: Users have zero agency (fully automated systems), technology properties are infinitely malleable (no material constraints)

Exam tip: Citing boundary conditions shows theoretical sophistication - acknowledge when your chosen theory doesn't fit perfectly

Philosophy/Ontology Faulkner & Runde, 2019 Important

Digital Object Theory (Faulkner & Runde, 2019)

An ontology for understanding what digital objects are through their material bearers and social positioning

Core Concept: Digital objects exist through multiple layers: bitstrings are borne by file formats, which are borne by storage systems, and so on. But what a digital object "is" and what it "does" depend fundamentally on its social positioning - the rights, responsibilities, and expectations assigned to it in specific organizational contexts.

🔗 Layered Bearers: Digital objects exist through nested material and nonmaterial layers (bitstrings, files, storage, systems)
👥 Social Positioning: Identity and system function emerge from rights, responsibilities, and organizational expectations, not technology alone
⚙️ System Function: What an object legitimately does depends on its position in the sociotechnical system
📊 Ontological Clarity: Precise conceptualization separates technical layers from social roles, enabling clear theorizing of IT artifacts
IS Relevance: Provides foundational clarity on what IT artifacts are, responding to critiques of under-theorization. Essential for moving beyond vague "technology" language to precise specification of digital object identity and function in sociotechnical systems.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Faulkner & Runde (2019), "Theorizing the Digital Object," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: an ontology for digital objects (material vs nonmaterial, bitstrings, syntactic objects) using bearer theory, plus the insight that identity and system functions come from social positioning.
Discipline of origin IS with roots in philosophy/ontology (objects and nonmaterial entities).
IS-native or borrowed? Developed in IS with philosophical foundations (ontology). Addresses under-theorization of the IT artifact using bearer theory from philosophy.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation of what digital objects are).
Core constructs Material vs nonmaterial objects, syntactic objects, bitstrings, bearers (material/nonmaterial) and layers, social positions (rights/responsibilities), system function.
Mechanism (plain) A digital object is always borne by something (material and sometimes nonmaterial bearers). But what the object "is" and what it "does" depends on social positioning and community expectations.
Boundary conditions Great for conceptual clarity, but hard to operationalize and test. Use it as a foundation for IT artifact theorizing, not a full EP model.
📚 Foundational Origins

Faulkner and Runde return to an old IS problem: we talk about "technology" all the time, but we are not always clear what exactly we mean by the IT artifact. This is a direct response to Orlikowski and Iacono's under-theorizing critique. In BCIS 6670 notes, the professor emphasized one takeaway as the most important: the idea of social positions and how rights and responsibilities give digital objects identity in a sociotechnical system.

💎 Theoretical Core
A digital object becomes what it is through social positioning: identity is not only technical

The paper separates material objects (physical bearers) and nonmaterial objects (like bitstrings and syntactic objects). It uses bearer theory to explain how a digital object exists through layers (a bitstring borne by a file format, which is borne by storage hardware, etc.).

The key IS insight: social positioning defines rights and responsibilities expected of an object in that position. That is why the same technical artifact can become "different" objects in different organizational settings.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

The course chain is clear in Week 2 materials: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) argues we under-theorize the IT artifact. Faulkner & Runde (2019) responds by giving an ontology for digital objects. Sarker et al. (2019) connects the discipline identity back to sociotechnical theorizing. Then Baird & Maruping (2021) uses a more agentic view (delegation) for AI-era artifacts.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Very strong logical structure; forces conceptual clarity about what a digital object is; makes social positioning central (very exam-ready).
  • Weaknesses: Can become cumbersome and hard to operationalize; easy to get lost in "where the bitstring lives" details.
  • Common misapplications: Treating the ontology as a predictive model; ignoring social positioning and using only the technical layer as "the object."
  • Alternatives/competitors: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) for views of the artifact; affordances for action possibilities; sociomateriality for entanglement-in-practice.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "How does the social positioning of a digital object shape what users treat as the object's legitimate system function?"

Example hypothesis: "When a digital object is positioned with clear rights and responsibilities, users will coordinate around it more consistently than when positioning is ambiguous."

What I would measure: Evidence of social positioning (formal role in processes, rights/responsibilities written into policies), plus evidence of how the artifact is enacted in practice (logs, handoffs, governance).

Seminal APA citation: Faulkner, P., & Runde, J. (2019). Theorizing the digital object. MIS Quarterly, 43(4), 1279-1302. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 2/Notes/Week2_Paper_Summaries.md, BCIS 6670/Week 2/Assignments/Critique_Faulkner.md, and extracted text files in BCIS 6670/Week 2/Papers/.

Connections to other theories: Digital Object Theory directly responds to Orlikowski & Iacono (2001)'s critique of under-theorization of the IT artifact. It also complements sociomateriality (Orlikowski 2007) by providing ontological clarity on what the artifact is before analyzing how it becomes entangled with practice. Use this connection in exams to show how digital objects have defined boundaries (per Faulkner & Runde) while their enactment is fluid (per sociomateriality).
Key people and seminal papers: Primary citation is Faulkner & Runde (2019) in MIS Quarterly. Also cite Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) for the under-theorization problem being addressed, and Orlikowski (2007) for sociomaterial practices. These three form a scholarly conversation about artifact identity and agency. For exams, emphasize that Faulkner & Runde's bearer theory and social positioning framework offers a different approach than affordances.
Exam strategy: Use Digital Object Theory when arguing for ontological precision in IS research. It works best when your research question requires clarity about what the artifact is before studying how it is used. Boundary conditions: this theory is not a user adoption model - avoid using it to predict behavior. Common mistake: treating social positioning as a design property rather than as emergent from organizational rights and responsibilities.
Digital Object Theory provides ontological boundaries (what is the artifact?), but does not explain how actors engage with it. Use it in concert with practice-oriented theories (sociomateriality, affordances) for complete explanatory power.
IS-Native Sarker et al., 2019 Important

Sociotechnical Axis of Cohesion (Sarker et al., 2019)

The sociotechnical perspective as the defining axis of cohesion and identity for the IS discipline

Core Concept: The IS discipline's distinctiveness rests on theorizing the interaction between social and technical dimensions. A six-type classification reveals that when IS research abandons sociotechnical theorizing, the field risks losing disciplinary identity, fragmenting internally, and failing to address humanistic and ethical outcomes.

🔀 Sociotechnical Axis: Discipline identity depends on theorizing the interplay of social and technical, not privileging one over the other
📊 Six-Type Classification: Papers vary from social-dominant to technical-dominant, with interaction types theorizing genuine interdependence
🎯 Distinctiveness Risk: When IS research becomes purely social or purely technical, it loses disciplinary distinctiveness and becomes vulnerable to fragmentation
⚖️ Humanistic Outcomes: Sociotechnical focus ensures attention to both instrumental and humanistic consequences of technology
IS Relevance: A discipline-level framework that clarifies what makes IS research distinctive. Essential for positioning research projects within the field and understanding how technology and organization truly interact rather than merely coexist.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Sarker et al. (2019), "The Sociotechnical Axis of Cohesion for the IS Discipline: Its Historical Legacy and Its Continued Relevance," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: argues the sociotechnical perspective is the axis of cohesion for IS, and offers a six-type classification of MISQ/ISR research along a social-technical continuum.
Discipline of origin IS (discipline identity and sociotechnical tradition).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Type I (analysis/classification) plus Type II (explanation of discipline fragmentation risks).
Core constructs Sociotechnical axis of cohesion, six types (Type I to Type VI), instrumental vs humanistic outcomes, distinctiveness and ethical failure risks.
Mechanism (plain) When IS stops theorizing social-technical interaction, the field loses distinctiveness, divides internally, and can fail ethically by ignoring humanistic outcomes.
Boundary conditions It is a discipline-level framework; not an individual-level causal model. Most useful as a critique and positioning lens.
📚 Foundational Origins

Sarker et al. were worried that IS research was drifting away from its sociotechnical roots. They reviewed 991 MISQ and ISR papers (2000-2016) and showed most work is either social-dominant or treats technology in narrow ways. BCIS 6670 Week 2 notes add exam-ready framing from Professor Sidorova, including the "Transport Test": if you can transport the model from an IT context (like IT security) to a non-IT context (like physical security in a school) with no change in constructs or argument, then the paper is primarily social and technology is not really theorized.

💎 Theoretical Core
The six types classify how papers treat the social and the technical - without a shared sociotechnical axis, IS risks losing distinctiveness

The six types are a classification of how papers treat the social and the technical. One clear use in comps answers is to show where a paper sits and why that matters.

If a paper is Type I social-dominant, it can be valuable, but it should not pretend to be sociotechnical. If a paper is Type IV (interaction), it needs an interaction mechanism, not only survey variables.
The mechanism is discipline-level: without a shared sociotechnical axis, IS risks losing distinctiveness, fragmenting into disconnected communities, and failing ethically by ignoring humanistic outcomes.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Week 2 materials also discuss why Type I dominates in top journals (practical reasons about data availability and publication incentives). For comps, I can use this to show I understand incentives without excusing shallow theorizing. This framework also connects to IT artifact debates: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) is a critique of missing IT; Faulkner & Runde (2019) gives an ontology; delegation work makes agency and authority explicit. The sociotechnical axis is the "discipline positioning" umbrella over these moves.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Gives a concrete classification of how IS research treats technology; provides language for discipline identity and ethical outcomes.
  • Weaknesses: It is easy to overuse as a policing tool ("everything must be in the middle") instead of a positioning lens.
  • Common misapplications: Treating the six types as survey categories without analyzing the IT artifact; claiming Type IV interaction without an interaction mechanism.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Sociomateriality and affordances are alternative ways to theorize the social-technical relationship, but they answer different questions (entanglement vs action possibilities vs discipline cohesion).
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "Does this IS study theorize social-technical interaction, or can it be 'transported' to a non-IT context without change?"

Example hypothesis: "The relationship between AI design features and user outcomes is stronger when the design features change work practices (social-technical interaction) than when technology is treated as background."

What I would measure:
  1. Evidence of technology-in-use (not only perceptions)
  2. Interaction mechanisms between social and technical
  3. Instrumental and humanistic outcomes together

Seminal APA citation: Sarker, S., Chatterjee, S., Xiao, X., & Elbanna, A. (2019). The sociotechnical axis of cohesion for the IS discipline: Its historical legacy and its continued relevance. MIS Quarterly, 43(3), 695-719. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 2/Notes/Week2_Paper_Summaries.md, BCIS 6670/Week 2/Papers/sarker_extracted.txt, and BCIS 6670/Week 2/Assignments/Critique_Sarker.md.

Connections to other theories: Sarker et al.'s sociotechnical axis directly builds on Orlikowski & Iacono (2001)'s critique and extends it with a discipline-level frame. It also aligns with Benbasat & Zmud (2003)'s call for IS identity and distinctiveness. In exams, use this framework to position other theories within the sociotechnical spectrum - show whether a given theory privileges social, technical, or genuine interaction.
Key people and seminal papers: Primary authors are Sarker, Chatterjee, Xiao, and Elbanna (2019) in MIS Quarterly. Key grounding papers: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) for the under-theorization problem and Benbasat & Zmud (2003) for disciplinary identity concerns. The Transport Test is a practical tool taught by Professor Sidorova in BCIS 6670: if a study's logic works identically in a non-IT context, the paper is Type I (social-dominant).
Exam strategy: Use the sociotechnical axis as a diagnostic lens in literature reviews and theory sections. Identify where prior research sits on the spectrum and argue for better positioning. Boundary condition: this is not a model for predicting user behavior - it is a discipline-level classification tool. Common mistake: claiming your work is Type IV (interaction) without showing the actual mechanism through which social and technical dimensions shape each other.
IS-Native Orlikowski & Iacono, 2001 Important

5 Views of the IT Artifact (Orlikowski & Iacono, 2001)

A taxonomy diagnosing how IS research conceptualizes and theorizes the IT artifact, from under-theorization to ensemble perspectives

Core Concept: IS research often under-theorizes the IT artifact, treating it as absent, nominal, or a black-box tool. A five-view taxonomy (Nominal, Tool, Computational, Proxy, Ensemble) exposes these patterns and calls for richer theorization where the artifact is embedded in organizational and practice contexts.

🏷️ Nominal & Tool Views: IT is either unnamed or treated as a stable black-box, risking under-theorization
🔢 Computational & Proxy Views: Focus on technical capabilities or surrogate measures (spending) instead of actual artifact properties
🔀 Ensemble View: Richest conceptualization where IT is embedded in users, organizations, and enacted through practices
⚠️ Discipline Distinctiveness: When IS research under-theorizes IT, the field loses distinctiveness and explanatory power
IS Relevance: A diagnostic and critiquing framework for IS research. Essential for literature reviews, positioning papers, and understanding how IT conceptualization affects theoretical strength. Pushes the discipline toward richer artifact theorization.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Orlikowski & Iacono (2001), "Desperately Seeking the IT in IT Research: A Call to Theorizing the IT Artifact," Information Systems Research. Key contribution: argues IS research often under-theorizes IT and offers five views (Nominal, Tool, Computational, Proxy, Ensemble).
Discipline of origin IS (self-critique about the discipline).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Type I (analysis/classification). Taxonomy of views rather than a causal model.
Core constructs Nominal view, Tool view, Computational view, Proxy view, Ensemble view; under-theorization of IT artifact.
Mechanism (plain) When the IT artifact is treated as "commonplace" and unproblematic, explanations of IT effects become weak. The taxonomy helps diagnose how papers treat the artifact and where it disappears.
Boundary conditions Best used for literature critique and theory-section design. It does not directly explain behavior outcomes.
📚 Foundational Origins

Orlikowski and Iacono reviewed ISR papers (1990s) and argued that in many studies the IT artifact is missing from theorizing or treated in a shallow way. Their discipline-identity point is simple: if "IT" is our subject matter but we do not theorize IT, then IS loses distinctiveness. Affiliation is visible in the PDF: Wanda J. Orlikowski was at MIT and C. Suzanne Iacono was at the National Science Foundation.

💎 Theoretical Core
The five views are a clean exam tool for diagnosing how papers treat the IT artifact

1️⃣ Nominal

IT is basically a label; the artifact is absent

2️⃣ Tool

IT is a stable black-box tool

3️⃣ Computational

Focus on computational capabilities

4️⃣ Proxy

IT is measured via surrogates (like IT spending)

5️⃣ Ensemble

IT embedded in users, organizations, and practices

In BCIS 6670 Week 2, the course chain was explicit: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) critique → Faulkner & Runde (2019) digital object ontology → Sarker et al. (2019) sociotechnical discipline identity → Baird & Maruping (2021) agentic artifacts and delegation.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS research, this paper became a reference point for pushing the field away from nominal/proxy treatments and toward richer artifact conceptualizations (ensemble views, artifact-in-practice arguments, digital objects, sociomateriality).

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Simple and powerful taxonomy; fast way to critique weak IT treatments; gives a clear angle in comps answers.
  • Weaknesses: Descriptive and does not provide causal mechanisms; a comps answer needs to go beyond critique and propose a better artifact theorization.
  • Common misapplications: Citing only "IT is under-theorized" while still relying on proxy measures; claiming an ensemble view without data on practices.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Digital Object Theory (ontology), sociomateriality (entanglement), affordances (action possibilities).
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "Which IT artifact view does my study implicitly adopt, and what does that hide or reveal?"

Example hypothesis: "Studies that treat AI as an ensemble (artifact-in-practice) produce stronger explanations than studies that rely on proxy measures alone."

What I would measure: Artifact features, usage practices, and organizational context (for an ensemble view). If I must use a proxy, I state the limitation and propose a plan for richer theorizing.

Seminal APA citation: Orlikowski, W. J., & Iacono, C. S. (2001). Desperately seeking the IT in IT research: A call to theorizing the IT artifact. Information Systems Research, 12(2), 121-134. Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Week 2/Notes/Week2_Paper_Summaries.md and scanned PDF in BCIS 6670/Week 2/Papers/.

Connections to other theories: Orlikowski & Iacono (2001) is the foundational critique that motivates Faulkner & Runde (2019)'s ontology and Sarker et al. (2019)'s discipline-level framing. The ensemble view aligns with sociomateriality (Orlikowski 2007) and affordances (Gibson; developed in HCI). Use this framework in exams to show how artifact theorization has evolved from critique toward positive ontologies and practice-based approaches.
Key people and seminal papers: Primary authors are Wanda Orlikowski and C. Suzanne Iacono (2001) in Information Systems Research. This paper catalyzed follow-up work: Faulkner & Runde (2019) responded with bearer theory and social positioning, and Sarker et al. (2019) positioned the critique within discipline identity. For exams, cite all three together to show a coherent conversation across two decades.
Exam strategy: Use the five-view taxonomy as a diagnostic tool to critique prior literature in your theory section. Argue that your study adopts an ensemble view (or higher) and explain why. Boundary condition: this theory is not a predictive model - do not use it alone to explain outcomes; use it as a foundation for richer theorization. Common mistake: merely noting that prior work was weak on artifact theorization without proposing a better approach.
The five views are a critique device, not a complete theory. In exams and proposals, go beyond diagnosis and offer positive theorization using affordances, sociomateriality, or digital object theory.
Psychology Zhang, 2013 Important

Affective Response Model (ARM) (Zhang, 2013)

A framework clarifying affective concepts and their relationships in ICT contexts through taxonomy and nomological network

Core Concept: Affect research in ICT is hindered by inconsistent concept definitions. ARM provides a structured vocabulary by organizing affective concepts (emotion, mood, feeling) across dimensions and proposing a nomological network showing how these concepts relate. This conceptual clarity improves measurement, hypothesis specification, and comparative understanding across studies.

🏷️ Affective Taxonomy: Distinguishes emotion (intense, object-specific), mood (diffuse, ambient), and feeling (subjective sensation) across dimensions
🔗 Nomological Network: Proposes explicit relationships among affective concepts showing how they influence each other and system use
📏 Measurement Clarity: Precise concept definitions enable defensible operationalizations that align across studies and prevent construct confusion
⏱️ Context-Specific: Tailored to ICT contexts and direct human-technology experience, not general psychology
IS Relevance: Exemplifies how theory papers can contribute through conceptual clarity rather than causal prediction. Helps IS researchers specify affective concepts precisely in technology contexts, improving measurement and enabling meaningful comparisons across empirical studies.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Zhang (2013), "The Affective Response Model: A Theoretical Framework of Affective Concepts and Their Relationships in the ICT Context," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: clarifies affective concepts in ICT via a taxonomy and proposes a nomological network (propositions) so affect research becomes more consistent.
Discipline of origin Psychology (affect) brought into IS/HCI in ICT contexts.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed concepts (affect), but theorized specifically for ICT context.
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (EP) with a strong Type I component (taxonomy).
Core constructs Affect (umbrella), emotion/mood/feeling, taxonomy dimensions, nomological network.
Mechanism (plain) The paper first clarifies concepts (taxonomy), then proposes relationships among them (network), so measurement and interpretation align across studies.
Boundary conditions ICT context and affective concepts (not a full cognitive model). Focus is direct experience with ICT.
📚 Foundational Origins
Zhang frames the problem as inconsistency: affect studies in ICT are growing, but concepts and definitions vary, which creates contradictions. Before asking what causes affect, we need conceptual clarity.

Zhang frames the problem as inconsistency: affect studies in ICT are growing, but concepts and definitions vary, which creates contradictions. Before asking "what causes affect" or "what affect causes," we need conceptual clarity about affective concepts and how they relate in ICT. The ARM is built around three research questions: (1) what are the pertinent affective concepts, (2) how are they similar/different, and (3) how are they related.

💎 Theoretical Core
ARM's core contribution is a structured vocabulary for affect in ICT contexts. The taxonomy organizes affective concepts across dimensions, and the nomological network proposes how these concepts relate.

ARM's core contribution is a structured vocabulary for affect in ICT contexts. The taxonomy organizes affective concepts across dimensions, and the nomological network proposes how these concepts relate.

For comps, the value is that you can defend measurement choices: "I am measuring mood, not emotion" is not cosmetic. It changes temporal dynamics, triggers, and interpretation.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In BCIS 6670, ARM is treated as a good "theory paper" exemplar because it shows what a clean conceptual contribution looks like: define constructs carefully, show relationships, and explain why definitions matter for empirical work. It also connects to the course's broader theme of "theory as clarity work" (not only theory as prediction).

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Reduces concept confusion; gives a defensible vocabulary and nomological structure; directly improves measurement and interpretation.
  • Weaknesses: Still requires good operationalization in context; taxonomy can be cited superficially without changing actual measures.
  • Common misapplications: Using "affect" as a catch-all label; mixing mood and emotion measures; claiming ARM but not aligning hypotheses with the network logic.
  • Alternatives/competitors: Related work in emotion and affect in HCI, but ARM is specifically ICT-focused. If the key story is cognition and effort rather than affect, cognitive load lenses may fit better.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Example research question: "How do different ICT design features trigger distinct affective responses (emotion vs mood), and how do these responses shape subsequent use?"

Example hypothesis: "In high-interruption contexts, ICT features that increase perceived control reduce negative emotion intensity and improve continued use intentions."

What I would measure: Separate measures for emotion vs mood, timing of experience, and the ICT stimulus type (object vs behavior stimulus), aligned with the ARM taxonomy.

Seminal APA citation: Zhang, P. (2013). The affective response model: A theoretical framework of affective concepts and their relationships in the ICT context. MIS Quarterly, 37(1), 247-274.

Connections to other theories: Zhang's Affective Response Model complements appraisal theory (Lazarus) by tailoring affective conceptualization specifically to ICT contexts. It provides the taxonomy for distinguishing emotion from mood, which is essential for using emotion-based theories like expectation disconfirmation or technology acceptance in IS research. Use ARM in exams to clarify which affective construct you are theorizing.
Key people and seminal papers: Primary author is Ping Zhang (2013) in MIS Quarterly, 37(1). Grounding in emotion theory: Lazarus and Zajonc on appraisal and emotion; Watson & Tellegen on mood taxonomy. Zhang brings this psychology foundation into IS by proposing an IS-tailored nomological network. For exams, cite the ARM paper first, then reference the psychology underpinning as needed.
Exam strategy: Use ARM as a measurement and concept-clarification tool. Before proposing hypotheses about emotion or mood effects, use ARM to define your constructs precisely. Boundary condition: ARM is not a causal model - it is a taxonomic and nomological framework. It tells you what emotion is, not when it causes behavior. Common mistake: conflating emotion and mood or using an emotion measure (like PANAS) when your theory requires mood.

Grounding in BCIS 6670 materials: BCIS 6670/Research Papers/Papers/Zhang2013AffectiveResponceTheory_MISQ2013.pdf.

IS-Native Baird & Maruping 2021 Important

IS Delegation Framework

Reframes IS use as ongoing delegation of authority between humans and agentic artifacts.

Core Concept: Delegation to agentic IS artifacts is governed by three interconnected processes: appraisal (assessing capabilities and risks), distribution (allocating authority and accountability), and coordination (managing interactions, overrides, and handoffs). This is a dynamic, adaptive process rather than a static adoption decision.

🔑 Three-Part Process: Appraisal (assess), Distribution (allocate authority), Coordination (manage daily interactions)
📊 Dynamic Adjustment: Delegation changes as experience grows, outcomes occur, and task conditions shift - not a one-time choice
⚡ Preference Alignment: Real delegation requires the artifact optimize human/organizational preferences, not only internal objectives
🎯 Boundary Conditions: Most salient with agentic artifacts (autonomy, learning, decision agency) and high-stakes tasks with accountability demands
IS Relevance: Shifts IS use theory from "intention to use" to fundamental questions of authority allocation, accountability, and governance in human-AI systems - essential for understanding modern agentic IS artifacts.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Baird and Maruping (2021), "The Next Generation of Research on IS Use: A Theoretical Framework of Delegation to and from Agentic IS Artifacts," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: reframes IS use as delegation of authority between humans and agentic IS artifacts.
Discipline of origin IS (drawing on delegation, authority, and human-automation research).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native (built for agentic IS artifacts like AI and robots).
Gregor (2006) type Type II leaning Type IV: it explains delegation dynamics and also supports prediction of when delegation will increase/decrease.
Core constructs Appraisal (evaluate capabilities and risk); distribution (allocate authority); coordination (manage interaction and handoffs); agent archetypes (reflexive, supervisory, anticipatory, prescriptive).
Mechanism (plain) People delegate when they believe the agent can perform well enough and when the cost of coordination is acceptable. Delegation is an ongoing adjustment of authority and accountability between human and system.
Boundary conditions Applies to agentic IS artifacts (autonomy, learning, and some decision agency). Less useful for traditional passive tools. Delegation depends on task stakes, explainability, and accountability needs.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Human-AI collaboration research in top outlets keeps expanding, including delegation and identity questions in JAIS and trust dynamics in MISQ.
📚 Foundational Origins
The IS delegation framework was proposed because "IS use" in the AI era is not just clicking buttons. With agentic artifacts, using the system often means handing over authority.

The IS delegation framework was proposed because "IS use" in the AI era is not just clicking buttons. With agentic artifacts, using the system often means handing over authority: letting an algorithm decide, letting a robot act, or letting an AI agent recommend a course of action. Baird and Maruping (2021) argue that classic IS use theories under-theorize this transfer of authority. Their framework puts delegation at the center and treats human-system relationships as something negotiated and adjusted over time. Even though the paper is IS-native, it responds to long-running ideas from delegation and authority in organizations and from human-automation interaction. The key IS move is to make delegation a theory of "use" that explicitly fits autonomy, learning, and dynamic behavior in modern artifacts.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Delegation between humans and agentic IS artifacts is a process with three connected components: appraisal, distribution, and coordination.

Appraisal

How people assess what the artifact can do, how reliable it is, and what the risks are.

Distribution

How authority is allocated (who decides, who acts, and who is accountable).

Coordination

How the relationship is managed day-to-day (monitoring, overrides, escalation rules, and handoffs).

The mechanism is dynamic. Delegation is rarely a one-time choice. As people gain experience, appraisal changes. As outcomes occur (good or bad), distribution shifts (more autonomy granted or taken back). As tasks change, coordination costs change.

Delegation is an adaptive governance process, not a static adoption decision.
Boundary conditions: Delegation becomes most salient when the artifact is agentic and when tasks have real consequences (medical decisions, hiring, credit, security monitoring). In low-stakes contexts, delegation can look like routine use. In high-stakes contexts, it becomes a question of accountability and control.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Since 2021, the broader IS conversation has moved quickly toward human-AI collaboration, accountability, and the social consequences of AI. Delegation fits naturally into that evolution because it gives a structured way to ask "who has authority" and "who is responsible" in sociotechnical systems.

BCIS 6670 Week 2 refinement: Delegation is not just "let the AI decide." It is about preference alignment. The class used a thermostat example: the thermostat might "prefer" keeping things very cold to maximize its longevity, but the human wants comfort (like 72F). If the artifact's objective is not aligned to the delegator's preferences, delegation is not real authority transfer, it is just automation that can work against the human.
