Example hypothesis: "Higher levels of bot-assisted fake engagement are positively associated with subsequent public attention to the targeted topic."

What I would measure (simple): Bot-assisted engagement (detected bots and fake interactions), public attention (search volume, posting volume, news coverage), and spillover to related topics. A good design is time series with shocks or quasi-experiments.

Seminal APA citation: McCombs, M. E., & Shaw, D. L. (1972). The agenda-setting function of mass media. Public Opinion Quarterly, 36(2), 176-187.

Marketing Engel-Blackwell-Miniard

Consumer Decision Process Theory

People move through decision stages from need recognition to post-purchase evaluation; different information matters at each stage.

Core Concept: Consumer decisions move through stages: need recognition, search, evaluation, purchase, post-purchase evaluation. High involvement decisions trigger deeper information processing; low involvement relies on cues and heuristics. Platform design, firm engagement, and information availability shape what consumers see during each stage, affecting search, evaluation, and purchase behavior.

🔑 Decision Stages: Need recognition → search → evaluation → purchase → post-purchase; staged process with different information needs
📊 Involvement-Dependent Processing: High involvement → deep information processing; Low involvement → cue-based heuristics and peripheral route
⚡ Information Availability: Reviews, comments, influencer engagement, and firm responses shape information available during evaluation stage
🎯 Platform Design Effects: E-commerce and social commerce platforms shape what information consumers access, potentially disrupting natural search/evaluation
IS Relevance: Explains how platform design, firm engagement, and e-commerce features affect consumer purchase journeys; shows why review systems, engagement metrics, and influencer presence shape sales outcomes.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Consumer decision process models in marketing (often associated with the Engel-Blackwell-Miniard tradition) describe stages from need recognition to post-purchase evaluation.
Discipline of origin Marketing / consumer behavior.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains how information, involvement, and evaluation processes shape purchase and engagement decisions.
Core constructs Decision stages (need recognition, search, evaluation, purchase, post-purchase), involvement, information processing, persuasion cues.
Mechanism (plain) People move through decision stages, and different information matters at different stages. High involvement decisions trigger deeper processing, while low involvement decisions can be driven by cues.
Boundary conditions Real decisions are not always staged and rational. People can be impulsive, social, and bounded. The theory fits better when decisions are deliberate and comparable across consumers.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" MISQ social media commerce papers use decision-process logic to connect engagement strategies to consumer behavior. In my course reading list, Ren et al. (2023) is a strong anchor for firm social media engagement and sales performance.
📚 Foundational Origins

Consumer decision process models were built to explain how people decide what to buy. The basic intuition is staged: people recognize a need, search for information, evaluate options, decide, and then evaluate the outcome afterward. This is not a perfect description of every purchase, but it is useful for structuring how information and persuasion work across time.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that decisions depend on information flow and involvement. When involvement is high, people process information more deeply and care about diagnostics. When involvement is low, people rely more on cues and heuristics.

IS mechanism: Platform design and firm engagement change what information and cues consumers see, which can shift how they search, evaluate, and decide.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS researchers use this theory to study e-commerce journeys and social commerce. A clean exam move is to connect it to platform features: reviews, comments, influencer disclosure, and firm responses are all inputs into decision stages.

Strong IS example: Ren et al. (2023) show how managing social media engagement connects to sales performance, which fits a consumer decision logic: engagement affects information availability and trust cues, which then shape behavior.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: It is practical and easy to explain. It helps you structure an exam answer about how digital channels shape purchase behavior.
  • Weaknesses: It can oversimplify. People do not always move through stages in order. Social influence and platform algorithms can also disrupt the "search and evaluate" story.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Treating engagement metrics as the same thing as decision outcomes, and ignoring endogeneity (firms engage more when customers are already interested).
  • Alternatives and competitors: Social presence and trust for relational mechanisms, prospect theory for risk framing, and information processing theory for attention limits.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use consumer decision process theory when the exam question is about how digital engagement strategies shape consumer behavior over a journey, not only one click.

Example research question: "When should a firm respond to comments on social media to increase conversion and sales?"

Example hypothesis: "Firm responses to user comments increase sales performance by improving consumers' evaluation of the firm and reducing uncertainty."

What I would measure (simple): Comment volume and sentiment, firm response timing and content, consumer actions (clicks, conversion), and sales outcomes. A stronger design would use instrumental variables or experiments (randomized response strategies).

Seminal APA citation: Engel, J. F., Blackwell, R. D., & Miniard, P. W. (1995). Consumer behavior (8th ed.). Dryden Press.

Psychology Lazarus 1991

Appraisal Theory

Emotions arise from cognitive appraisals of events in relation to goals and coping capacity; same event produces different emotions depending on interpretation.

Core Concept: Emotions are not automatic reactions; they depend on appraisal - how people interpret events in relation to personal goals and coping ability. Same event creates anxiety if appraised as threatening and coping feels low; creates anger if appraised as unfair and caused by others. Digital environments (social media, health communities) trigger emotional responses when users appraise exposure to others as threatening to self-evaluation or goal-relevant.

🔑 Appraisal-Emotion Linkage: Emotion type determined by appraisal content (threat, unfairness) and coping potential, not objective situation
📊 Goal Relevance & Congruence: Events appraised as relevant to personal goals and incongruent with those goals trigger stronger emotional reactions
⚡ Coping Potential: Events appraised as threatening with low coping potential trigger anxiety; same events with high coping potential trigger challenge emotions
🎯 Digital Amplification: Online visibility of others' outcomes, support, success creates appraisals of self-evaluation threat and goal incongruence
IS Relevance: Explains unintended emotional effects in online communities, social media, and digital platforms where users observe others and appraise their own standing and goal progress.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Lazarus (1991), Emotion and adaptation. Key contribution: emotions arise from cognitive appraisals of situations in relation to goals and coping resources.
Discipline of origin Psychology (emotion and stress).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains why specific emotions arise and how they drive behavior.
Core constructs Appraisal, goal relevance, goal congruence, coping potential, emotion categories.
Mechanism (plain) People feel emotions based on how they interpret (appraise) an event: is it good/bad for my goals, and can I cope with it? Different appraisals lead to different emotions.
Boundary conditions Strong for emotion episodes and stress contexts. Harder to use when emotions are weak, habitual, or socially performed rather than internally experienced.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" In MISQ health community research, appraisal logic helps explain how online exposure leads to unintended emotions and behavior. Zhou et al. (2023) is a strong example in this space.
📚 Foundational Origins

Appraisal theory was developed to explain why the same event can produce different emotions across people. Lazarus argues that emotions are not only automatic reactions. They depend on appraisal: how the person interprets the event in relation to goals and coping capacity.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: appraisal → emotion → action. If a situation is appraised as threatening and coping feels low, anxiety may arise. If it is appraised as unfair and caused by others, anger may arise. These emotions then shape behavior.

In IS, appraisal theory is useful when you want to explain emotional reactions in digital environments, especially social and comparative contexts (social media, health communities).

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS researchers apply appraisal theory to understand digital emotion dynamics (stress, envy, shame, pride). A clean recent case is online health communities: people may feel negative emotions not only from their own situation but from what they observe about others.

Strong IS example: Zhou et al. (2023) show unintended emotional effects in such communities, which fits appraisal and social comparison logic.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: It gives a mechanism for emotions that is more precise than just saying "people feel bad." It helps you link platform design (what people see) to emotional outcomes.
  • Weaknesses: Appraisal can be hard to measure in real time, and retrospective self-reports can be biased. Emotions are also socially shaped, so purely cognitive appraisal can miss norms and identity.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Treating emotions as generic positive/negative outcomes without specifying the appraisal process, and ignoring timing (emotion episodes change quickly).
  • Alternatives and competitors: Emotional contagion for spread, social comparison for self-evaluation, and ego depletion for resource limits when you want to explain lapses rather than feelings.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use appraisal theory when the exam question is about emotions triggered by digital exposure and how those emotions lead to behavior (disclosure, quitting, engagement).

Example research question: "How does seeing others receive support in an online health forum shape observers' emotions and participation?"

Example hypothesis: "Exposure to others' support is associated with increased negative emotion among observers when it is appraised as a threat to self-evaluation, which reduces posting behavior."

What I would measure (simple): Exposure (what content people see), appraisal indicators (self-evaluation threat), emotions (specific emotions like envy, sadness), and behavior (posting, quitting). A strong design uses experience sampling or platform trace data paired with short surveys.

Seminal APA citation: Lazarus, R. S. (1991). Emotion and adaptation. Oxford University Press.

Psychology / Cognitive Science Simon 1955; Galbraith 1974 IS: Wright et al. 2023

Information Processing Theory

How cognitive capacity limits and context affect deception detection and security behavior.

Core Concept: People have limited cognitive capacity. Context shapes what cues are noticed and how deeply they are processed, which affects deception detection. Phishing succeeds when attackers exploit attention limits and context pressures.

🔑 Bounded Rationality: People cannot process all information and rely on cues and heuristics under constraints
📊 Context Effects: Workload, social pressure, and trust reduce careful processing of diagnostic cues
🔄 Multilevel View: Individual processing is shaped by organizational and network context
⚡ IS Application: Explains phishing susceptibility through cognitive mechanisms rather than blame
IS Relevance: Provides a clear mechanism for why security violations occur even with awareness training by focusing on cognitive capacity and contextual constraints.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Information processing is a broad tradition in cognitive science and organizational behavior. In IS security, Wright et al. (2023) use a multilevel information processing perspective to explain deception detection and phishing susceptibility in context.
Discipline of origin Cognitive psychology / cognitive science / organizational behavior.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type II/IV: explains cognitive limits and processing mechanisms (Type II) and can support prediction of deception detection outcomes under different contexts (Type IV).
Core constructs Limited attention, cognitive capacity, cues, processing depth, deception detection.
Mechanism (plain) People have limited capacity. Context shapes what cues are noticed and how deeply they are processed, which affects whether deception is detected.
Boundary conditions Strong when cognition and attention are central. Less useful when the outcome is driven mainly by incentives, culture, or structural opportunity.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Wright et al. (2023) in MISQ is a direct top-outlet example connecting information processing to phishing susceptibility.
📚 Foundational Origins

Information processing perspectives start from bounded rationality: people cannot process all information, so they rely on cues and heuristics under constraints. In security, this matters because phishing succeeds when attackers exploit attention limits and context pressures.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Deception detection depends on processing. People detect deception when they notice diagnostic cues and have enough capacity and motivation to process them.

Context changes both attention and capacity. For example, workload, social pressure, or trust can reduce careful processing.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)
Multilevel perspective: Wright et al. (2023) make the perspective explicitly multilevel: individual processing is shaped by organizational and network context. This is useful for IS because phishing does not happen in a vacuum. Work routines, email volume, and social connections shape exposure and processing.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: It provides a clear mechanism for why people fall for deception even when they "know better." It shifts the story from blame to context and cognition.
  • Weaknesses: It can underplay deliberate risk taking and incentives. Also, cognitive processing is hard to measure without intrusive methods.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Treating awareness training as sufficient without considering context, and using generic self-report measures of "attention" that do not match the real task.
  • Alternatives and competitors: PMT for threat appraisal and coping, routine activity/opportunity for situational exposure, and neutralization for rationalization after violations.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use information processing when the exam question is about why people do not detect deception and how context changes cognitive capacity.

Example research question: "How do workload and social network position affect employees' phishing susceptibility?"

Example hypothesis: "Higher workload is positively associated with phishing susceptibility because it reduces available cognitive capacity for deception detection."

What I would measure (simple): Workload (email volume, task load), network position (centrality), phishing outcomes (simulation clicks, real incidents), and attention proxies (response time, email reading patterns). A multilevel design fits well.

Seminal APA citation: Wright, R. T., Johnson, S. L., & Kitchens, B. (2023). Phishing susceptibility in context: A multilevel information processing perspective on deception detection. MIS Quarterly, 47(2).

Organizational / Behavioral Johns 2006; Bamberger 2008 IS: Wright et al. 2023

Contextual Theory

How social and organizational context shapes cognitive processing and behavior across situations.

Core Concept: Context changes what people notice and how they interpret cues. That changes behavior even when the person's traits do not change. Workload, network exposure, and routines shape cognition and action differently across contexts.

🔑 Context Shapes Cognition: Workload, routines, and social networks affect what employees notice and prioritize
📊 Multilevel Effects: Context operates at individual, team, and organizational levels simultaneously
🔄 Specificity Required: Context works best when operationalized clearly and linked to cognitive mechanisms
⚡ Behavior Variation: Same person behaves differently under different contextual pressures
IS Relevance: Explains security behavior variation across teams and time periods by focusing on context rather than individual blame, supporting situational interventions.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work In my course materials, "contextual theory" is used as a label for the idea that social and organizational context shapes cognitive processing and behavior. A concrete IS formalization is Wright et al. (2023) on phishing susceptibility in context.
Discipline of origin Organization science / psychology (context effects).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed logic, but applied in IS security research.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains why the same individual can behave differently under different contexts.
Core constructs Context (workload, routines, social network), cognition, attention, behavior.
Mechanism (plain) Context changes what people notice and how they interpret cues. That changes behavior even when the person's traits do not change.
Boundary conditions Context is broad. If not specified, it becomes vague. The theory works best when context is operationalized clearly and linked to a specific cognitive mechanism.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Wright et al. (2023) is a direct top-outlet example: it connects work context and network context to phishing outcomes through an information processing mechanism.
📚 Foundational Origins

The simple idea is that people do not make decisions in a vacuum. In IS, context is often the missing piece. Two employees can have the same training, but different workload, different network exposure, and different routines. Those differences shape what they see and how they react.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Context shapes cognition, and cognition shapes behavior. This is not only "environment matters" as a vague statement. The mechanism is specific: context affects attention, processing depth, and cue interpretation.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)
Strong IS example: Wright et al. (2023) operationalizes context at multiple levels and links it to deception detection. The exam move is to argue that context is not just a control variable. It is part of the causal story.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Contextual theory prevents individual-blame explanations and supports richer interventions (changing workflow, reducing exposure, redesigning systems).
  • Weaknesses: It can be too broad. If you include too many context variables without a clear mechanism, you get noise and weak theory.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Saying "context matters" and then only adding context as a moderator without explaining why, or using context as a post-hoc explanation for unexpected results.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Institutional theory for macro context, routine activity for exposure and opportunity, and PMT for threat appraisal.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use contextual theory when the exam question is about why behavior changes across situations (phishing, compliance, adoption across departments).

Example research question: "Why do some teams become consistently more vulnerable to phishing during peak workload periods?"

Example hypothesis: "Teams with higher workload and higher external exposure have higher phishing susceptibility, mediated by reduced attention to deception cues."

What I would measure (simple): Workload, exposure (external emails), team routines (meeting load), and phishing outcomes. A good design includes time variation (within-person or within-team) to show context shifts.

Seminal APA citation: Wright, R. T., Johnson, S. L., & Kitchens, B. (2023). Phishing susceptibility in context: A multilevel information processing perspective on deception detection. MIS Quarterly, 47(2).

Psychology Watson & Clark, 1984

Negative Affectivity Theory

Stable personality tendency to experience and report negative emotions, relevant to survey method bias.

Core Concept: Some people carry a stable negative affective style that shapes how they perceive and report. This trait creates interpretive bias and can inflate relationships among self-report measures if not controlled.

🔑 Trait Stability: Negative affectivity is a stable personality characteristic across contexts
📊 Interpretive Bias: High negative affectivity individuals interpret situations more negatively
🔄 Common Method Bias: Artificial correlations can inflate when all measures are self-reported
⚡ Methodological Control: Use objective measures or include it as a control variable to separate trait from actual experience
IS Relevance: Critical for validity of self-reported security perceptions; helps distinguish actual behavior changes from survey response bias in IT use and security compliance studies.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Watson and Clark (1984) and related work define negative affectivity as a stable tendency to experience negative emotions and view situations negatively. This concept is often used in methods discussions about common method bias in self-reports.
Discipline of origin Personality psychology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type I/II: it classifies a trait (Type I analysis) and explains stable differences in affective experience (Type II). In IS, it is often used as a methods control rather than a main theory.
Core constructs Trait negative affectivity, negative mood, pessimistic appraisal tendencies.
Mechanism (plain) Some people are more likely to interpret events negatively. This shapes their survey responses and reported experiences across many measures.
Boundary conditions Most relevant for self-reported perceptions (satisfaction, stress, trust). Less relevant when outcomes are objective behaviors or trace data.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Negative affectivity is mainly a measurement and survey-quality concern rather than a main IS theory lens. It appears indirectly in discussions of common method bias and survey design. (Not a common "theory" citation in top IS empirical papers.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Negative affectivity was developed to capture stable personality differences. For IS comps, the reason it matters is methodological: if respondents have a general tendency to rate everything negatively, you can get artificial correlations across self-reported constructs. This is why negative affectivity is discussed alongside common method bias.

💎 Theoretical Core
Core claim: Stability - some people carry a negative affective style that shapes how they perceive and report. The mechanism is interpretive bias plus mood. This can inflate relationships among self-report measures if you do not design studies carefully.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)
IS usage: Negative affectivity is most often used as a control variable or as a warning about survey design. The exam move is to connect it to validity: if your key variables are all self-reported perceptions in the same survey, negative affectivity is one plausible alternative explanation.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: It helps explain why self-report data can be misleading. It gives you a concrete alternative explanation that is not "bad respondents."
  • Weaknesses: It can be over-controlled. If negative affectivity is part of the real phenomenon (for example, stress leading to security mistakes), controlling it away can remove real variance.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Using it as a checkbox control without thinking about whether it is confounding or part of the mechanism.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Common method bias frameworks (method variance), and research designs that use multiple sources (trace data + surveys) to reduce self-report bias.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use negative affectivity in exam answers when you need to discuss survey validity threats and alternative explanations (common method bias).

Example research question: "Do employees who report high technostress also report lower compliance, or is the relationship partly driven by negative affectivity?"

Example hypothesis: "Negative affectivity is positively associated with reported technostress and negatively associated with reported satisfaction, inflating their correlation."

What I would measure (simple): Include a negative affectivity scale, separate measurement waves, and use objective outcomes when possible (policy compliance logs). Then test whether relationships hold after controlling for trait affect.

Seminal APA citation: Watson, D., & Clark, L. A. (1984). Negative affectivity: The disposition to experience aversive emotional states. Psychological Bulletin.

Psychology Behavioral Genetics

Polygenic Score Theory (Biological Foundations of IT Use)

Biological and genetic foundations of individual differences in IT adoption and security behaviors.

Core Concept: Some variation in technology use and security behavior may have genetic components. Polygenic scores aggregate genetic predispositions relevant to IT use traits like openness and conscientiousness.

🔑 Genetic Contribution: Technology adoption traits have partial genetic basis
📊 Polygenic Aggregation: Multiple genetic variants combine to predict behavioral predispositions
🔄 Nature-Nurture Interaction: Genetic tendencies interact with environment and training
⚡ IS Implication: Some security-related traits (conscientiousness, risk awareness) have biological foundations
IS Relevance: Provides boundary conditions for training and intervention effectiveness; explains why awareness programs may not fully override biologically-based risk tolerance or openness traits.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Brown and Sias (2023), "The fault in our stars: Molecular genetics and information technology use," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: introduces polygenic scores as a way to connect genetic predispositions to IT use outcomes.
Discipline of origin Genetics / behavioral genetics.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed methods and theory, applied in IS.
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): predicts IT use from biological predispositions and explains part of variance through stable individual differences.
Core constructs Polygenic score (PGS), genetic predispositions (education, neuroticism), IT use over the life course.
Mechanism (plain) Genetic variants shape traits and capabilities that influence learning and behavior, which then influence technology use across the life span.
Boundary conditions High ethical sensitivity (privacy, discrimination). Also, biology is not destiny: environment and context still matter, and effect sizes are usually small.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Brown and Sias (2023) in MISQ is the key top-outlet proof-of-life example in my local set. This is a newer stream, so the top-outlet footprint is still limited.
📚 Foundational Origins

Polygenic score approaches come from genetics, where researchers aggregate many small genetic effects into a score that predicts an outcome or trait. Brown and Sias (2023) bring this logic into IS by asking whether biological predispositions relate to long-run IT use.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: NOT determinism. Stable predispositions can be one input into technology use patterns, alongside environment and opportunity.

The mechanism is indirect: genes influence traits (like education-related propensity or neuroticism), traits influence learning and comfort with technology, and that influences use.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)
Emerging IS direction: The big exam value is not "genetics explains everything." It is methodological and theoretical: it pushes IS to consider deeper sources of heterogeneity and to separate stable predispositions from situational drivers.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: It introduces a new source of variance that IS often ignores. It can help explain persistent individual differences in use even after controlling for demographics.
  • Weaknesses: Ethical risk is high. There are also risks of population stratification, weak generalizability across ancestries, and misinterpretation as destiny. Another weakness is that the link to IS theory can be thin if the paper does not connect biology to real mechanisms and contexts.
  • Common misapplications in IS: Over-claiming causality, ignoring ethics and governance, and treating genetic predictors as substitute for context and social factors.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Traditional behavioral theories (TAM, UTAUT, role identity) explain proximal beliefs and meanings. Contextual and institutional theories explain environment and constraints. A strong exam answer can position genetics as distal antecedents, not replacements.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use polygenic score theory when the exam question invites a discussion of deep heterogeneity, long-run adoption, or novel data sources (genetics) and their ethical implications.

Example research question: "Do genetic predispositions related to education predict later-life IT use, and does training moderate that relationship?"

Example hypothesis: "Higher polygenic scores associated with educational attainment are positively associated with IT use in adulthood."

What I would measure (simple): IT use measures (device use, online behaviors), polygenic scores from genetic data, education and training exposures, and strong privacy governance. Designs should be careful about sample composition and ethical consent.

Seminal APA citation: Brown, S. A., & Sias, R. W. (2023). The fault in our stars: Molecular genetics and information technology use. MIS Quarterly, 47(2).

📝 Practice Questions: Part C (Behavioral & Psychological Theories)

Question 1: Define trust according to Mayer et al. (1995) and explain the three antecedents (ability, benevolence, integrity). Why is trust especially critical in online environments, and how does swift trust differ from traditional trust?

Model Answer (100 words):

Trust is "willingness to be vulnerable to another party based on expectations" (Mayer et al., 1995). Three antecedents: Ability (competence in domain), Benevolence (caring about trustor's welfare), Integrity (adherence to acceptable principles). Online environments lack physical cues and repeated interaction that build trust, making reputation systems, security assurances, and interface design critical (Gefen et al., 2003). Swift trust forms quickly in temporary teams through role-based expectations rather than personal knowledge - fragile but necessary for virtual collaboration (Meyerson et al., 1996). Measurement: Trust scales (ability, benevolence, integrity subscales). Critique: Trust research conflates trustworthiness (attributes) with trusting behavior (risk-taking).

Question 2: Explain the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) and how it extends the Theory of Reasoned Action. Why does perceived behavioral control matter, and what IS contexts make TPB more appropriate than TAM?

Model Answer (98 words):

TPB (Ajzen, 1991) extends Theory of Reasoned Action by adding Perceived Behavioral Control (PBC) - belief in one's capability to perform behavior - recognizing not all behaviors are volitional. Three predictors of intention: Attitude (evaluative beliefs), Subjective Norm (social pressure), PBC (self-efficacy + controllability). PBC also directly predicts behavior when it reflects actual control. TPB suits contexts where volitional control varies: security compliance (constrained by policy), complex system adoption (requires skills), health IT use (ability barriers). TAM assumes ease-of-use captures control; TPB explicitly models external constraints. Measurement: Multi-item scales for each construct. Critique: TPB explains intentions better than actual behavior (intention-behavior gap).

Question 3: Describe Expectation-Confirmation Theory (ECT) and explain how Bhattacherjee (2001) adapted it for IS continuance. Why does satisfaction with continued use differ from initial acceptance, and what role does confirmation play?

Model Answer (99 words):

ECT explains repurchase behavior through expectation confirmation/disconfirmation (Oliver, 1980). Bhattacherjee (2001) adapted ECT for IS continuance: users form initial expectations, experience system, compare performance to expectations (confirmation), which affects satisfaction and updated perceived usefulness, jointly predicting continuance intention. Initial acceptance (TAM) emphasizes pre-use beliefs; continuance emphasizes post-use experience. Confirmation adjusts expectations - positive disconfirmation (exceeds expectations) boosts satisfaction. For example, cloud service users continue when reliability confirms/exceeds expectations. Measurement: Confirmation, satisfaction, continuance intention scales. Critique: Assumes rational evaluation; ignores habit and switching costs that retain dissatisfied users (lock-in effects).

Question 4: Explain Protection Motivation Theory (PMT) including threat appraisal (severity, vulnerability) and coping appraisal (response efficacy, self-efficacy, costs). How does PMT apply to cybersecurity behavior, and why do fear appeals sometimes backfire?

Model Answer (100 words):

PMT (Rogers, 1975) explains protective behavior through two appraisals: Threat appraisal - perceived severity (how bad?) and vulnerability (will it happen to me?) - and Coping appraisal - response efficacy (does countermeasure work?), self-efficacy (can I do it?), and response costs (too difficult/expensive?). High threat + high coping = protection motivation. In cybersecurity, employees adopt security measures when they perceive real phishing risk (threat) and believe antivirus software works and they can use it (coping) (Boss et al., 2015). Fear appeals backfire when threat is high but coping is low - inducing denial rather than action (Witte, 1992). Measurement: 5-factor PMT scales. Critique: Rational model ignores affect and social norms.

🔒 Part D: Criminological & Security Theories

Theories of cybersecurity, crime, and deviant behavior

📖 What This Section Covers

این بخش از «چرا آدم‌ها فناوری رو قبول می‌کنن؟» فاصله می‌گیره و می‌ره سمت «چرا آدم‌ها جرم سایبری انجام میدن و چطور جلوشون رو بگیریم؟» تئوری‌ها از criminology و sociology قرض گرفته شدن و برای حوزه cyber apply شدن.

Deterrence Theory: دو نوع: General (بازدارندگی عمومی - همه می‌دونن تنبیه وجود داره) و Specific (بازدارندگی خاص - فرد خاصی تنبیه شده). نکته مهم: certainty of punishment (قطعیت تنبیه) خیلی مهم‌تر از severity (شدت) هست. یعنی مهم‌تره که بدونی حتماً گیر می‌افتی تا اینکه تنبیه خیلی سنگین باشه.

Routine Activity Theory: جرم وقتی اتفاق می‌افته که سه چیز همزمان باشه: motivated offender (فرد با انگیزه)، suitable target (هدف مناسب)، و absence of capable guardian (نبود نگهبان). در فضای سایبری، guardian می‌تونه firewall، monitoring system، یا حتی awareness training باشه.

Neutralization Theory: مجرمان سایبری قبل از ارتکاب جرم، خودشون رو justify می‌کنن: «همه این کار رو می‌کنن»، «به کسی آسیب نمی‌رسه»، «شرکت خودش مقصره». این techniques of neutralization رو Sykes و Matza معرفی کردن.

Social Bond Theory (Hirschi): چهار bond که جلوی رفتار مجرمانه رو می‌گیره: attachment (وابستگی)، commitment (تعهد)، involvement (مشغولیت)، belief (باور به قوانین). وقتی این bond‌ها ضعیف بشن، احتمال insider threat بالا میره.

برای comps: تفاوت General و Specific Deterrence رو بدون. Routine Activity رو بتونی به یک سناریوی سایبری apply کنی. بدونی چرا فقط training کافی نیست (چون neutralization وجود داره).

Criminological & Security Theories explain cybercrimes, deviant behavior, and organizational security. These theories are borrowed from criminology and adapted to the digital and cyber context. In today's world where cyber threats (hacking, phishing, ransomware, insider threats) are major organizational concerns, understanding these theories is critical for designing security systems and policies. This section covers Routine Activity Theory (Cohen & Felson), General Deterrence Theory (Beccaria), Neutralization Theory (Sykes & Matza), Rational Choice Theory, Security Culture Theory, and Insider Threat Theory.

Why this is very important for the comprehensive exam: Cybersecurity is one of the most critical areas in IS. Exam questions frequently ask: How can security policy compliance be increased? (Deterrence, PMT, Fear Appeals, Compliance Theory) How to design deterrence mechanisms? (certainty matters more than severity) How to reduce insider threats? (Routine Activity, Neutralization, Rational Choice) What approaches exist for reducing cybercrime? (deterrence-based vs. compliance-based vs. culture-based).
Key concepts and theory families:
  • Crime opportunity and environment: Routine Activity Theory (motivated offender + suitable target + absence of guardian), Situational Crime Prevention (25 techniques - Clarke), Insider Threat Theory
  • Deterrence and rational choice: General Deterrence (certainty > severity > celerity), Specific Deterrence, Rational Choice Theory (cost-benefit - Cornish & Clarke)
  • Neutralization and moral disengagement: Neutralization Theory (5 techniques - Sykes & Matza), Moral Disengagement (Bandura - 8 cognitive mechanisms)
  • Security culture and compliance: Security Culture Theory, Compliance Theory (Tyler's procedural justice), Security Awareness & Training, Insider Threat (Fraud Triangle / MICE)
  • Exam tips: General vs. Specific Deterrence, certainty vs. severity, deterrence-based vs. compliance-based vs. culture-based approaches, how Neutralization explains why "good" employees violate security policies

🗺️ Criminological & Security Theories Map

How theories explain cybercrime, deviant behavior, and organizational defense

Opportunity & Situation
  • Routine Activity - Motivated offender + Target
  • SCP - Hardening targets
  • Insider Threat - Abuse of access
Choice & Motivation
  • Rational Choice - Calc. of sanctions
  • GDT - Fear of punishment
  • PMT - Threat appraisal (Defense side)
Social & Control
  • Social Learning - Peer influence
  • Neutralization - Justifications
  • Control Theory - Social bonds
  • Deviance - Norm violation
Organizational Defense
  • Security Culture - Shared values
  • Security Awareness - Knowledge/Attitude
  • Compliance - Rule following

🔗 Key Relationships

Routine Activity → SCP RAT explains crime convergence (offender + target + no guardian); SCP provides the intervention roadmap (increase effort, increase risk, reduce rewards, reduce provocations, remove excuses) - diagnosis to treatment
Rational Choice ⊃ GDT GDT is application of Rational Choice to legal sanctions: Certainty and Severity of punishment alter cost-benefit calculation. Both assume offenders weigh consequences, though GDT focuses specifically on formal sanctions
Social Learning + Neutralization Peers teach two things: techniques for deviant behavior (hacking skills) AND techniques of neutralization (justifications: "everyone does it", "no real harm"). Social learning transmits both the "how" and the "why it's okay"
GDT ∩ Compliance Deterrence is one path to Compliance (fear-based), but not the only path. Add Legitimacy (fairness increases voluntary compliance) and Awareness (knowledge enables compliance). Full compliance model = Deterrence + Legitimacy + Awareness
PMT ↔ GDT PMT = defense perspective (protect yourself from threat), GDT = offense perspective (fear sanctions). Flip sides: PMT explains protective behavior (install antivirus), GDT explains avoidance of deviance (don't hack because punishment). Use GDT for policy compliance, PMT for self-protection

⚔️ Offense-Defense Pairing Matrix

Offense side (explaining deviance): Routine Activity (opportunity), Rational Choice (cost-benefit), Social Learning (peer influence), Neutralization (moral disengagement). Defense side (protecting systems): SCP (target hardening), GDT (sanctions), Security Culture (norms), Compliance (rule enforcement). For complete model, pair offense + defense: "Why hacking occurs (Routine Activity) and how to prevent it (SCP)"

🏢 Multi-Level Analysis (Individual → Organizational → Societal)

Individual level: Rational Choice (individual calculation), Neutralization (individual justification), PMT (individual threat response). Focus: Why does this person violate/comply?

Organizational level: Security Culture (collective norms), Compliance Theory (organizational enforcement), Insider Threat (organizational vulnerability). Focus: Why does this organization have high/low violation rates?

Societal/Environmental level: Routine Activity (convergence patterns), Institutional pressures (regulations), GDT (legal system deterrence). Focus: Why is cybercrime prevalent in this context?

Cross-level integration example: "Employee piracy study - Individual: Neutralization (justifications), Organizational: Security Culture (anti-piracy norms weak?), Societal: Routine Activity (easy access to pirated software + weak enforcement)."

🛡️ Policy Intervention Mapping (Theory → Intervention)

If using GDT: Intervention = Increase certainty (monitoring, audits) and severity (penalties) of sanctions. Example: "Deterring data breaches - increase audit frequency (certainty) and GDPR fines (severity)."
If using SCP: Intervention = 25 techniques across 5 categories - increase effort (authentication), increase risk (alerts), reduce rewards (encrypt data), reduce provocations (streamline workflows), remove excuses (clear policies). Example: "Preventing insider threat - MFA increases effort, activity logging increases risk."
If using Neutralization: Intervention = Counter-neutralization messaging. Example: "If employees say 'everyone downloads pirated software' (neutralization), counter with 'most employees use licensed software' (social norms campaign)."
If using Security Culture: Intervention = Shape collective values through leadership, training, rewards. Example: "Build security culture - executive championing, gamified training, recognize secure behavior."

⚠️ Common Mistakes in Security Research

Mistake 1: Using GDT without measuring certainty AND severity. Fix: Deterrence requires both - high severity with low certainty doesn't deter (people think they won't get caught).

Mistake 2: Treating RAT as only about motivated offenders. Fix: RAT equally emphasizes guardians and targets. Most security interventions remove guardians or harden targets, not change offender motivation.

Mistake 3: Confusing awareness with culture. Fix: Security Awareness = individual knowledge; Security Culture = collective norms and values. Awareness is a prerequisite for culture but not sufficient.

🌐 Cross-Domain Bridges (Part D ↔ Other Parts)

D → C (Behavioral): GDT (sanctions) connects to Fear Appeals and PMT (threat/coping). Both use fear, but GDT is external (legal punishment), PMT is self-protection (shield yourself from threat).

D → B (Organizational): Security Culture (Part D) is organizational-level; it connects to Institutional Theory (Part B) when regulations shape security practices. Also connects to Organizational Learning (how firms build security capabilities).

D → A (Core IS): Insider Threat theory connects to Trust and IS Delegation - when employees abuse trusted access (delegation failure).

📝 Exam Usage Guide & Scenarios

Quick decision: Explaining hacking? Social Learning + Neutralization. Designing secure systems? RAT + SCP. Maximizing policy compliance? GDT (sanctions) + PMT (self-protection) + Legitimacy.

Scenario 1: "Why do employees violate security policies despite training?"
Answer structure: (1) Neutralization: "Policy is too strict" (denial of injury), "Management doesn't follow it" (condemnation of condemners). (2) Rational Choice: Perceived benefits (convenience) > perceived costs (low detection probability). (3) Lack of Security Culture: Collective norms don't prioritize security. (4) Intervention: Counter-neutralization (show real harm), increase certainty (audits), culture-building (leadership modeling). ~100 words.
Scenario 2: "Design a study on cyberbullying in online communities"
Answer structure: (1) Routine Activity: Motivated bullies + vulnerable targets + weak moderation (absent guardians). (2) Social Learning: Peers model aggressive behavior. (3) Neutralization: "They deserved it", "It's just a joke". (4) Prevention: SCP interventions (increase effort: CAPTCHA, increase risk: user reporting, reduce provocations: tone moderation). (5) Method: Platform data for RAT patterns, surveys for neutralization/learning.
Scenario 3: "Compare deterrence and self-protection approaches to ransomware"
Answer structure: (1) GDT (Deterrence): Increase certainty (international cooperation, attribution) and severity (criminal penalties) to deter attackers. (2) PMT (Self-Protection): Organizations assess threat severity (how bad is ransomware?) and response efficacy (do backups work?) to decide on protective measures. (3) Limitation: GDT harder for transnational cybercrime; PMT more feasible for organizational self-defense. (4) Recommendation: Combine - GDT at policy level, PMT at organizational level.
Criminology Cohen & Felson, 1979 Core for comps

Routine Activity Theory

Crime and security incidents happen when opportunity structures align - not just when people are "bad"

Core Concept: Security incidents occur when three elements converge: a motivated offender, a suitable target, and the absence of a capable guardian. Change any one element and incident rates drop - it is a theory of situational opportunity, not psychology.

🎯 Suitable Target: Exposed data, weak access controls, valuable assets
👤 Motivated Offender: Presence of someone with capability and intent
🛡️ Capable Guardian: Monitoring, authentication, supervision that blocks convergence
⏱️ Convergence: All three must align in time and space for an incident
IS Relevance: Core framework for IS security and cybercrime research - maps directly to access controls, monitoring systems, and organizational change contexts that create or remove opportunities
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Cohen and Felson (1979), "Social change and crime rate trends: A routine activity approach," American Sociological Review. Key contribution: crime occurs when motivated offenders, suitable targets, and lack of capable guardians converge in time and space.
Discipline of origin Criminology/sociology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS security and cybercrime research).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains when harmful events occur based on opportunity structures.
Core constructs Motivated offender, suitable target, capable guardian, convergence (time/space), routine activities.
Mechanism (plain) Security incidents happen when opportunity structures line up. Reduce any one element (offender, target, weak guardianship) and incidents drop.
Boundary conditions Focuses on opportunity, not offender psychology. Best for explaining patterns of incidents and situational risk, not deep motivations.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Routine activity logic appears in IS security work on opportunity contexts. In my local reading set, the clearest top-outlet anchors are Wang et al. (2019) on unauthorized access attempts and Liang et al. (2025) on M&A-related breach risk, so I log this as partial 2020-2025 coverage.
📚 Foundational Origins

Routine activity theory was developed to explain crime patterns without assuming crime is only about offender motivation. Cohen and Felson (1979) argue that crime happens when three elements converge in time and space: motivated offenders, suitable targets, and absence of capable guardians. The theory was originally used to explain social change and crime rate trends, but the mechanism is general: changes in routine activities change opportunity structures.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Opportunity. Even if motivation stays constant, incidents rise when targets become more suitable and guardianship becomes weaker.

In cyber settings, this maps cleanly to access rights, exposed data, weak monitoring, and limited deterrence. The causal mechanism is convergence. Security incidents happen when an offender has access to a target at a moment when guardianship is weak. Guardianship can be technical (authentication, monitoring) and social (supervision, norms).

Key exam move: Show that routine activity is not a theory of "bad people." It is a theory of situational risk.
Boundary conditions: Routine activity theory does not explain deep motivations or moral reasoning. It is best for explaining incident patterns and the effect of controls. If your research question is "why would an employee want to do this," you usually need a complementary theory like neutralization.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS security researchers use routine activity logic because digital systems create and remove opportunities. The unit of analysis can be log events (access attempts), organizational changes (role transitions), or structural changes that alter guardianship.

Strong IS anchors: Wang et al. (2019) studies unauthorized access attempts over time and frames the behavior through opportunity contexts. Liang et al. (2025) shows how mergers and acquisitions create integration complexity, which often produces temporary weak guardianship and exposed targets. Even if the paper is framed as complexity, routine activity logic helps you explain the situational opportunity mechanism during organizational change.

Routine activity also integrates well with deterrence perspectives and with neutralization theory. Routine activity explains the situational opportunity, while neutralization explains how people justify misuse once the opportunity exists.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Routine activity theory is simple and actionable. It gives you a clear way to design controls: reduce target suitability, increase guardianship, or reduce offender capability. It also fits well with security log data because many constructs can be operationalized directly (access, monitoring, exposure).
  • Weaknesses: The theory can look too mechanical if you ignore motivation and morality. Many violations are not only opportunity, they are also justification and stress. Another limitation is that defining "capable guardianship" can be tricky in digital systems where monitoring exists but is ignored.
  • Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is treating any security control as guardianship without specifying how it actually changes opportunity. Another is using routine activity to explain intentional fraud without considering motivation and rationalization.
  • Alternatives and competitors: Neutralization theory is a strong alternative when the mechanism is moral justification. Agency theory is useful for governance and incentives. Institutional theory is useful when policy and regulation shape security behavior at the field level.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use routine activity when the question is about exposure and opportunity in cyber incidents (targets, offenders, guardianship) rather than attitudes.

Example research question: "Does expanding employee access to systems increase unauthorized access attempts, even when motivation does not change?"

Example hypothesis: "Increases in system access privileges are associated with increases in unauthorized access attempts."

What I would measure (simple): I would use access control logs to measure privilege changes and unauthorized access attempts over time. I would also capture guardianship measures (monitoring alerts, audit frequency) and organizational changes (role transitions, restructuring) to isolate opportunity contexts. A simple exam point is that this design directly matches the theory: opportunity structures change first, incidents follow.

Seminal APA citation: Cohen, L. E., & Felson, M. (1979). Social change and crime rate trends: A routine activity approach. American Sociological Review, 44(4), 588-608.

Criminology IS-Native Core for comps

Neutralization Theory

How individuals rationalize rule-breaking and policy violations before and after committing them.

Core Concept: Before violating rules, people use neutralization techniques to manage cognitive dissonance: denying responsibility, condemning condemners, appealing to higher loyalty, or denying injury. After violation, these rationalizations persist.

🔑 Cognitive Dissonance: Neutralization resolves conflict between beliefs and behavior
📊 Technique Varieties: Denial of responsibility, injury, victim, condemner condemnation, higher loyalties
🔄 Pre- and Post-Violation: Both prevents and justifies violations after they occur
⚡ IS Application: Explains persistent insecurity despite knowledge of rules and policies
IS Relevance: Essential for understanding why policy compliance fails; shows that rule-breakers use moral reasoning to justify violations, requiring cultural and structural interventions beyond rules alone.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Sykes and Matza (1957), "Techniques of neutralization: A theory of delinquency," American Sociological Review. Key contribution: people justify deviance through neutralization techniques that reduce guilt.
Discipline of origin Criminology/sociology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS for security policy violations and insider misuse).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains why people violate rules even when they know the rules, by focusing on moral rationalizations.
Core constructs Techniques of neutralization: denial of responsibility, denial of injury, denial of victim, condemnation of condemners, appeal to higher loyalties.
Mechanism (plain) People protect their self-image by reframing rule-breaking as acceptable ("everyone does it", "no one is harmed", "I had no choice"). These rationalizations weaken internal controls and enable deviance.
Boundary conditions Works best for violations by "normal" people who still care about norms. Less relevant for offenders who fully reject the norm system.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Neutralization is used in MISQ behavioral cybersecurity work (often combined with ego depletion) to explain security policy violations over time.
📚 Foundational Origins

Neutralization theory was developed to explain why deviance can happen without a full rejection of social norms. Sykes and Matza (1957) argue that many offenders still accept conventional values, but they temporarily neutralize those values through justifications. This is why neutralization is useful for insider security behavior. Many employees who violate policy still see themselves as good people.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: People use neutralization techniques to reduce guilt and protect self-image, which makes rule-breaking psychologically easier.

The classic techniques: denial of responsibility ("I had no choice"), denial of injury ("no harm done"), denial of victim ("they deserve it"), condemnation of condemners ("security is unreasonable"), and appeal to higher loyalties ("I did it for the team"). The mechanism is moral disengagement through talk and reasoning. Neutralizations weaken internal controls. In IS settings, this often shows up as productivity justifications ("I needed to get the work done") or fairness justifications ("this policy is unrealistic").

Boundary conditions: Neutralization is strongest for "ordinary" offenders who still care about norms. Less relevant for actors who fully reject the norm system.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Neutralization became a core IS security lens because it matches the reality of policy violations. Many violations are done by employees who feel time pressure, convenience needs, or loyalty to work goals.

Classic IS anchor: Siponen and Vance (2010) in MISQ show how neutralization helps explain employee security policy violations and why awareness alone does not solve the problem. In the 2020-2025 reading set, Cram et al. (2024) is a useful anchor for within-person and time-sensitive designs.

Integration with ego depletion is common: depletion can explain when people slip, and neutralization can explain how they justify the slip.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Neutralization gives a realistic mechanism for insider violations. Explains why good people do bad security actions, and suggests interventions beyond training.
  • Weaknesses: Measuring neutralization is not always easy. Neutralization can be post-hoc rationalization after the violation, creating causal ambiguity if study design is not careful.
  • Common misapplications: Treating neutralization as a personality trait (it's more situational). Using neutralization language without measuring actual techniques.
  • Alternatives: Deterrence for punishment, Ego depletion for temporal lapses, Routine activity for opportunity. Neutralization is most useful for moral-justification mechanisms.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use neutralization when the question is about how people justify rule breaking (policy violations, piracy, misuse) to protect their self-image.

Example research question: "When employees bypass security controls for productivity, what neutralization techniques do they use, and which techniques predict repeated violations?"

Example hypothesis: "Employees who endorse stronger neutralization beliefs (especially appeal to higher loyalties) will report higher rates of security policy violations."

What I would measure: Neutralization using survey items mapping to classic techniques, combined with behavioral outcomes (self-reported violations and log-based indicators). A strong design is time-separated (measure neutralization first, then observe behavior) to reduce post-hoc rationalization problems.

Seminal APA citation: Sykes, G. M., & Matza, D. (1957). Techniques of neutralization: A theory of delinquency. American Sociological Review, 22(6), 664-670.

Criminology Becker 1968 Core for comps

General Deterrence Theory

Punishment severity, certainty, and celerity deter potential offenders

Core Concept: People weigh costs and benefits before committing crimes. Certainty of punishment is more deterrent than severity. Swift punishment (celerity) enhances the link.

⚠️ Certainty: Probability of getting caught
⚡ Severity: Harshness of punishment
🎯 Best For: Security policy enforcement, sanction design
📚 Seminal Citation

Becker, G. S. (1968). Crime and punishment: An economic approach. Journal of Political Economy, 76(2), 169-217.

🔄 Theory Contrast: Deterrence vs Neutralization

Two competing explanations for security policy violations:

Dimension Deterrence Theory Neutralization Theory
Core Question "Will I get caught and punished?" "How can I justify this to myself?"
Mechanism Cost-benefit calculation before action Moral justification before/after action
Key Constructs Certainty, Severity, Celerity Denial of injury, Appeal to higher loyalties
Intervention Increase sanctions and detection Block rationalization pathways
Best For Policy design, sanction structure Understanding "good employees" who violate
🎯 Exam integration: Use deterrence when the question is about sanctions and policy enforcement. Use neutralization when explaining why well-meaning employees still violate. Siponen & Vance (2010) combines both in one model.
Criminology Akers 1973 Important

Social Learning Theory (Criminological)

Criminal behavior is learned through differential association and reinforcement

Core Concept: Differential association with deviant peers, definitions favorable to crime, differential reinforcement, and imitation explain deviant behavior.

👥 Differential Association: Who you associate with shapes behavior
🎯 Best For: Security culture, peer influence on compliance
📚 Seminal Citation

Akers, R. L. (1973). Deviant behavior: A social learning approach. Belmont, CA: Wadsworth.

Criminology/Economics Cornish & Clarke 1986 Important

Rational Choice Theory

Offenders make rational cost-benefit decisions about whether to commit crimes

Core Concept: Offenders are rational actors who evaluate costs (punishment, effort) vs benefits (rewards). Crime-specific decisions depend on situational factors.

⚖️ Cost-Benefit: Offenders weigh risks against rewards
🎯 Best For: Security policy design, incentive structures
📚 Seminal Citation

Cornish, D. B., & Clarke, R. V. (1986). The reasoning criminal. New York: Springer-Verlag.

Criminology Clarke 1983 Important

Situational Crime Prevention

Reducing crime opportunities through environmental design and management

Core Concept: Crime can be prevented by increasing effort, increasing risks, reducing rewards, reducing provocations, and removing excuses.

🔒 Hardening: Making targets harder to attack
🎯 Best For: Security control design, system hardening
📚 Seminal Citation

Clarke, R. V. (1983). Situational crime prevention: Its theoretical basis and practical scope. Crime and Justice, 4, 225-256.

Criminology Hirschi 1969 Important

Social Control Theory

Weak social bonds lead to deviance; attachment, commitment, involvement, belief prevent crime

Core Concept: Four bonds to conventional society: attachment (to others), commitment (stakes in conformity), involvement (in conventional activities), and belief (in rules).

🔗 Social Bonds: Stronger bonds = less deviance
🎯 Best For: Organizational commitment and policy compliance
📚 Seminal Citation

Hirschi, T. (1969). Causes of delinquency. Berkeley: University of California Press.

Sociology Merton 1938 Important

Deviance Theory

Deviance results from strain between cultural goals and legitimate means

Core Concept: When people cannot achieve cultural goals through legitimate means, they may turn to innovation (deviant means), retreatism, ritualism, or rebellion.

🎯 Best For: Understanding workarounds, shadow IT, policy violations
📚 Seminal Citation

Merton, R. K. (1938). Social structure and anomie. American Sociological Review, 3(5), 672-682.

Psychology Bandura 1999 Important

Moral Disengagement

Cognitive mechanisms that allow people to behave unethically without guilt

Core Concept: Eight mechanisms: moral justification, euphemistic labeling, advantageous comparison, displacement of responsibility, diffusion of responsibility, disregard of consequences, dehumanization, attribution of blame.

🎯 Best For: Unethical IS behavior, insider threats, data misuse
📚 Seminal Citation

Bandura, A. (1999). Moral disengagement in the perpetration of inhumanities. Personality and Social Psychology Review, 3(3), 193-209.

Psychology/Org Tetlock 1992 Important

Accountability Theory

When people expect to justify decisions, they think more carefully

Core Concept: Accountability - expectation of having to justify one's actions - increases cognitive effort and reduces biased decision-making under certain conditions.

🎯 Best For: Audit mechanisms, decision logging, AI accountability
📚 Seminal Citation

Tetlock, P. E. (1992). The impact of accountability on judgment and choice. Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 25, 331-376.

Security/IS Multiple sources Important

Compliance Theory (IS Security)

Understanding why employees do or don't follow security policies

Core Concept: Integrative model combining deterrence, neutralization, protection motivation, and organizational factors to explain security policy compliance.

🎯 Best For: Security policy compliance research
📚 Seminal Citation

Siponen, M., & Vance, A. (2010). Neutralization: New insights into the problem of employee information systems security policy violations. MIS Quarterly, 34(3), 487-502.

Security Multiple sources Important

Insider Threat Theory

Understanding how trusted insiders become security threats

Core Concept: Insider threats develop through personal predisposition + stressor + organizational opportunity. Grievance, financial pressure, and ideological motivation are common paths.

🎯 Best For: Insider threat programs, behavioral indicators
📚 Seminal Citation

Shaw, E. D., Ruby, K. G., & Post, J. M. (1998). The insider threat to information systems. Security Awareness Bulletin, 2(98), 1-10.

Security/Org Multiple sources Important

Security Culture

Shared values, beliefs, and practices about information security

Core Concept: Security culture encompasses shared assumptions, values, and behaviors that shape how employees approach security. Goes beyond formal policy to informal norms.

🎯 Best For: Security climate, organizational security posture
📚 Seminal Citation

Ruighaver, A. B., Maynard, S. B., & Chang, S. (2007). Organisational security culture: Extending the end-user perspective. Computers & Security, 26(1), 56-62.

Security/IS Multiple sources Important

Security Awareness

Knowledge and attention to security threats, policies, and protective behaviors

Core Concept: Security awareness includes knowledge of policies, threats, and consequences, plus motivation to comply. Necessary but not sufficient for behavior change.

🎯 Best For: Training programs, awareness campaigns
📚 Seminal Citation

Bulgurcu, B., Cavusoglu, H., & Benbasat, I. (2010). Information security policy compliance. MIS Quarterly, 34(3), 523-548.

Psychology Slovic 1987 Important

Risk Perception

Subjective judgments about the probability and severity of risks

Core Concept: Risk perception is influenced by affect heuristic, dread, familiarity, and controllability. Objective risk often differs from perceived risk.

🎯 Best For: Security behavior, threat communication, privacy decisions
📚 Seminal Citation

Slovic, P. (1987). Perception of risk. Science, 236(4799), 280-285.

Criminology Brantingham & Brantingham

Environmental Criminology / Opportunity Theory

How physical and temporal environments create opportunities for security breaches and violations.

Core Concept: Crime (including insider threats and data theft) requires three elements: motivated offender, suitable target, and absence of guardianship. Opportunity theory focuses on how environment enables violation.

🔑 Routine Activity: Environmental patterns create predictable opportunities and guard gaps
📊 Three Elements: Motivated offender + suitable target + lack of guardianship = violation opportunity
🔄 Situational Design: Redesigning workflows and systems reduces opportunity for violation
⚡ IS Security: Access controls, monitoring, and system design reduce opportunity regardless of offender motivation
IS Relevance: Practical framework for designing secure systems; shifts focus from policing intent to reducing structural opportunity for violations through architecture and workflow design.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Brantingham and Brantingham (1981), Environmental Criminology. Key contribution: crime patterns are shaped by environmental and situational opportunity structures, including place and routine movement.
Discipline of origin Criminology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS to study insider threat and unauthorized access as opportunity-driven).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains misconduct through opportunity contexts, not only motives.
Core constructs Opportunity, situational context, environmental conditions, access, guardianship, target exposure.
Mechanism (plain) Access and situational opportunity create temptation and lower the cost of misconduct, increasing the chance of incidents even when motivation is constant.
Boundary conditions Strongest for situational explanations and incident patterns. Does not fully explain offender motivation or moral reasoning without combining with other theories.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Opportunity logic appears in MISQ work showing that system access itself increases unauthorized access attempts, highlighting opportunity beyond motivation.
📚 Foundational Origins

Environmental criminology developed as a way to explain crime patterns through place and opportunity. Brantingham and Brantingham (1981) argue that crime is not randomly distributed. It clusters where opportunities exist. The key idea is that motivation may be relatively stable, but opportunity varies across environments, and that variation drives incident patterns. In IS security, this maps well to insider threats. An employee may have similar motivation across days, but if they gain access to sensitive systems or if monitoring weakens, the opportunity to offend increases. This is why opportunity-focused theories are attractive in log-based security research.

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: Opportunity is a key driver of misconduct, sometimes even more than motivation. If the environment makes misconduct easy and low-risk, incidents rise.

In IS terms, opportunity is created by access rights, weak authentication, poor monitoring, and transitions (role changes, mergers). The mechanism is not "people are bad" but that systems can unintentionally create low-cost paths to misuse.

Boundary conditions: Strongest for explaining incident patterns and situational risk, but not why a specific person violates norms. For that, need neutralization or deterrence theory.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, environmental criminology and opportunity logic are often used with routine activity theory. Routine activity provides three-element structure (offender, target, guardian), while opportunity theory emphasizes that access and situational conditions drive attempts even when motivation is constant.

Strong IS example: Wang et al. (2019) provide empirical evidence using longitudinal data: having system access itself increases attempts, suggesting opportunity creates temptation. This links theory to behavioral trace data, not only surveys.

Integration with complexity theory: As organizations become more complex, opportunity contexts multiply. Complexity can weaken guardianship and increase accidental exposure.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Practical and measurable. Fits log data and guides interventions. Avoids blaming only "bad actors" by showing how systems create risk.
  • Weaknesses: Can underplay agency and moral reasoning. Can be used deterministically ("if access exists, crime happens"), which oversimplifies.
  • Common misapplications: Using opportunity language without measuring it (access, monitoring, exposure). Ignoring interaction with motivation and rationalization.
  • Alternatives: Deterrence for sanctions, Neutralization for moral justification, PMT for threat appraisal. Opportunity best for situational access questions.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use note: Use opportunity theory when the question is about how environment and system design create opportunities for misuse, and how changing the situation reduces incidents.

Example research question: "Does expanding employee access to systems increase unauthorized access attempts, even when employee motivation does not change?"

Example hypothesis: "Increases in system access privileges are associated with increases in unauthorized access attempts."

What I would measure: Access control logs to measure privilege changes and unauthorized access attempts over time. Capture guardianship measures (monitoring, alerts) and organizational changes (role transitions, restructuring) to isolate opportunity contexts.

Seminal APA citation: Brantingham, P. J., & Brantingham, P. L. (1981). Environmental criminology. Beverly Hills, CA: Sage.

Psychology Rogers, 1975

Protection Motivation Theory (PMT)

How threat and efficacy beliefs drive protective behavior and security compliance.

Core Concept: Protection motivation arises from threat appraisal (severity + vulnerability) and coping appraisal (response efficacy + self-efficacy). High threat plus high efficacy beliefs promote protective behavior.

🔑 Threat Appraisal: Severity and vulnerability of threat influence motivation to protect
📊 Efficacy Appraisal: Belief that response works and that you can do it both matter equally
🔄 Fear Without Efficacy: High threat + low efficacy = denial and avoidance, not protection
⚡ IS Application: Explains why fear-based security training often fails without capability building
IS Relevance: Fundamental for designing effective security training; reveals why threat-only messaging backfires unless paired with capability-building and accessible protective responses.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Rogers (1975), "A protection motivation theory of fear appeals and attitude change," The Journal of Psychology. Key contribution: explains protective behavior as the result of threat appraisal and coping appraisal.
Discipline of origin Psychology (health behavior and fear appeals).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (widely used in IS security and privacy behavior research).
Gregor (2006) type Type II/IV: PMT explains why people take protective actions and supports prediction of protection intentions when constructs are measured well.
Core constructs Threat appraisal (severity, vulnerability, rewards), coping appraisal (response efficacy, self-efficacy, response costs), protection motivation, protective behavior.
Mechanism (plain) People protect themselves when they feel the threat is real and serious, and when they believe the protective action works, they can do it, and the cost is not too high.
Boundary conditions Works best for deliberate protective choices. Less suitable for habitual behavior, fatigue-driven lapses, or contexts where behavior is constrained by organizational power and workflow.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" In recent IS security work, PMT-family logic often shows up through fear-appeal and message-effectiveness studies and through extensions like the Extended Parallel Process Model (EPPM). Chen et al. (2021) in ISR is a clear example using EPPM in the security policy compliance context.
📚 Foundational Origins

PMT was created to explain why fear appeals sometimes work and sometimes backfire. Rogers argues that protective behavior is not driven by fear alone. It depends on two appraisals. Threat appraisal answers: "How bad is this, and how likely is it to happen to me?" Coping appraisal answers: "Does the recommended action work, can I do it, and what will it cost me?" Protection motivation is the intention that comes from these appraisals.

💎 Theoretical Core
Core proposition: threat appraisal + coping appraisal → protective action. In IS security, this maps to behaviors like enabling MFA, updating software, using strong passwords.
Boundary conditions: PMT is not a full theory of organizational security behavior. People can intend to protect but fail due to time pressure, depletion, conflicting incentives, or policy misfit. PMT often needs organizational context theories.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS work extends PMT: adapting to workplace constraints and using fear-appeal message frameworks. EPPM (Extended Parallel Process Model) distinguishes danger control (protective action) from fear control (defensive avoidance).

IS example: Chen et al. (2021) use EPPM to explain inconsistent compliance. Exam move: Keep mechanisms distinct - PMT is appraisal-based, deterrence is incentive-based, neutralization is moral-justification-based.
⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Mechanism-focused, easy to operationalize. Produces clear hypotheses. Flexible - can plug in different threats and actions.
  • Weaknesses: Can over-assume rational appraisal. Many studies measure intention, not behavior, then claim protection.
  • Common misapplications: Using PMT as checklist without specifying threat/action, collapsing self-efficacy and response efficacy, ignoring organizational constraints.
  • Alternatives: TTAT (IS-specific), routine activity for exposure, ego depletion for temporal lapses.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use: Use PMT when question is about why people take (or don't take) protective actions, especially when threat perceptions and coping beliefs are central.

Example research question: "What drives employees to enable MFA, and when do time costs override perceived risk?"

Example hypothesis: "Self-efficacy and response efficacy are positively associated with MFA adoption intentions, while response cost is negatively associated."

What I would measure: Threat severity and vulnerability perceptions, response efficacy and self-efficacy for MFA, response costs (time, friction), plus actual enabling behavior from system logs.

Seminal APA citation: Rogers, R. W. (1975). A protection motivation theory of fear appeals and attitude change. The Journal of Psychology, 91(1), 93-114.

IS-Native Liang & Xue, 2009

Technology Threat Avoidance Theory (TTAT)

How individuals manage threats to personal data and privacy through technology use decisions.

Core Concept: When individuals perceive threats (data breaches, surveillance), they decide to use protective technologies. Decision depends on threat level, efficacy of protection, and costs of adoption.

🔑 Threat Perception: Privacy violations and data theft trigger threat avoidance motivation
📊 Efficacy Judgment: Protective technology effectiveness and usability affect adoption decisions
🔄 Cost-Benefit Analysis: Privacy protection tool adoption depends on tradeoffs with convenience
⚡ IS Relevance: Explains adoption patterns for VPNs, password managers, encryption, and privacy tools
IS Relevance: Directly applicable to technology adoption for security; shows that privacy protection requires both threat salience and usable, effective technical solutions.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Liang and Xue (2009), "Avoidance of information technology threats: A theoretical perspective," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: introduces TTAT as an IS-grounded model of threat avoidance (threat appraisal + safeguard appraisal -> avoidance motivation -> avoidance behavior). Liang and Xue (2010) provides an early empirical test in personal computer usage.
Discipline of origin IS (built by adapting PMT-style appraisal logic to IT threat avoidance).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native (with psychology roots).
Gregor (2006) type Type IV (explanation and prediction): specifies appraisals that explain and predict avoidance motivation and avoidance behavior.
Core constructs Perceived threat (severity, susceptibility), safeguard effectiveness, safeguard cost, self-efficacy, avoidance motivation, avoidance behavior.
Mechanism (plain) People avoid IT threats when they feel threatened and believe the safeguards work, are affordable, and doable. Motivation mediates the move from appraisal to behavior.
Boundary conditions Best for user-facing threat avoidance (malware, phishing defenses, safe computing). Less suited for organizational governance decisions where incentives and power dominate.
