2020-2025 IS "proof of life" TTAT is a core behavioral security lens, but in my local reading set I did not verify multiple 2020-2025 top-outlet papers that use TTAT as the main framing.
📚 Foundational Origins

TTAT was created because security behavior in personal computing is not only about "compliance." It is about avoidance: avoiding risky sites, avoiding unsafe behavior, and using safeguards. Liang and Xue propose TTAT as a theory of threat avoidance in personal computer usage. The theory adapts threat and coping appraisals into an IS model with clear IT-specific constructs (safeguard effectiveness and cost) and a clear motivational mediator (avoidance motivation).

💎 Theoretical Core
Main proposition: threat appraisal and safeguard appraisal jointly drive avoidance motivation, which then drives avoidance behavior. Mechanism: appraisal → motivation → behavior.
Boundary conditions: TTAT fits best at individual level and for behaviors under user's control. If behavior is constrained by organization policy, time pressure, or workflow, need additional theories.
🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)
Positioning TTAT vs PMT: PMT is a general psychology theory of protection, while TTAT is an IS-native theory tailored to technology threats and safeguards.

TTAT integrates with contextual and temporal perspectives. Even if threat appraisal is high, fatigue and workload can reduce self-efficacy and increase perceived cost of safeguards. This is where TTAT can be combined with ego depletion or contextual information processing.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)
  • Strengths: Specific to IT threats with clear constructs matching real security interventions. Easy to use for hypothesis testing and designing interventions.
  • Weaknesses: Can be too individualistic. Many studies rely on self-reported intentions rather than observed behavior.
  • Common misapplications: Treating avoidance motivation as same as compliance, ignoring actual safeguard constraints, not specifying threat/safeguard clearly.
  • Alternatives: PMT for broader protection, routine activity for exposure, neutralization for rationalization.
⚠️ Practical Application for Exam
Exam use: Use TTAT when question is about user threat avoidance (phishing, malware) and mechanism is about threat and safeguard appraisals.

Example research question: "What drives employees to avoid risky links and use secure browsing tools during high workload periods?"

Example hypothesis: "Safeguard effectiveness and self-efficacy are positively associated with threat avoidance motivation, which is positively associated with threat avoidance behavior."

What I would measure: Perceived threat severity/susceptibility, perceived safeguard effectiveness and cost, self-efficacy, then behavior from simulations or logs (click-through rates, safe browsing tool usage). Measure workload to capture boundary conditions.

Seminal APA citation: Liang, H., & Xue, Y. (2009). Avoidance of information technology threats: A theoretical perspective. MIS Quarterly, 33(1), 71-90.

Organizational Thornton & Ocasio, 2008

Institutional Logics

How competing institutional belief systems shape organizational security practices and compliance.

Core Concept: Organizations contain multiple institutional logics: market logic (efficiency, profit), bureaucratic logic (rules, stability), professional logic (expertise, reputation). These compete and shape IT security decisions.

🔑 Competing Logics: Organizations navigate multiple legitimate but conflicting institutional pressures
📊 Logic Influence: Which logic dominates shapes whether security is cost, compliance, or expertise-driven
🔄 Practice Heterogeneity: Security practices vary across organizations based on dominant institutional logic
⚡ IS Application: Explains why similar companies differ in security investment and governance
IS Relevance: Explains organizational variation in security investment and culture; shows that security effectiveness depends on institutional alignment, not just best practices.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Thornton, Ocasio, and Lounsbury (2012), The Institutional Logics Perspective: A New Approach to Culture, Structure, and Process. Key contribution: organizations are shaped by multiple competing logics (market, professional, state, community, etc).
Discipline of origin Sociology and organizational theory (institutionalism).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS to explain value conflicts, power shifts, and heterogeneity in digital change).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains variation and conflict through competing cultural logics rather than assuming one institutional environment.
Core constructs Institutional logics, organizing principles, legitimacy, fields, hybridity, logic conflict and accommodation.
Mechanism (plain) Different logics define what is legitimate and valuable. Technology can reinforce one logic, weaken another, or create hybrids, which changes practices and power.
Boundary conditions Best for settings with multiple stakeholder groups and value conflicts (healthcare, platforms, public sector). Needs careful empirical grounding (you must show the logics, not just name them).
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" MISQ work uses institutional logics to explain how IT affordances shape societal change and shift power between logics.
📚 Foundational Origins

Institutional logics grew out of institutional theory, but it focuses more on heterogeneity and conflict. Classic institutional theory often emphasizes isomorphism and convergence. Institutional logics asks: convergence toward what? It argues that society contains multiple logics, each with its own values, norms, and "ways of organizing" (for example, market logic values efficiency and profit, professional logic values expertise and standards, state logic values rules and public interest). Thornton, Ocasio, and Lounsbury (2012) provide the core synthesis of this perspective.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that organizational action is shaped by multiple, sometimes competing logics. Outcomes depend on which logic dominates, how logics conflict, and how actors build hybrids. This is why institutional logics is often used when you want to explain variation and power struggles, not only convergence. The mechanism is meaning and legitimacy. Logics define what counts as legitimate and valuable. When technology is introduced, it can reinforce one logic, weaken another, or enable hybrid practices. That then changes routines, roles, and power because different groups gain or lose legitimacy under different logics. Boundary conditions include empirical grounding. A common reviewer critique is "you named a logic but did not show it." Good institutional logics work must show how the logics are present in discourse, practices, and governance, not only claim they exist.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, institutional logics is often used to study digital transformation in settings with strong value conflict, like healthcare, public sector, platforms, and societal change. It is also frequently combined with technology affordance thinking to show how IT matters in shifting practices and power. Faik et al. (2020) is a strong MISQ example because it uses an affordance-based institutional logics perspective to explain how IT matters in societal change. This is a clean comps example: affordances explain "how IT enables action," and institutional logics explain "why certain actions become legitimate and powerful." Integration with institutional theory is common. In comps answers, the clean contrast is: institutional theory explains convergence through isomorphic pressures; institutional logics explains heterogeneity through competing logics and hybridization.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Institutional logics is useful because it explains conflict and variation. It helps you talk about digital change as a political and cultural shift, not only a technical implementation. It is also a strong lens for studying power and legitimacy in platform and public sector settings. Weaknesses: The theory can become vague if logics are not clearly specified and measured. It also requires rich data and careful interpretation, which can be challenging in purely survey-based studies. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is treating logics as just another set of independent variables. Logics are organizing principles, not simple perceptions. Another misuse is mixing institutional logics and institutional theory without clarifying whether you are explaining convergence or conflict. Alternatives and competitors: Stakeholder theory and sensemaking can explain value conflict at different levels. Institutional theory is stronger when the main pattern is convergence and diffusion. Sociomateriality is stronger when the main focus is on practices and entanglement rather than competing organizing principles.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use institutional logics when the question is about conflicts between value systems (professional vs market vs state) shaping digital change in the same field. Example research question: "In healthcare digitalization, how do market logic and professional logic shape whether AI decision support is used as an efficiency tool or as a quality-of-care tool?" Example hypothesis (more like an exam proposition): "When market logic dominates, AI tools will be justified and designed around efficiency and cost, while when professional logic dominates, AI tools will be justified and designed around expertise, standards, and quality of care." What I would measure (simple): In an empirical study, I would operationalize logics through discourse and governance signals (policy language, KPI focus, training standards, leadership messaging) and connect them to design choices and use patterns. A mixed-method design works well here because logics are cultural and institutional, not only individual beliefs. Seminal APA citation: Thornton, P. H., Ocasio, W., & Lounsbury, M. (2012). The institutional logics perspective: A new approach to culture, structure, and process. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press.

Exam tip 1: Relationship to Institutional Theory. Institutional logics is a key evolution beyond the institutional theory of DiMaggio & Powell (1983). While DiMaggio and Powell explain isomorphic convergence through coercive, mimetic, and normative pressures, institutional logics (Thornton & Ocasio, 1999, 2008) explains why organizations diverge: they navigate competing institutional logics, each with its own norms and values. In comps answers, use institutional theory for convergence patterns and institutional logics for value conflict and heterogeneity.
Exam tip 2: Key authors and seminal papers. The foundational paper is Thornton, P. H., & Ocasio, W. (1999). Institutional logics and the historical contingency of power in organizations. American Journal of Sociology, 105(3), 801-843. The expanded synthesis is Thornton, Ocasio, & Lounsbury (2012). When exam questions ask how competing organizational values shape technology adoption, cite Thornton and Ocasio's concept of competing logics and their integration with power dynamics.
Exam tip 3: Boundary conditions and exam strategy. Institutional logics is strongest in settings with genuine value conflict (healthcare professionals vs hospital administrators, platform participants vs platform operators, public sector efficiency vs citizens' rights). It is weaker in homogeneous organizations where one logic dominates. For your exam: identify whether the context has multiple stakeholder groups with different logics; if not, institutional theory or stakeholder theory may be cleaner. Also note that logics must be empirically demonstrated through discourse, governance signals, and practices - not just asserted.
Organizational Pfeffer, 1985

Relational Demography Theory

How demographic similarity between individuals shapes trust, information sharing, and collaboration.

Core Concept: People are more likely to trust, communicate with, and collaborate with others similar to them in demographic attributes (age, gender, race, background). This affects information security and insider threat dynamics.

🔑 Demographic Similarity: Individuals favor and trust demographically similar others
📊 Trust and Disclosure: Higher demographic similarity increases information sharing and reduces skepticism
🔄 Insider Threat Dynamics: Similar-demographic colleagues may have lower suspicion thresholds
⚡ IS Security: Affects social engineering success and insider threat vulnerability
IS Relevance: Explains why social engineering exploits demographic trust; important for understanding how homogeneous teams may increase insider threat risk through demographic-based trust.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Tsui and O'Reilly (1989), relational demography in superior-subordinate dyads. Key contribution: similarity/dissimilarity between people shapes interaction quality and outcomes.
Discipline of origin Organizational behavior / social psychology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS for trust, teams, and human-robot collaboration).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains how demographic similarity affects trust, communication, and collaboration.
Core constructs Relational demography (similarity/dissimilarity), ascribed vs achieved attributes, social categorization, trust, interaction quality.
Mechanism (plain) People tend to trust and coordinate more easily with similar others, partly due to shared expectations and social categorization. Dissimilarity can create distance and reduce trust, depending on context.
Boundary conditions Effects depend on salience of attributes, diversity climate, task interdependence, and whether the "other" is human or technology (robots change attribution).
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Relational demography is applied in recent MISQ work to explain trust and preference in working with robots versus humans.
📚 Foundational Origins

Relational demography theory begins with a simple observation: similarity and difference between people shape how they relate to each other. Tsui and O'Reilly (1989) develop relational demography in superior-subordinate dyads and show that demographic similarity and dissimilarity can influence interaction quality and outcomes. The deeper idea is social categorization: people use visible cues to categorize others, which shapes trust, communication, and perceived fit.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that similarity tends to reduce friction. When people perceive similarity, they often expect shared norms and easier coordination, which can increase trust and cooperation. Dissimilarity can create distance and misinterpretation, which can reduce trust, especially when tasks require close coordination. The mechanism is categorization and expectation. Similarity influences how quickly people form positive expectations and how they interpret ambiguous behavior. In modern IS settings, this mechanism becomes interesting when the "other" is not a human. Robots and AI systems can trigger categorization processes too, but the cues and attributions can be different. Boundary conditions include salience and climate. Similarity effects are stronger when the attribute is salient and when the environment makes difference meaningful (for example, low inclusion climate). In diverse and inclusive contexts, dissimilarity can be less harmful or even beneficial.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, relational demography is increasingly relevant for human-AI and human-robot collaboration because digital coworkers create new similarity questions. People may feel similarity or distance based on personality cues, communication style, voice, or appearance, not only on human demographics. You and Robert (2024) is a strong recent MISQ anchor because it directly applies relational demography theory to preference for robotic over human coworkers. It shows how trust and willingness to work together can depend on perceived similarity, and it provides a clean exam story for why social categorization matters in technology-mediated work. Integration with trust theory is natural. Relational demography can explain why trust forms more easily with some targets than others, while trust theory explains how that trust translates into vulnerability and reliance.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Relational demography gives a simple social mechanism for collaboration and trust. It is especially useful in IS now because digital coworkers make similarity cues designable, which creates interesting research questions about how design shapes coordination. Weaknesses: Similarity is not always good. Homophily can reduce learning and diversity of ideas, and similarity effects can reflect bias. Another limitation is that demographic attributes may be less meaningful in virtual work unless they are made salient. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is treating similarity as a control variable instead of a mechanism. Another is using broad demographic categories without measuring whether those categories are actually salient in the interaction. Alternatives and competitors: Social identity theory can be stronger when group belonging is the main mechanism. Trust theory can be stronger when similarity is not the main driver and competence signals dominate. Institutional perspectives can be stronger when collaboration norms are shaped by professional standards rather than dyad similarity.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use relational demography when the question is about similarity and difference between people shaping trust, collaboration, or preferences (including human vs robot coworkers). Example research question: "Do people prefer to work with robots that communicate in a similar style to them, and does that similarity increase trust and reliance?" Example hypothesis: "Greater perceived similarity between a worker and a robot coworker is positively associated with trust in the robot and willingness to collaborate." What I would measure (simple): I would measure perceived similarity (communication style, values, personality cues) and trust using validated scales. Behavioral outcomes can include willingness to accept robot recommendations, delegation decisions, and performance in team tasks. A strong design is an experiment where the robot's communication style is manipulated. Seminal APA citation: Tsui, A. S., & O'Reilly, C. A. (1989). Beyond simple demographic effects: The importance of relational demography in superior-subordinate dyads. Academy of Management Journal, 32(2), 402-423.

Exam tip 1: Relationship to Social Identity Theory. Relational demography is about dyadic similarity and trust, while Social Identity Theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1979) is about group belonging and in-group bias. They are complementary: relational demography explains why people trust similar individuals, while social identity explains why people favor their in-group over out-groups. In an exam answer, use relational demography when discussing dyadic preferences (human-human, human-robot) and social identity theory when discussing group-level conflict or competitive dynamics.
Exam tip 2: Key authors and seminal papers. The anchor is Tsui, A. S., & O'Reilly, C. A. (1989). Beyond simple demographic effects: The importance of relational demography in superior-subordinate dyads. Academy of Management Journal, 32(2), 402-423. Tsui et al. (1992) extend this in Journal of Applied Psychology showing that demographic similarity affects turnover intentions. For IS security contexts, use relational demography to explain homophily effects in insider threat teams: demographically similar colleagues may be more susceptible to social engineering and may trust similar-demographic colleagues even when competence signals are missing.
Exam tip 3: Boundary conditions in security contexts. Relational demography effects are strongest when similarity dimensions are salient and when diversity climate is low. In inclusive organizations or when tasks are expertise-driven, demographic similarity becomes less influential. Important boundary condition for security: in diverse security teams, demographic homophily can create blind spots (everyone similar to each other may miss threats an outsider would catch). For exam strategy, note that relational demography is weaker when the "other" is a system (algorithm, AI) rather than a human, unless designers explicitly incorporate demographic cues into the technology.
Psychology Hatfield et al., 1992

Emotional Contagion Theory

How emotions spread through groups and affect collective behavior, risk perception, and security response.

Core Concept: Emotions spread automatically through observation and mimicry. Fear of breaches, distrust of management, or urgency of security incidents spread through teams and affect collective compliance behavior.

🔑 Emotional Spread: Emotions transmit through groups via observation and unconscious mimicry
📊 Collective Effects: Group-level emotions influence coordination, risk perception, and cohesion
🔄 Context Amplification: Crises amplify emotional contagion and can override individual judgment
⚡ IS Application: Security incidents create fear cascades; culture of paranoia reduces effectiveness
IS Relevance: Explains how security breaches create organizational panic and compliance spikes; shows that managing emotions collectively improves sustained compliance more than repeated incidents.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Hatfield, Cacioppo, and Rapson (1994), Emotional Contagion. Key contribution: emotions can spread between people through automatic mimicry and social feedback.
Discipline of origin Social psychology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used in IS to explain emotional dynamics on social media and online communities).
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains how emotions propagate through social interaction and shared attention.
Core constructs Emotional contagion, mimicry, synchrony, feedback, shared affect, exposure to emotional signals.
Mechanism (plain) People unconsciously mimic others' expressions and emotional signals, and that mimicry feeds back into their own feelings. In digital settings, text, emojis, and visible reactions can play a similar role.
Boundary conditions Stronger with repeated exposure, strong ties, and high attention. Online, contagion depends on visibility and interpretability of signals (tone, emojis, reactions).
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Emotional contagion mechanisms appear in MISQ work on unintended emotional effects in online health communities and shared emotional reactions to breach events on social media.
📚 Foundational Origins

Emotional contagion theory is about how emotions spread. Hatfield, Cacioppo, and Rapson (1994) argue that people can "catch" emotions through automatic mimicry and social feedback. In face-to-face settings, this happens through expressions, posture, tone, and synchrony. In digital settings, the signals change (text tone, emojis, reactions), but the basic logic is similar: repeated exposure to emotional signals can shift your affect.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that emotions can transmit through interaction. People subconsciously mimic emotional expressions and that mimicry feeds back into their own feelings. In online contexts, mimicry can be less direct, but social feedback and shared attention can still create emotional waves. The mechanism in digital settings is exposure plus interpretation. Exposure to emotional content raises the accessibility of that emotion, and social signals (likes, comments, supportive replies) make the emotion socially reinforced. This is why contagious effects are often strongest in high-attention events with repeated exposure. Boundary conditions include visibility and tie strength. Contagion is stronger when the emotional signal is clear, when exposure is repeated, and when relationships are strong. Online, contagion also depends on whether the platform makes emotional signals visible and interpretable.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS research uses emotional contagion to explain emotional dynamics on social media and in online communities. The advantage in IS is that many emotional signals are observable in digital traces (text sentiment, reaction patterns), which makes it possible to study contagion at scale. Zhou et al. (2023) is a useful recent MISQ anchor because it studies unintended emotional effects in online health communities. Bachura et al. (2022) is another strong anchor in a very different context: a data breach event and shared emotional reactions on Twitter. Together, these show how emotional contagion can matter in both support communities and crisis events. Emotional contagion also integrates naturally with social comparison. Contagion explains spread, while comparison explains self-evaluation and why a person might interpret an emotional wave as threatening or motivating.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Emotional contagion gives a clear mechanism for why emotions move through networks and why platform design matters for collective mood. It fits modern IS contexts where emotion is visible and amplified through algorithms and engagement features. Weaknesses: In online settings, it can be hard to separate contagion from homophily (similar people connect) and from common exposure (everyone sees the same news). This creates identification challenges. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is calling any correlated emotion "contagion" without testing a diffusion mechanism. Another is ignoring platform algorithms, which can create apparent contagion by co-exposure. Alternatives and competitors: Social comparison theory is a strong alternative when the mechanism is self-evaluation. Appraisal theories are useful when the key mechanism is interpretation of events. Sociomateriality can be useful when the main focus is on practices and platform mechanics rather than emotional transmission alone.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use emotional contagion when the question is about emotions spreading through digital networks and shaping collective reactions or engagement. Example research question: "After a public data breach, how do negative emotions spread on social media, and what platform features amplify or dampen that spread?" Example hypothesis: "Higher exposure to negative-emotion posts is associated with higher likelihood of posting negative-emotion content, consistent with contagion dynamics." What I would measure (simple): I would use social media data to measure exposure (feed position, network neighbor posts) and emotional expression (sentiment and emotion classifiers). I would include time and network structure and try to separate contagion from homophily using designs like time-lagged exposure models or natural experiments. Seminal APA citation: Hatfield, E., Cacioppo, J. T., & Rapson, R. L. (1994). Emotional contagion. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

Exam tip 1: Relationship to Social Learning Theory. Emotional contagion (Hatfield et al., 1994) is about automatic spread of affect through mimicry and synchrony. Social Learning Theory (Bandura, 1977) is about learning through observation of models and consequences. They interact in practice: fear of security breaches can spread through emotional contagion (everyone sees others' distress and adopts it), and that same fear can reinforce careful security practices through social learning. In exam answers, use contagion to explain rapid emotional waves and social learning to explain sustained behavioral change.
Exam tip 2: Key authors and seminal papers, especially for fear appeals in security. Hatfield, E., Cacioppo, J. T., & Rapson, R. L. (1994) is the foundational book on contagion mechanisms. For IS security, Liang & Xue (2009) on avoidance behavior and fear appeals is relevant. A key insight: fear appeals spread through contagion, but excessive fear can backfire (people become paralyzed or dismissive). When designing security campaigns, understand that fear will spread - the exam-relevant question is whether the organization has clear coping strategies to channel that fear productively.
Exam tip 3: Boundary conditions and common misapplications. Emotional contagion is strongest in high-attention crises with repeated exposure and strong social ties. It is harder to detect in online settings where you cannot easily separate contagion from homophily (similar people connect) or common external shocks (everyone got the same breach notification email). For exam strategy, when claiming emotional contagion, show that exposure precedes emotional response and that network structure matters (did emotion spread through network paths or was it common exposure?). Also identify boundary conditions: contagion weakens with weak ties, asynchronous communication, and low-salience emotions.
Organizational Giddens, 1984 Core for comps

Structuration Theory

How structures (rules, systems) and human action mutually constitute each other in organizational systems.

Core Concept: Structures do not simply constrain action; people construct and reconstruct structures through action. IT security policies are both constraining and enabling, and their meaning changes through practice.

🔑 Duality of Structure: Structures enable and constrain action simultaneously
📊 Recursive Relationship: Structures shape actions, but actions reproduce or transform structures
🔄 Situated Interpretation: Rules and systems are interpreted and reinterpreted through use
⚡ IS Implementation: Security policies succeed only when embedded in organizational practice
IS Relevance: Explains why IS implementations sometimes fail despite good design; shows that security success requires ongoing co-production of rules and practices, not one-time deployment.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Giddens (1984), The constitution of society. Key contribution: structure and agency are a duality, not opposites. Structures shape action, but action reproduces and changes structures.
Key IS adaptations DeSanctis and Poole (1994) develop Adaptive Structuration Theory (AST) for group technologies; Orlikowski (1992) applies structuration ideas to technology in organizations.
Discipline of origin Sociology.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation): explains how social structures and human action recursively shape each other over time.
Core constructs Structure, agency, duality of structure, rules and resources, enactment, routinization, institutionalization.
Mechanism (plain) People act using rules and resources (structures). By acting, they reinforce or change those structures. Technology is part of the resources and routines through which this happens.
Boundary conditions Best for process and practice questions about how technology and organization co-evolve. Harder for short cross-sectional prediction questions because the mechanism is recursive and time-based.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Structuration is often present as a foundational logic inside practice-based IS theorizing (including sociomateriality and technology-in-practice work), but it is less common today as an explicit headline theory in top outlets. (Logged as partial coverage for strict 2020-2025 main-lens examples.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Structuration theory started from a classic social science tension: are people shaped by social structure, or do people shape structure? Giddens argues the two are inseparable. Structures are the rules and resources that make action possible, but they also constrain it. At the same time, structures only exist because people keep enacting them in practice.

💎 Theoretical Core

The central proposition is recursion: structure -> action -> structure. In organizations, this means routines, norms, and authority structures are not fixed objects. They are continuously produced through everyday work. In IS, the exam move is to show how technology enters this loop. Technology is not only a tool that "impacts" structure. It is part of the resources and routines that people draw on. When people adopt or adapt technology, they can reproduce existing structures (reinforcing control) or transform them (shifting coordination and power). Boundary conditions are important. Structuration is strongest when you can trace sequences of enactment over time (process data). It is weaker for one-shot surveys where you only observe static perceptions.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

DeSanctis and Poole (1994) adapt structuration to group decision support and other advanced information technologies. Their key move is to translate abstract ideas (rules/resources) into technology features and "spirit" (the intended values embedded in the technology). Users appropriate structures in use, sometimes faithfully, sometimes ironically. Orlikowski (1992) uses structuration ideas to argue that technology is both shaped by human action and shapes action. This work is also part of the path that later leads to sociomateriality debates. Integration with sociomateriality is common. A clean way to frame it is: structuration gives you the structure-agency recursion, while sociomateriality pushes further to treat social and material as inseparable in practice.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Structuration gives a strong process mechanism. It helps you avoid naive determinism ("technology causes change") and naive voluntarism ("people choose whatever"). It supports nuanced exam answers about digital transformation. Weaknesses: It can be too abstract. Without careful operationalization, structuration talk can become vague. It also makes prediction difficult because the mechanism is recursive and path-dependent. Common misapplications in IS: Using structuration language as decoration without tracing enactment, or treating structures as static variables in a regression model while claiming structuration. Alternatives and competitors: Sociomateriality for stronger entanglement claims, institutional theory for field-level pressures, and affordance theory for goal-oriented action possibilities.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use structuration when the exam question is about how technology and organization shape each other over time (routines, power, transformation). Example research question: "How does introducing AI decision support reshape decision authority and routines in a professional service firm over time?" Example hypothesis: Structuration is often tested with process claims rather than classic hypotheses. A good exam claim is: "AI decision support will initially be appropriated to reinforce existing authority structures, but over time it can shift routines and redistribute decision rights as practices stabilize." What I would measure (simple): Longitudinal qualitative data (interviews, observation), traces of technology use (override behavior, workflow changes), and documented rule changes (policies, roles). The key is to show enactment and reproduction or change of structures. Seminal APA citation: Giddens, A. (1984). The constitution of society. Berkeley, CA: University of California Press. # Part F. Meta and philosophical frameworks

Exam tip 1: Giddens vs Orlikowski vs DeSanctis & Poole. Giddens (1984) developed the foundational structure-agency duality. Orlikowski (1992) adapted it for technology use and introduced the concept of "structures in use" and "improvisational enactment." DeSanctis & Poole (1994) developed Adaptive Structuration Theory (AST), which adds the concept of system "spirit" (intended design values) and "faithful vs ironic appropriation" of technology. In exam answers, use Giddens for the abstract recursion, Orlikowski for technology-in-practice, and DeSanctis & Poole when discussing how users adapt group technologies in ways both faithful and divergent from design intent.
Exam tip 2: The duality of structure in IS security contexts. Security policies exemplify duality: they constrain action (you must authenticate), but they also enable action (authentication tools provide confidence). The critical insight is that the meaning of the policy is negotiated in practice. A policy that says "report all suspicious behavior" may be enacted as "report after confirming, to avoid false alarms" or "report everything, which overwhelms the team." Both are enactments of the same rule, and both reproduce or change the underlying authority structure. For security breach response, structuration explains why response procedures are not simply followed; they are adapted, reinterpreted, and sometimes circumvented, which shifts decision authority and accountability over time.
Exam tip 3: Boundary conditions and exam strategy for process claims. Structuration is a process theory, not a variance theory. It requires longitudinal data showing sequences of enactment, adaptation, and structural change. It is weaker for predicting single cross-sectional outcomes and stronger for explaining how technology co-evolves with organization over time. For exam boundary conditions: structuration works best when you can trace repeated cycles of action and reproduction (or transformation), and weakest in one-shot surveys. Also note: structuration avoids both technological determinism ("technology causes change") and naive voluntarism ("people do whatever they want"). It is the nuanced middle ground - valuable for exam answers on digital transformation and implementation failure.
Meta-Framework Gregor, 2006

Gregor's Five Theory Types

Taxonomy for evaluating and categorizing different types of IS and social theories.

Core Concept: Gregor distinguishes five theory types: I (description), II (explanation), III (prediction), IV (explanation and prediction), V (design/technology). Knowing a theory's type clarifies what it claims and how to test it.

🔑 Type I Description: Defines and categorizes phenomena without explaining causes
📊 Type II Explanation: Explains why phenomena occur without predicting outcomes
🔄 Type IV Prediction: Explains and predicts outcomes under different conditions
⚡ Meta-Value: Clarifies what each theory can and cannot answer in research design
IS Relevance: Essential framework for interpreting IS theories; prevents misapplication of descriptive theories to predictive questions and vice versa.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Gregor (2006), "The nature of theory in Information Systems," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: a widely used typology of theory types in IS (Type I to Type V).
Discipline of origin IS (meta-theory about what "theory" means in IS).
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type This is a typology, not a theory of a phenomenon. It is best treated as a meta-theoretical framework for positioning contributions.
Core constructs Type I (analysis), Type II (explanation), Type III (prediction), Type IV (explanation and prediction), Type V (design and action).
Mechanism (plain) The "type" depends on the goal of knowledge: describing, explaining, predicting, or designing. Different research designs and contributions fit different types.
Boundary conditions Useful for positioning and evaluating theory contributions in IS. Not a substitute for a substantive theory of a specific phenomenon.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Still widely cited in top journals when authors position contributions or discuss what counts as theorizing in IS.
📚 Foundational Origins

Gregor (2006) wrote this paper because IS scholars often use the word "theory" in inconsistent ways. Some papers call a framework a theory. Some call a list of variables a theory. Some call a design method a theory. That creates confusion in comps answers and in journal reviewing because people argue about "theory" without agreeing on what they mean. Gregor's contribution is a typology that makes the differences explicit. It is also an IS-native move. Gregor is not importing a typology from sociology or psychology. She is writing directly for the IS community and trying to strengthen how we build and evaluate knowledge in the field.

💎 Theoretical Core

Gregor's core idea is that "theory" is not one thing. The type depends on the goal of the knowledge. Type I (analysis) focuses on describing and classifying. Type II (explanation) focuses on why something happens. Type III (prediction) focuses on what will happen without necessarily explaining why. Type IV combines explanation and prediction. Type V (design and action) focuses on how to build and evaluate an artifact or method that solves a problem. BCIS 6670's session 3 StudyGuide adds a very exam-useful detail from Gregor (2006) Table 3: theory has components, and different types include different components. The StudyGuide lists seven components: (1) means of representation, (2) primary constructs, (3) statements of relationship, (4) scope, (5) causal explanations, (6) testable propositions, and (7) prescriptive statements. The key point is that Type I is mainly 1-4, Type II adds 5, Type III adds 6 (often without 5), Type IV includes 1-6, and Type V can include all 7. This is a clean way to defend why a paper is "not predictive" but still theoretical, and it helps you see what is missing when a paper claims a type but does not deliver its components. The same StudyGuide also makes the variance vs process distinction explicit. Variance theories ask how much variance in Y is explained by X and are often tested cross-sectionally. Process theories ask about sequences and unfolding, and they usually need longitudinal evidence. A common comps trap is calling something "process" while testing only variance. This connects directly to Markus and Robey (1988) and is a strong reviewer-like critique move. The mechanism here is not causal in the usual sense because this is a meta framework. The mechanism is about evaluation logic. Different types imply different standards. For example, a Type V contribution should be evaluated by usefulness and rigor of design and evaluation, not by the same criteria as a Type IV variance model. Boundary conditions are important. Gregor's typology helps you position contributions, but it does not replace substantive theory. Calling something "Type IV" does not make it explanatory by itself. You still need constructs, mechanisms, and boundaries.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

Gregor's typology has become a standard reference in IS writing. You see it in conceptual papers, editorials, and design science work where authors want to justify that design knowledge can be theory (Type V). It also shows up when authors position why their paper is not trying to predict but is trying to explain (Type II). In the 2020s, it remains relevant as IS scholars debate what next-generation theorizing should look like. Burton-Jones et al. (2021) call for stronger theorizing and for moving beyond narrow templates, and Gregor's language helps because it reminds us that strong work can take different forms. Gregor also pairs naturally with Whetten (1989). Gregor helps you state what kind of theory you are offering. Whetten helps you check whether your theory is complete (what, how, why, boundaries), regardless of type. In comps answers, using both sounds practical and reviewer-aware, not robotic.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: The typology is simple and very useful. It helps you avoid vague statements like "this paper contributes to theory" and instead say what kind of theory and what standards apply. It also helps you explain why design science and conceptual work are legitimate forms of theorizing in IS. Weaknesses: Because it is widely used, it can become a ritual citation. Some papers label a type but do not actually deliver what that type requires (for example, claiming Type IV but providing no mechanism). Another limitation is that real papers can mix types, so the classification can be debated. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is treating the typology as a box-checking exercise. Another is using "Type V" to label any practical guideline, even when there is no design artifact and no evaluation. Alternatives and competitors: Whetten's framework is the closest complement because it focuses on completeness. Other "what theory is" pieces exist in organization studies, but Gregor is the clearest IS-native typology in common use.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use Gregor when the exam question is about positioning your contribution (analysis, explanation, prediction, design, or action) and you need clear language for what your theory does. Example research question: "What type of theory contribution is this paper making, and what should we evaluate it on?" Example hypothesis (Type IV example): "Perceived trust is positively associated with private information disclosure." The point is not the specific hypothesis. The point is that if you claim Type IV, you need both a why story (mechanism) and a testable prediction. What I would measure (simple): If I am positioning a paper, I would identify the constructs and relationships (Whetten's what/how), the mechanism (why), and the boundaries (who/where/when). Then I would say whether the paper is mainly describing (Type I), explaining (Type II), predicting (Type III), explaining and predicting (Type IV), or designing an artifact (Type V). Seminal APA citation: Gregor, S. (2006). The nature of theory in Information Systems. MIS Quarterly, 30(3), 611-642.

Exam tip 1: Gregor (2006) and Weber (2012) for theory positioning. Gregor's typology is the standard IS framework for classifying theory types. Weber (2012) "Still Desperately Seeking the IT Artifact" complements Gregor by critiquing how IS often conflates borrowed theories with IS-native theories, and by emphasizing that we should measure how theory actually contributes to IS knowledge, not just borrow frameworks wholesale. In comps answers, use Gregor to position your theory type, then use Weber to argue why that type matters for IS (e.g., "this paper is Type II explanation, which contributes to IS by showing why technology adoption diverges across firms").
Exam tip 2: How to identify theory type in papers and exam questions. Type I papers focus on defining and categorizing phenomena (e.g., "here are the dimensions of digital culture"). Type II papers focus on explanation without prediction (e.g., "institutional logics explain why firms adopt AI differently"). Type III papers focus on prediction without explaining why (rare in IS, but examples exist). Type IV papers do both - they explain and predict (e.g., "trust positively predicts disclosure because of X mechanism"). Type V papers offer design artifacts and prescriptions (e.g., design science methods, frameworks for building secure systems). For exam questions, first identify: is the question asking you to explain something or predict something? Then position your theory appropriately. Avoid mixing types; a Type II theory is not "weak" just because it does not predict - it is just a different kind of contribution.
Exam tip 3: Exam strategy for theory classification and boundary conditions. When exam questions ask "what theory type is this?" or "what should you use for this research question?", use Gregor's framework plus these practical heuristics: Use Type II (explanation) for "why" questions about organizational behavior. Use Type IV (explanation and prediction) when you have a testable mechanism and want to predict. Use Type V for design problems. Always specify boundary conditions: explain where your theory applies and where it breaks down. Also note that many real papers mix types: they might be primarily Type IV but include Type I classification contributions, or primarily Type II but include some predictive propositions. Strong exam answers recognize this complexity instead of over-simplifying.
Meta-Framework Whetten, 1989

Whetten's Theory Building Elements

Components necessary for strong theory: what, how, why, and boundary conditions.

Core Concept: Strong theories specify WHAT variables matter, HOW they relate (mechanisms), WHY they relate (underlying logic), and WHERE the theory applies (boundary conditions). Weak theories omit one or more elements.

🔑 WHAT Variables: Clear specification of constructs and their definitions
📊 HOW Mechanisms: Explicit description of relationships and pathways
🔄 WHY Logic: Theoretical reasoning that makes the theory comprehensible and defensible
⚡ Boundary Conditions: Specification of when theory applies and when it fails
IS Relevance: Practical framework for evaluating and building IS theories; guides what to include in theory development for exam and research questions.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Whetten (1989), "What constitutes a theoretical contribution?," Academy of Management Review. Key contribution: a complete theory answers What (constructs), How (relationships), Why (logic), and Who/Where/When (boundaries).
Discipline of origin Management/organization research (theory building guidance).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed (used widely in IS to evaluate theory sections and contributions).
Gregor (2006) type Meta-theoretical guidance, not a substantive theory type. It is best treated as a completeness checklist for any theory.
Core constructs What, How, Why, Who/Where/When; theoretical contribution; boundary conditions.
Mechanism (plain) Whetten's point is that a diagram is not a theory unless it includes the "why" logic and clear boundaries.
Boundary conditions Applies to building and evaluating theory in most social science fields, including IS. Not a causal theory of a specific phenomenon.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" Recent IS editorials and reflection papers still use Whetten-style logic when discussing what counts as a strong theoretical contribution.
📚 Foundational Origins

Whetten (1989) wrote this piece to answer a question reviewers and editors always ask: what counts as a real theoretical contribution? His answer is simple but strict. A theory is more than a list of variables or a diagram. A complete theory should answer four things: What (the key constructs), How (the relationships among them), Why (the logic and mechanism), and Who/Where/When (the boundaries). This paper is not IS-native, but it has become part of IS culture because it matches what top journals expect in theory sections. If your dissertation or comps answer is missing the "why" or the boundaries, Whetten gives you a clear way to diagnose the problem.

💎 Theoretical Core

The core proposition is completeness. Constructs without relationships are not theory. Relationships without logic are not theory. Logic without boundaries is not convincing theory. Whetten is basically saying: you need a coherent package that tells a reader what matters, how it connects, why it should be true, and where it should or should not hold. The mechanism here is rhetorical and scientific at the same time. A strong "why" story helps readers believe the model is not a coincidence. Boundaries help readers understand what the model is not claiming, which makes the claims more falsifiable and easier to test. Boundary conditions are especially exam-friendly. When you add "who/where/when," you show you can think like a reviewer. You are not only trying to win significance. You are showing conceptual discipline. BCIS 6670's session 3 StudyGuide also lists Whetten's 7 key reviewer questions. These are easy to use in a comps answer because they sound like actual editor/reviewer logic, not textbook talk: "What's new?", "So what?", "Why so?", "Well done?", "Done well?", "Why now?", and "Who cares?". If I can answer these cleanly in 1 or 2 sentences each, my theory story is usually in good shape. The same StudyGuide also distinguishes incremental versus radical contributions. Incremental contributions extend or refine existing theory. Radical contributions challenge foundational assumptions. This is a useful link to Sun et al. (2025) because it helps you argue that novelty is not only "new constructs," it can be a deeper reframing of assumptions.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, Whetten is used as a writing and reviewing tool. You often see it in how authors justify the completeness of a theoretical argument, and you see it indirectly in reviewer comments ("what is the mechanism?" "what is the boundary condition?"). Gopal et al. (2024) is a recent top-outlet editorial reflection in Information Systems Research that echoes the same message: strong empirical papers need clear theoretical logic, not only interesting data. That is basically Whetten's point in modern form. Whetten also complements Gregor (2006). Gregor helps you state the type of theory you are aiming for. Whetten helps you make it complete for that type. That combination is a strong comps move because it sounds like you understand both theory and how theory is judged in our field.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: Whetten is practical. It gives you a clean checklist to improve theory writing and to diagnose weak theory sections. It also pushes you to include boundaries, which is often what separates a strong comps answer from a generic summary. Weaknesses: If used too mechanically, it can push people toward variance models and away from process theorizing. For example, a process paper may not fit cleanly into "X causes Y" even though it still has a strong why story and boundaries. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is writing a long "what" section (many constructs) and a long "how" section (many arrows) but a weak "why" section. Another is ignoring boundaries because the author wants the model to feel universal. Alternatives and competitors: Gregor's typology is complementary, not a competitor. Process theorizing guidance is an alternative when the phenomenon is unfolding and dynamic, but even then, Whetten's "why" and boundaries are still useful.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use Whetten when you need to critique or build a theory and show you can cover what, how, why, and boundary conditions clearly. Example research question: "Is my theory section complete in terms of what, how, why, and boundaries?" Example hypothesis: "Explainability of an AI system increases user trust, which increases willingness to rely on the system." The key point is that a good answer would also explain why (mechanism) and where it holds (boundary conditions). What I would measure (simple): I would define the constructs clearly (what), specify the relationships (how), explain the causal logic (why), and state the boundaries (who/where/when). For example, I might argue the effect is stronger in high-stakes decisions and weaker when use is mandatory. Seminal APA citation: Whetten, D. A. (1989). What constitutes a theoretical contribution? Academy of Management Review, 14(4), 490-495.

💡 Exam-Tips
Connection to Gregor (2006): Whetten's framework complements Gregor's type system perfectly. While Gregor helps you identify whether you are building a Type I (descriptive), Type II (explanatory), or Type III (predictive) theory, Whetten ensures that whatever type you choose is complete and rigorous. On exams, reference both together: "My theory fits Gregor's Type II because it explains the mechanism, and it satisfies Whetten's criteria because it specifies what variables matter (trust, monitoring), how they relate (inverse), why (dignity signals), and where it holds (surveillance contexts)."
Evaluating Theory Completeness: Use Whetten as a diagnostic checklist when you critique papers or build theories. Ask: Does the paper clearly specify WHAT constructs? Does it show HOW they relate mechanistically? Does it explain WHY the relationship exists theoretically? Does it state WHO/WHERE/WHEN boundaries? If any element is missing, you can pinpoint the weakness in your comps answer. This makes your critique sound like a real reviewer, not a student summary.
Boundary Conditions as Exam Strategy: Adding explicit boundary conditions is one of the easiest ways to strengthen a comps answer. For example, instead of claiming "monitoring reduces compliance," add: "Monitoring reduces voluntary compliance when employees perceive the monitor as distrusting, but may increase enforced compliance in high-stakes compliance environments." This shows Whetten thinking and maps naturally to moderators or context-dependent theory, which examiners respect.
Philosophy Popper, 1963

Correspondence Theory (Process View)

Theory as representation of underlying processes; truth as correspondence between claims and reality.

Core Concept: Theories are statements about how processes work. A theory is true if its claims correspond to actual underlying mechanisms. This view emphasizes identifying real causal processes, not just predictive accuracy.

🔑 Representation View: Theories represent actual underlying processes and mechanisms
📊 Causality Focus: Emphasis on discovering true causal processes, not patterns
🔄 Mechanism Specificity: Good theories identify specific mechanisms, not just associations
⚡ IS Application: Drives search for deep mechanisms in security behavior, not surface correlations
IS Relevance: Philosophical framework justifying mechanism-focused IS theories; explains why understanding "how" and "why" matters more than just predicting "what."
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Ingold (2015), process-oriented view of "correspondence" and lines/flows. Key contribution: shifts focus from static entities to ongoing processes and relations.
Key IS adaptation Mousavi Baygi, Introna, and Hultin (2021), "Everything Flows: Studying Continuous Socio-Technological Transformation in a Fluid and Dynamic Digital World," MIS Quarterly.
Discipline of origin Anthropology / philosophy of practice (process thinking).
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed foundations, applied in IS for continuous transformation.
Gregor (2006) type Type II (explanation) with strong ontological flavor (process ontology).
Core constructs Process, flow, becoming, correspondence, kairos (timing), attentionality, undergoing.
Mechanism (plain) Digital transformation is not a sequence of discrete projects. It is continuous becoming. Researchers should study rhythms, timing, attention, and lived experience of change as it unfolds.
Boundary conditions Best for continuous, fluid phenomena where "before/after" is misleading. Hard to convert into cross-sectional hypotheses. Often requires process methods and rich data.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" MISQ process research explicitly uses this lens to study continuous sociotechnological transformation.
📚 Foundational Origins

Correspondence here is not the correspondence theory of truth. It is a process view that comes from anthropology and practice-oriented philosophy. Ingold (2015) argues that we should not treat the world as made of fixed objects with stable properties. We should treat it as made of lines, relations, and ongoing movement. In that view, life is not "being," it is "becoming." This matters for IS because many digital phenomena are not stable. Platforms update continuously, practices evolve, and transformation is ongoing. A process view gives you language to study that without forcing everything into before-and-after snapshots.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that reality is processual. Instead of focusing on static entities, you focus on flow, timing, and relational becoming. This is why key constructs in this lens include kairos (meaningful timing), attentionality (what people attend to), and undergoing (how people experience change as it happens). The mechanism is not "X causes Y" in a simple way. It is about how patterns emerge through ongoing action. Change happens through rhythms, interruptions, and re-alignments. In IS terms, transformation is continuous sociotechnological movement, not a sequence of discrete projects. Boundary conditions are practical. This lens fits continuous, fluid phenomena, but it is hard to translate into cross-sectional hypotheses. It often requires process methods, longitudinal data, and sensitivity to timing and context.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

IS scholars have long studied processes (implementation, change management, technology-in-practice). What is newer in Mousavi Baygi et al. (2021) is the explicit push to treat the digital world as fluid and to adjust our theorizing and methods accordingly. Their argument is that stage models and discrete event logic often miss what transformation looks like in a digital era where change never stops. This lens also integrates naturally with sociomateriality and complexity. Sociomateriality emphasizes entangled practices. Correspondence emphasizes flow and becoming. Complexity emphasizes emergence and coevolution. In comps answers, a good move is to show how these align but also how they differ. For example, sociomateriality is a strong claim about inseparability, while correspondence is a strong claim about process and flow.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: The process view is useful because it matches many modern digital settings. It helps you avoid forcing continuous transformation into artificial stages. It also fits interpretive and qualitative work well because it centers lived experience and timing. Weaknesses: It can be hard to make testable predictions, and some reviewers expect variance models. The language can also feel abstract if the paper does not connect concepts like kairos and attentionality to real data and observable patterns. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is using process words ("fluid", "flow") but still studying the phenomenon with a simple before/after design that ignores timing and unfolding. Another misuse is treating process as a slogan instead of an analytic commitment. Alternatives and competitors: Stage models and life-cycle models are alternatives when the phenomenon does have clear phases. Sociomateriality is a related alternative when the main focus is entanglement rather than flow. Complexity theory is a related alternative when the main focus is interdependence and emergence.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use correspondence (process view) when the question is about ongoing becoming and change, and you want to explain digital transformation as a lived process, not a fixed before and after. Example research question: "How does a digital transformation unfold as a continuous process of sociotechnological change rather than as a sequence of projects?" Example hypothesis (better as a proposition here): "Digital transformation in platform organizations unfolds through ongoing reconfiguration and re-attunement, where small events and timing (kairos) shape major shifts in routines." What I would measure (simple): I would use longitudinal data (interviews, observations, digital trace data) and build a process narrative that tracks rhythms, turning points, and reconfigurations. I would look for how attention shifts, how practices change, and how technology features and governance evolve together. Seminal APA citation: Ingold, T. (2015). The life of lines. London, UK: Routledge.

💡 Exam-Tips
Variance vs. Process Theory Debate (Mohr 1982, Van de Ven 2007): Correspondence Theory aligns with process theorizing, not variance theory. Mohr (1982, "Explaining Organizational Behavior") defined variance theories as focusing on "which variables matter" while process theories focus on "how things unfold over time." Van de Ven and Poole (2005, "Alternative Approaches for Studying Organizational Change") expanded this distinction. On exams, distinguish your approach: "My answer uses process theory logic (Correspondence view) rather than variance theory because the phenomenon is continuous becoming, not discrete X→Y causation." This shows you understand the epistemological difference.
Markus & Robey (1988) IT and Organizational Change: Markus and Robey (1988, "Information Technology and Organizational Change in Digital Economies," Journal of Management Information Systems) classified IT change into three views: technological determinism, organizational determinism, and emergent views. Correspondence theory aligns with the emergent view - change emerges through interaction over time. On exams, cite this paper when explaining why process theories (including correspondence) are better for digital transformation: "Correspondence theory supports the emergent perspective (Markus & Robey 1988) where IT and organizational change co-produce outcomes unpredictably."
Distinguishing Process from Variance on Exams: When asked to evaluate or design a study, use these signals to pick process theory: continuous unfolding, timing matters, qualitative longitudinal data, narrative explanation. Use variance theory signals: discrete events, hypothesis testing, cross-sectional data, effect sizes. A critical-point: Be explicit about your choice. Don't just say "I study change over time." Say: "I use process theory (correspondence view) because this phenomenon is continuous becoming where timing and attentionality reshape practices, not just a linear X→Y causation (variance view)."
Behavioral Margalit, 1996

CARE Theory of Dignity

Dignity and respect shape compliance, cooperation, and organizational justice perceptions.

Core Concept: People comply more with rules and authority when they feel respected and trusted. Surveillance, monitoring, and distrust reduce dignity and trigger resistance and violation rather than compliance.

🔑 Dignity Perception: Individuals seek and respond to treatment showing respect and trust
📊 Compliance Drivers: High dignity increases voluntary compliance over coercion
🔄 Surveillance Backfire: Excessive monitoring signals distrust and reduces dignity perception
⚡ IS Culture: Security implemented with dignity support builds culture; punitive approach triggers circumvention
IS Relevance: Explains why punitive security approaches reduce effectiveness; shows that sustainable compliance requires treating employees as trusted professionals, not potential threats.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Leidner and Tona (2021), "The CARE Theory of Dignity Amid Personal Data Digitalization," MIS Quarterly. Key contribution: explains how digitalization creates dignity claims and affronts, triggering responses to restore equilibrium.
Discipline of origin IS-native theory with roots in moral philosophy and dignity scholarship.
IS-native or borrowed? IS-native.
Gregor (2006) type Type II leaning Type IV: explains dignity dynamics and supports predictions about when responses (regulation, protest, redesign) will emerge.
Core constructs Claims, Affronts, Response, Equilibrium (CARE); dignity types: behavioral, meritocratic, inherent.
Mechanism (plain) Personal data digitalization can enhance dignity (claims) and violate dignity (affronts). When affronts outweigh claims, dignity disequilibrium triggers responses aimed at restoring equilibrium.
Boundary conditions Best for contexts involving personal data, surveillance, profiling, and AI systems that affect autonomy, recognition, and human worth. Not designed as a narrow behavioral intention model.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" CARE is part of a broader top-journal movement toward digital responsibility and ethical IS theorizing.
📚 Foundational Origins

CARE theory was developed because many older IS theories do not cover dignity well. As personal data digitalization expands, people experience both claims (new abilities, recognition, convenience) and affronts (surveillance, loss of autonomy, unfair profiling). Leidner and Tona (2021) argue that IS research needs a mechanism-based theory that explains these dignity dynamics, not only adoption and use. CARE provides that structure through four elements: Claims, Affronts, Response, and Equilibrium. This is also an IS-native contribution. The theory is motivated by moral philosophy and dignity scholarship, but it is written for IS phenomena like datafication, profiling, and AI systems that reshape autonomy and recognition.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main proposition is that digitalization creates a dignity balance. Claims are dignity-enhancing aspects of digitalization. Affronts are dignity-violating aspects. When affronts outweigh claims, a dignity disequilibrium occurs and triggers responses aimed at restoring equilibrium. Responses can include individual behaviors (withdrawal, resistance) and collective actions (regulation, activism, redesign). The mechanism is moral and social at the same time. People experience dignity threats as more than inconvenience. They experience them as violations of worth, autonomy, and recognition. That is why responses can be strong and can escalate beyond individual choice into institutional change. Boundary conditions matter. CARE fits best when the phenomenon involves personal data, surveillance, profiling, or algorithmic decision making that affects autonomy and status. It is not designed as a narrow adoption model, and it should not be used as a simple "ethics variable" without tracing claims, affronts, and responses.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

CARE is still new, so the evolution is mostly about how it connects to broader streams. One stream is responsible and ethical AI. Another stream is digital responsibility. Recker et al. (2025) discuss digital responsibility perspectives and future directions, which reflects the broader top-journal movement: IS research is increasingly expected to theorize not only value creation, but also harms and responsibility. CARE is a strong kernel theory in that movement when dignity is the focal harm. Integration with other theories is also exam-friendly. Institutional theory helps you study how responses become formalized as regulation and legitimacy shifts. Sociomateriality helps you study how dignity is enacted in everyday practices with artifacts (logs, interfaces, controls). Delegation helps when the dignity issue comes from authority being shifted to an AI system without accountability.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: CARE addresses an important gap in IS theorizing. It gives you a mechanism to talk about dignity, not only as a value statement but as a dynamic process that produces observable responses. It is also very relevant for comps because ethical and societal impact questions are now common. Weaknesses: Measurement and design are hard. Dignity is deep and multi-dimensional, so simple survey proxies can feel shallow. Another limitation is that the theory can be applied too broadly. Not every negative reaction to technology is a dignity affront. Common misapplications in IS: A common misuse is treating CARE as an "ethics section" and not using it as the main mechanism. Another is labeling something as an affront without specifying which dignity type is threatened (behavioral, meritocratic, inherent) and without showing how responses attempt to restore equilibrium. Alternatives and competitors: Trust theory is useful when the mechanism is vulnerability and reliance rather than dignity. Institutional theory is useful when the main pattern is regulation and legitimacy. Sociomateriality is useful when the main focus is practice-level enactment. CARE is most useful when dignity is the central concept, not an add-on.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use CARE when the question is about how datafication affects dignity, respect, and moral harm, and you need an IS-native lens beyond a basic privacy calculus. Example research question: "When workplaces introduce AI monitoring and profiling tools, how do employees experience dignity claims and affronts, and what responses follow?" Example hypothesis (or proposition): "Greater perceived dignity affronts from AI monitoring are associated with stronger resistance responses (complaints, avoidance, demands for transparency) aimed at restoring dignity equilibrium." What I would measure (simple): I would measure claims and affronts using validated dignity-related items adapted to data practices (autonomy, recognition, fairness), and I would measure responses through a mix of survey and behavior (opt-out rates, grievance reports, policy changes). A strong design would also trace escalation from individual response to institutional response (policy and regulation). Seminal APA citation: Leidner, D. E., & Tona, O. (2021). The CARE Theory of Dignity Amid Personal Data Digitalization. MIS Quarterly, 45(1), 343-370.

💡 Exam-Tips
Connection to Organizational Justice Theory (Bies & Moag 1986, Colquitt 2001): CARE Theory extends interactional justice from Bies and Moag (1986, "Interactional Justice: Communication Criteria of Fairness," Research on Negotiation in Organizations). Interactional justice focuses on how people perceive treatment quality and respect in interactions. Colquitt (2001, "On the Dimensionality of Organizational Justice: A Construct Validation of a Measure," Journal of Applied Psychology) showed that dignity and respect are core to fairness perceptions. CARE elevates this: in security and datafication contexts, dignity is not just about fair treatment - it is about preserving autonomy and recognition. On exams, connect these: "My answer draws on organizational justice theory (Colquitt 2001) but applies it specifically to data practices through CARE's dignity lens, which extends justice theory to continuous monitoring contexts."
CARE as Alternative to Deterrence Theory: Traditional security compliance research uses deterrence theory (threat of punishment increases compliance). CARE inverts this: high dignity increases intrinsic motivation for compliance, while surveillance (perceived distrust) undermines it. This is critical for exam answers. If asked about security compliance, don't just cite deterrence. Say: "Deterrence theory predicts punishment increases compliance, but CARE theory shows this is extrinsic motivation that is fragile and breeds resentment. Dignity-supporting approaches trigger intrinsic motivation (I comply because I respect the system and want to) which is more sustainable."
Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Motivation in Security Compliance: CARE aligns with Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan 1985) which distinguishes intrinsic motivation (do it because it matters to you) from extrinsic motivation (do it to avoid punishment or gain reward). On exams, when discussing security compliance or employee behavior, use this distinction: "High-dignity security implementations foster intrinsic motivation (employees feel trusted to make good choices), while surveillance-heavy implementations trigger extrinsic motivation (I comply only because I'm being watched), which research shows is less durable and more prone to workarounds." This shows sophisticated understanding of behavioral theory.
Meta-Framework Kerlinger & Lee, 2000

Kerlinger and Lee's Theory of Theory (Definition of Theory)

Definition and standards for what constitutes a scientific theory.

Core Concept: A scientific theory is a set of related propositions with logical consistency, predictive power, and testability. It distinguishes true theories from ad-hoc explanations, hunches, and frameworks.

🔑 Propositional Set: Theory must be a connected set of claims, not isolated observations
📊 Logical Consistency: Propositions must relate logically without internal contradiction
🔄 Testability Requirement: Theory must generate empirical predictions that can be falsified
⚡ Scientific Standards: Separates theory from opinion, intuition, and purely descriptive frameworks
IS Relevance: Standards framework ensuring IS theories meet scientific rigor; prevents confusion between true theories and informal explanatory frameworks.
📊 Quick Reference
Field Notes
Seminal work Kerlinger and Lee (2000), Foundations of behavioral research. Key contribution: a widely cited definition of theory as an interrelated set of constructs and propositions that explains and predicts phenomena.
Discipline of origin Social science methodology / research methods.
IS-native or borrowed? Borrowed.
Gregor (2006) type Meta (definition, not a theory of a phenomenon). It is used to clarify what counts as theory.
Core constructs Constructs, definitions, propositions, relationships, explanation, prediction.
Mechanism (plain) A theory is not just a list of variables. It is a connected explanation: what concepts matter and how they relate.
Boundary conditions Useful for evaluating theory quality, not for directly explaining an IS outcome.
2020-2025 IS "proof of life" This definition is mostly used in doctoral training and in theory-building debates rather than as a citation lens inside empirical IS papers. (It is a comps tool, not a dependent-variable theory.)
📚 Foundational Origins

Kerlinger and Lee's contribution is definitional. They provide language that helps you distinguish theory from description. In comps, this definition is useful because it gives you a clean way to critique weak papers: if there are no propositions and no causal story, it may be conceptual labeling, not theory.

💎 Theoretical Core

The main claim is about structure. A theory needs constructs (what), definitions (what the constructs mean), and propositions (how/why). The mechanism is not a causal mechanism of the world. It is a standard for building explanations. Boundary conditions: This definition is helpful, but it is not the only one. Some IS theorizing is process-based and not easily reduced to static propositions. So in exam answers, you can use Kerlinger and Lee to push for clarity without forcing every theory into a variance model.

🔗 Evolution and Extensions (IS focus)

In IS, this kind of definition supports debates about contribution. It connects naturally to Whetten (what/why/who/where/when) and Gregor's types. Together, they help you position what kind of "theory work" a paper is doing.

⚠️ Critical Analysis (Exam-ready)

Strengths: It is simple and powerful. It helps you evaluate whether a paper actually has a theoretical argument or only variables and results. Weaknesses: It can bias you toward variance theorizing and away from process and interpretive theorizing if used rigidly. Common misapplications in IS: Using the definition as a "gatekeeping" tool to dismiss process theories or design theories that do not look like hypothesis models. Alternatives and competitors: Gregor (2006) for theory types and Whetten (1989) for theory-building elements.

⚠️ Practical Application for Exam

Exam use note: Use Kerlinger and Lee when the exam question is "what is theory?" or when you need to critique a paper for weak theorizing. Example research question: "What makes an IS paper's theory contribution strong enough for a top journal?" Example hypothesis (or proposition): "Papers that specify constructs and causal mechanisms clearly will be evaluated as stronger contributions than papers that only report significant relationships." What I would measure (simple): I would operationalize theory quality using coding of construct clarity, presence of mechanisms, and boundary conditions, then link that to reviewer outcomes or citation impact. Seminal APA citation: Kerlinger, F. N., & Lee, H. B. (2000). Foundations of behavioral research (4th ed.). Harcourt College Publishers.

💡 Exam-Tips
Connection to Popper's Falsification: Kerlinger and Lee's emphasis on testability aligns directly with Popper's (1959, The Logic of Scientific Discovery) principle of falsifiability: a theory is scientific if it can potentially be proven wrong.
