Both predict that framing and context matter for disclosure reception. Both apply well to cybersecurity because cyber events are high-uncertainty, salient, and carry both reputational and regulatory consequences. The integration point is clear: managers strategically choose disclosure framing (agency), and investors respond through loss aversion and probability weighting (prospect). In a cyber incident, managers face pressure to disclose (regulation, monitoring), but they also control how the incident is framed (preventive efforts, mitigation readiness, industry context). Investors then respond to that framing, which feeds back into managers' disclosure incentives for future incidents.

🎯 When to Use Which
Use Agency Theory when: The research question is about governance incentives, monitoring, or disclosure timing. For example: "Do firms with stronger institutional ownership disclose cyber incidents faster?" or "How does audit quality shape cyber risk reporting?" Agency theory explains why certain governance structures lead to different disclosure choices.
Use Prospect Theory when: The research question is about investor reaction or framing effects. For example: "Do investors respond differently to cyber disclosures that emphasize prevention versus incident response?" or "How does breach timing (fiscal quarter, post-earnings) change stock price impact?" Prospect theory explains the psychological mechanism behind differential responses to the same cyber event.
Use both together when: The full story is about disclosure production and reception. For example: "How do governance structures shape whether managers frame disclosures in preventive versus mitigative terms, and how do those framing choices affect investor reactions and future disclosure incentives?" This is the richest answer and shows sophisticated integration.
Comparative Analysis

Contrast 2: Institutional Theory vs Institutional Logics

Macro-level isomorphic pressures (why organizations converge) versus micro-level competing meaning systems (why organizations differ in how they respond).

📊 Quick Comparison
Dimension Institutional Theory Institutional Logics
Core Focus How field-level pressures (coercive, normative, mimetic) push organizations toward similar structures and practices How multiple competing belief systems (market, professional, state, community logics) shape heterogeneous organizational responses to the same pressures
Key Mechanism Isomorphism through legitimacy-seeking: organizations adopt practices to appear legitimate to stakeholders Logic dominance: which logic is salient shapes whether an organization treats a policy as cost (market logic), compliance (state logic), or expertise (professional logic)
Unit of Analysis Organizational field (industry/sector) convergence; adoption diffusion patterns Intra-organizational conflict and hybrid practices; power shifts between stakeholder groups with different logics
Assumptions Organizations in the same field will converge because legitimacy pressures are similar Organizations in the same field may diverge because different groups pull toward different logics; power determines which logic wins
IS Application Explains why similar firms adopt similar security frameworks (NIST, ISO) and why security practices diffuse across industries Explains why two banks with the same compliance mandate invest differently in security: one as cost-minimization (market logic) vs. expertise-driven (professional logic)
🔑 Key Differences

Explanatory scope: Institutional theory excels at explaining convergence and similarity. Why do most firms in healthcare, finance, or tech adopt similar governance structures? Institutional theory says: legitimacy pressures in the field push toward isomorphism. Institutional logics focuses on variation and conflict. Why do two firms adopt the same framework but implement it differently? Institutional logics says: competing logics within each firm shape implementation differently, depending on which groups have power.

Ontology of institutions: Institutional theory treats institutions as external pressures on organizations. The field is "out there," and organizations respond. Institutional logics treats institutions as internalized meaning systems held by actors inside organizations. Different stakeholders (IT leaders, CFOs, auditors) carry different logics, and outcomes reflect which logic dominates internally.

Homogeneity vs. heterogeneity: Institutional theory predicts homogeneity within fields: similar organizations converge on similar practices. Institutional logics predicts heterogeneity even in similar organizations when different logics compete. A state agency and a private firm may face the same regulatory pressure (institutional theory says they'll converge), but if the agency emphasizes state/public-interest logic and the firm emphasizes market logic, they'll diverge in actual practice.

Change mechanism: In institutional theory, change happens through field-level shifts (new regulation, new norms). In institutional logics, change happens through power shifts between logics within organizations. Technology can tip the balance toward one logic over another.

🤝 Where They Overlap

Both theories recognize that legitimacy and social acceptance matter, not just efficiency. Both emphasize that organizations are embedded in broader social environments, not islands. Both apply well to security and compliance contexts where external regulation meets internal organizational dynamics. The integration is natural: institutional theory explains field-level isomorphic pressures (why security frameworks converge across firms), and institutional logics explains how different firms interpret and prioritize those pressures differently based on internal logic competition. For example, GDPR creates a coercive institutional pressure (everyone must comply), but which logic organizations prioritize - market logic (compliance as cost to minimize), professional logic (compliance as data governance excellence), or state logic (compliance as citizen protection) - determines investment level, governance structure, and actual practice intensity.

🎯 When to Use Which
Use Institutional Theory when: The question is about field-level convergence or adoption diffusion. For example: "Why do cybersecurity frameworks like NIST diffuse across industries?" or "How do regulatory changes shape compliance adoption rates?" Institutional theory explains isomorphism and legitimacy-driven diffusion at the field level.
Use Institutional Logics when: The question is about intra-organizational variation, conflict resolution, or practice heterogeneity. For example: "Why do two firms with identical compliance mandates invest differently in security?" or "How do competing logics between IT and Finance shape security budgeting?" Institutional logics explains variation driven by which internal stakeholder logic dominates.
Use both together when: The full story involves field-level pressures creating convergence at the formal level, but internal logic conflicts creating divergence at the implementation level. For example: "How do regulatory pressures (institutional theory) create convergence in security policies, but internal logic conflicts between market and professional logics (institutional logics) produce divergent implementation and effectiveness?" This shows understanding of nested dynamics.
Comparative Analysis

Contrast 3: Technology Affordances vs Sociomateriality

Action possibilities that exist independent of practice (affordances) versus technology and practice co-produced together as ontologically inseparable (sociomateriality).

📊 Quick Comparison
Dimension Technology Affordances Sociomateriality
Core Focus Action possibilities that a technology makes available to goal-oriented users given their capabilities and context How technology and social practice are ontologically entangled and co-produced through ongoing work routines
Key Mechanism Relational property between artifact, user goals, and context; actualization occurs when users perceive and enact affordances Performativity: outcomes emerge through practices where material features, norms, and intentions are inseparably produced together
Unit of Analysis Specific action possibilities (e.g., anonymity affordance, persistence affordance); actualization by user groups over time Situated practices and organizational routines; material and social features as a single analytical unit
Technology Status Technology has properties/potentials that exist independent of whether they are used; affordances can be latent (not actualized) Technology is only ever known through practice; "what the technology is" is produced simultaneously with "what the practice is"
IS Application Explains why a feature (e.g., encryption) enables some users (privacy-conscious) but constrains others (compliance auditors); heterogeneous use of same technology Explains how security tools reshape organizational routines; audit practices don't just implement the tool, they enact what the tool becomes in use
🔑 Key Differences

Ontological commitment: Technology affordances assume that technology has properties independent of practice. A password manager affords certain actions (generating complex passwords, auto-filling, securely storing) whether or not a user actualizes them. The affordance exists as a potential. Sociomateriality claims that technology and practice are ontologically inseparable. "What the password manager is" is not separate from "how it is used in organizational routines." The technology is enacted through practice; it does not pre-exist practice.

Causality and determinism: Affordance theory avoids technological determinism by saying technology enables, constrains, and affords possibilities - but users actualize or ignore those possibilities based on goals and context. This is still somewhat directional: technology has properties that enable/constrain. Sociomateriality avoids determinism more radically by claiming no directionality - social and material are mutually constituting in real time. Neither determines the other; they are produced together.

Latency and actualization: Affordances can exist but not be actualized. A security tool might afford transparency (through audit logs), but if no one reads the logs, the transparency affordance remains latent. Sociomateriality does not separate potential from actual; it focuses on what emerges through practice. The question is not "what affordances exist" but "what practices and routines are performed, and what realities do those performances bring into being?"

Measurement and methods: Affordance research often uses comparative designs: how do different user groups actualize the same affordances differently? Or longitudinal: how does actualization change over time? Sociomaterial research uses deep observation of practices: ethnography, process tracing, or document analysis to show how material and social features are entangled in specific routines.

🤝 Where They Overlap

Both theories reject technological determinism (technology automatically causes outcomes). Both take the artifact seriously without reducing outcomes to user choice or social factors alone. Both emphasize heterogeneous implementation - the same technology can produce different outcomes in different contexts. Both apply well to understanding digital transformation in organizations because they explain why a security tool, platform, or system does not have one single effect; it depends on what users can do (affordances) and how practices evolve (sociomateriality). The tension between them is real and worth acknowledging: affordance theory allows that technology has properties independent of practice; sociomateriality denies that separation. Some scholars argue they can be combined by treating affordances as stable potentials and sociomateriality as practice-specific enactments of those potentials, but others argue that's a compromise that violates sociomateriality's core ontological claim.

🎯 When to Use Which
Use Affordances when: The research question is about action possibilities and heterogeneous actualization across user groups. For example: "How do platform ephemerality affordances change user disclosure behavior?" or "What affordances do security dashboards provide to different stakeholders (auditors, IT teams, executives) and how do they perceive and use them differently?" Affordance theory is designed to explain why the same feature enables some and constrains others.
Use Sociomateriality when: The research question is about how practices and technology co-evolve or how organizational routines are reshaped through technology. For example: "How do employees and an AI audit tool co-produce new audit practices, and how do those practices reshape what the tool is used for over time?" or "How do security incident response routines become entangled with log analysis tools?" Sociomateriality is designed to show how the boundary between "tool" and "practice" is blurry and fluid.
Use both together (carefully) when: You want to explain both what possibilities the technology creates (affordances) and how those possibilities are enacted through situated practices (sociomateriality). For example: "A security monitoring tool affords real-time threat detection (affordance), but whether that affordance is actualized depends on organizational routines of alert triage and response, which co-evolve with how the tool is configured and used (sociomateriality)." If you combine them, be explicit about whether you treat affordances as independent potentials (slight risk of slipping back toward determinism) or as always-already enacted in practice (pure sociomaterial stance).
Comparative Analysis

Contrast 4: Routine Activity Theory vs Neutralization Theory

Structural opportunity vs. cognitive justification - two complementary lenses for understanding security violations in cybercrime and insider threats.

📊 Quick Comparison
Dimension Routine Activity Theory Neutralization Theory
Core Focus When and where incidents occur (situational opportunity) Why people justify violations (cognitive dissonance reduction)
Key Mechanism Convergence of three elements: motivated offender + suitable target + weak guardianship Moral rationalization through neutralization techniques before and after violation
Unit of Analysis Time-space convergence; organizational structure; control systems Individual belief systems and self-justification processes
Causal Logic Opportunity → Incident (structural determinism) Belief → Rationalization → Reduced internal control → Violation (psychological enablement)
What Changes Behavior Removing opportunity (access controls, guardianship, monitoring) Changing moral reasoning and cultural norms (values, peer influence, accountability)
IS Application System design, access control architecture, monitoring systems Policy communication, culture building, insider threat profiling
Strongest Assumption Motivation is constant; opportunity structures drive incidents People care about norms; they rationalize violations to protect self-image
Boundary Conditions Does not explain motivation or moral psychology; weaker with fully committed offenders Works best for "normal people"; less relevant for actors who fully reject norms
🔑 Key Differences

Routine Activity Theory is fundamentally about structures and opportunities. It says: incidents happen when three elements line up in time and space, regardless of deep motivation. The theory is agnostic about "why" someone is motivated. It focuses on the environmental architecture that enables or blocks violations. You reduce incidents by changing access rights, improving monitoring, or removing targets - all external to the person. The causal flow is straightforward: opportunity exists → incident occurs.

Neutralization Theory is about internal moral management. It says: violations happen when people temporarily suspend their moral rules through rationalization. People do not wake up as "bad people." They are usually people who care about norms, but they manage cognitive dissonance by talking themselves into rule-breaking. The techniques are well-known: "I had no choice," "No harm was done," "Security is unreasonable," "I was helping the team." These rationalizations weaken internal controls and make violations psychologically easier. The causal flow is more complex: dissonance → neutralization techniques → reduced guilt → weaker internal control → violation more likely.

In IS terms: Routine activity explains why expanding employee access increases unauthorized attempts even when motivation does not change. Neutralization explains why employees with clear knowledge of policies still violate them because they have rationalized the violation as acceptable.

🤝 Where They Overlap

Both theories are criminological in origin and focus on normal people committing violations, not just pathological offenders. Neither theory reduces deviance to "bad personality." Instead, both treat violations as situated - shaped by context, not only by who the person is.

Both also integrate well. In fact, they are complementary. Routine activity explains the structural preconditions (opportunity exists). Neutralization explains the psychological enablement (person justifies using that opportunity). A comprehensive IS security explanation often uses both: "Access expansion creates opportunity (RAT), and employees rationalize policy violations as productivity-necessary or unfair (neutralization), making incidents more likely."

Both also have practical implications for security interventions. Routine activity points to technical and structural controls. Neutralization points to cultural and communication interventions (policy clarity, value alignment, peer norms).

🎯 When to Use Which
Use Routine Activity Theory when:
  • Your research question is about when and where incidents occur (temporal or structural patterns)
  • You are studying control effectiveness (monitoring, access rights, system architecture)
  • Motivation is relatively constant or not the focus of your mechanism
  • You can observe opportunity structures changing (e.g., post-acquisition access explosion, role transitions)
  • Example: "Does expanding system access increase unauthorized access attempts?"
Use Neutralization Theory when:
  • Your research question is about why people violate policies they know and understand
  • You are studying moral reasoning, beliefs, or cultural factors in policy violations
  • You want to explain persistence of violations despite awareness (training does not solve it)
  • You can measure or observe rationalization language or techniques (interviews, surveys, behavioral traces)
  • Example: "What neutralization techniques do employees use to justify password sharing, and do these predict repeated violations?"
Use Both together when:
  • You want a multi-level explanation: structural conditions + individual psychological response
  • Your study design captures both opportunity contexts and moral reasoning
  • Example: "After M&A integration, access expansion (RAT) creates opportunity, and employees rationalize policy shortcuts as necessary for integration work (neutralization)."
💡 Exam Strategy

In comps or quals: Show you understand the distinction by talking about mechanism. Routine activity is about structure-to-behavior (opportunity matters). Neutralization is about psychology-to-behavior (how people manage moral conflict). A strong answer cites both, explains why they address different questions, and shows which is appropriate for your research question. Avoid saying one is "better" - they explain different causal processes. The real power is using both together to build a richer story: opportunity creates the conditions, and neutralization explains how people respond to those conditions.

Comparative Analysis

Contrast 5: RBV vs Human Capital Theory

Firm-level resource competitive advantage vs. individual skill investment - two complementary lenses on why digital capabilities matter organizationally.

📊 Quick Comparison
Dimension Resource-Based View (RBV) Human Capital Theory
Core Focus Why some firms achieve sustained competitive advantage through valuable, rare, inimitable resources Why individuals and firms invest in skills when expected returns exceed costs
Key Mechanism Resource heterogeneity + immobility = sustained advantage; VRIN logic (valuable, rare, inimitable, non-substitutable) Investment logic: costs (education, wages) vs. productivity gains; skills raise output; accumulation over time
Unit of Analysis Firm-level competitive position; resource bundles; causal ambiguity and path dependence Individual-level investment choice and skill accumulation; organizational hiring and training decisions
Primary Question "Why does this firm outperform and sustain that advantage?" "Why invest in this skill, and what productivity returns justify that investment?"
What Creates Advantage Resources hard to imitate (causal ambiguity, path dependence, complementary assets) Skills that increase productivity and are firm-specific (high switching costs)
IS Application Why digital transformation creates sustainable advantage for some firms but not others; complementary assets matter Why firms hire analytics talent after breaches; IT talent investments; role of education in capability building
Strongest Assumption Differences persist; difficult-to-imitate resources generate performance premiums Expected returns drive investment; skills are productive assets that compound over time
Boundary Conditions Hard to measure VRIN without circular logic; digital resources diffuse quickly; RBV less effective in fast-moving markets Hard to measure skills directly; assumes rational investment logic; ignores institutional/signaling motives
🔑 Key Differences

RBV is fundamentally about firm-level competitive advantage. It starts with an observation: firms differ in performance in ways that last. Why? Because they have different resources and capabilities, and some of those differences are protected by isolating mechanisms (causal ambiguity, path dependence). The theory is about competitive positioning. You get advantage when your resources are valuable (valued in the market), rare (few competitors have them), hard to imitate, and not easily substitutable. The causal question is: "How does this resource lead to sustained advantage?" In IS, RBV addresses digital strategy questions: Why does IT investment work for some firms but not others? Answer: IT alone is easy to copy. Advantage comes from complementary assets (governance, talent routines, data quality processes) that are bundled with IT.

Human Capital Theory is fundamentally about investment rationale. It starts with a different observation: people and firms spend money on education and training. Why? Because they expect future returns. The theory models skills as capital with costs and benefits. General skills are portable (you take them to another firm). Firm-specific skills are valuable only inside that firm and create switching costs. The causal question is: "Does this investment in skills pay off?" In IS, human capital theory addresses talent questions: Why do firms hire more cybersecurity people after breaches? Answer: The breach increased perceived returns to security talent. Why is analytics talent valuable? Answer: Analytics skills increase productivity in data-driven work.

In IS terms: RBV explains why some firms sustain digital advantage (they have rare complementary assets). Human capital theory explains why firms invest in digital talent (expected productivity returns justify hiring). They address different questions: RBV is about advantage, human capital is about investment.

🤝 Where They Overlap

Both theories treat talent and skills as economically important assets that shape organizational outcomes. Both recognize that not all resources are equal - some are easy to buy or imitate, others are not.

Critically, they integrate well together. RBV can explain why human capital matters: when a firm builds firm-specific skills (through training, hiring experienced talent, or developing routines that embed knowledge), those skills become hard to imitate and create competitive advantage. Human capital theory explains why the firm makes that investment: expected productivity returns justify the cost. A strong IS paper often uses both: "Firms invest in analytics talent (HC motivation: expected returns). This talent is most valuable when embedded in firm-specific data governance routines (RBV: competitive advantage). The combination of skilled people + firm-specific processes is hard for competitors to replicate, creating sustained advantage."

Both also focus on observable outcomes. RBV researchers can measure performance differences. Human capital researchers can measure hiring, training, and skill proxies (job postings, certifications). This makes both theories empirically tractable in IS.

🎯 When to Use Which
Use RBV when:
  • Your research question is about sustained competitive advantage or performance differences
  • You are asking "why does this firm outperform?" rather than "why did they hire?"
  • You can observe or argue for rare, inimitable resources (complementary assets, routines, governance)
  • You want to explain why IT alone rarely creates advantage (IT is easy to buy)
  • Example: "Why do some firms extract more value from data analytics investments than others? Answer: RBV. Advantage comes from complementary assets (talent + governance + data quality routines), not from analytics tools alone."
Use Human Capital Theory when:
  • Your research question is about talent investment decisions or hiring
  • You are asking "why invest in this skill?" rather than "why does advantage persist?"
  • You can measure investment responses to shocks (breach → hiring surge) or expected returns
  • You want to explain why firms respond to opportunities by building capability through talent
  • Example: "Do firms hire more cybersecurity talent after breaches, and is the hiring intensity related to breach visibility? Answer: HC. Breaches raise perceived returns to security talent, triggering investment."
Use Both together when:
  • You want to explain how talent investments build competitive advantage
  • Your study design shows investment → skill accumulation → advantage
  • You are studying firm capability building over time (dynamic advantage)
  • Example: "Firms invest in cybersecurity talent (HC: expected returns from breach risk reduction) and embed that talent in firm-specific processes (RBV: competitive advantage). Together, they build sustained advantage in breach prevention."
⚠️ Critical Considerations

RBV pitfalls: Avoid circular logic. Do not define a resource as "valuable" only because the firm performs well. Define resources before looking at performance. Specify competitors and explain why imitation is hard.

Human Capital pitfalls: Do not assume all investment is productivity-driven. Hiring can be symbolic or driven by institutional pressure (compliance). Measure investment and returns separately.

Combining them: If you use both, be clear about levels. HC is individual/hiring level. RBV is firm level. Show how talent investments (HC) build into firm capabilities (RBV).

💡 Exam Strategy

In comps: Show you understand the distinction by level of analysis. RBV answers "why does the firm have sustained advantage?" Human capital answers "why invest in skills?" A strong answer uses RBV for competitive advantage questions and HC for investment questions. If you use both, explain the causal chain: investment → capability → competitive advantage. Avoid treating talent as just another resource without explaining why the investment makes sense economically (HC) or why the capability is hard to replicate (RBV).

Comparative Analysis

Contrast 6: Trust Theory vs TAM (e-commerce and privacy)

Vulnerability-based adoption vs. utility-based adoption - why different mechanisms dominate when risk and uncertainty matter.

📊 Quick Comparison
Dimension Trust Theory TAM (Technology Acceptance Model)
Core Focus Whether people will accept vulnerability and share sensitive information or rely on systems under uncertainty Whether people perceive a system as useful and easy to use, predicting intention to adopt
Key Mechanism Perceived trustworthiness (ability, benevolence, integrity) → willingness to be vulnerable → disclosure/reliance behavior Perceived usefulness (PU) + perceived ease of use (PEOU) → attitude/intention → use behavior
Psychological Logic Risk perception + uncertainty about trustee behavior → need for trust to accept vulnerability Belief about system value + effort cost → rational choice to adopt based on personal benefit
Unit of Analysis Willingness to be vulnerable (behavioral intention toward disclosure/reliance); context-dependent risk Individual adoption intention; belief-to-behavior pathway; focus on task productivity
What Drives Adoption Perceived trustworthiness (ability, benevolence, integrity) + context safeguards (controls, norms, accountability) Perceived usefulness (does it help my work?) + perceived ease of use (is it easy?)
IS Application E-commerce privacy disclosure; AI system reliance; sharing health data; human-robot collaboration under uncertainty Enterprise system adoption; software tool adoption; productivity systems where voluntary use dominates
Strongest Assumption Risk and vulnerability are salient; trustee characteristics matter more than task benefits; people need reassurance about intent and competence Usefulness and ease dominate decision-making; voluntary contexts; productivity is the primary motivation
Boundary Conditions Strongest when: high vulnerability, high uncertainty, cannot fully monitor outcomes. Weaker when: low risk, long-term habit, strong institutional controls substitute for trust Strongest when: voluntary use, early adoption, productivity-driven work. Weaker when: mandatory use, privacy risks, high consequences, AI explainability matters
🔑 Key Differences

Trust Theory addresses vulnerability and uncertainty. It says: People adopt or disclose when they accept vulnerability based on perceived trustworthiness. The core mechanism is about managing risk when you cannot fully control outcomes. In e-commerce, when you enter payment information, you have vulnerability: the merchant could misuse your data, charge you incorrectly, or sell your information. Trust in the merchant's ability (can they process payments correctly?), benevolence (do they care about my interests?), and integrity (are they honest?) determines whether you accept that vulnerability. The causal flow is psychological: trustworthiness beliefs → reduce perceived risk → increase willingness to be vulnerable → disclosure/reliance behavior.

TAM addresses utility and effort. It says: People adopt when they believe the system is useful (helps their work) and not hard to use. The mechanism is about rational choice based on task benefits and effort costs. In e-commerce, when you adopt a shopping site, you do so because it helps you find products efficiently and is easy to navigate. The causal flow is straightforward and cognitive: perceived usefulness + perceived ease of use → intention to use → actual use.

Key distinction in e-commerce: Trust matters for disclosure decisions (Will I give my payment information?). TAM matters for adoption decisions (Is this a useful place to shop?). Privacy contexts demand trust because the risk is information misuse. Productivity contexts demand TAM because the value is efficiency gains.

In IS terms: Trust explains why people disclose private information to some platforms and not others, even if both are equally useful. TAM explains why people adopt productivity tools but TAM alone fails in privacy-critical settings because usefulness does not address vulnerability concerns.

🤝 Where They Overlap

Both theories model beliefs driving behavior and are rooted in rational decision-making logic. Both are about adoption and reliance on systems. Both have been validated extensively in IS research.

Critically, they are not incompatible - they explain different causal pathways that often operate simultaneously. In e-commerce adoption, for example:

  • TAM pathway: "Is this site easy to navigate and will it help me find products quickly?" → Adoption increases
  • Trust pathway: "Is this merchant trustworthy? Will they protect my payment information?" → Disclosure increases

A strong e-commerce platform needs both. High usefulness (TAM) gets people to visit. High trustworthiness (Trust) gets them to share payment information and come back.

Both theories also point to context and moderators. Trust increases with safeguards, regulations, and reputation signals. TAM effects are stronger in voluntary, productivity-focused contexts.

🎯 When to Use Which
Use Trust Theory when:
  • Your research question involves vulnerability, risk, or uncertainty about outcomes
  • The dependent variable is disclosure of sensitive information, reliance on advice, or delegation/authority allocation
  • You are studying privacy-sensitive behaviors: data sharing, health information disclosure, financial information
  • The adoption context involves asymmetric information or consequences you cannot fully control
  • Example: "How do security badges and company reputation signals shape user trust and willingness to share payment information on e-commerce sites?"
Use TAM when:
  • Your research question is about adoption of productivity tools or systems
  • The dependent variable is intention to use or actual system use (not disclosure or reliance on risky information)
  • You are studying voluntary adoption contexts where people have discretion
  • The primary value of the system is task performance or efficiency gains, not trust or risk mitigation
  • Example: "Do employees adopt an analytics dashboard when they perceive it as useful for their daily decisions and easy to learn?"
Use Both together when:
  • Your adoption context involves both productivity and risk (e.g., AI-assisted decision-making, health apps, financial platforms)
  • You want to show that trust explains vulnerability-related decisions while TAM explains utility decisions
  • Your study design can measure both trust and usefulness as separate mechanisms
  • Example: "E-commerce adoption involves two decisions: (1) Is this site useful? (TAM) and (2) Can I trust them with my payment info? (Trust). Both pathways matter, and when trust is low, high usefulness cannot overcome the disclosure barrier."
💡 Key Exam Insight: AI and Privacy Contexts

This distinction becomes critical in modern IS contexts. Traditional TAM focuses on usefulness (PU) and ease of use (PEOU), which fits productivity systems. But in AI adoption, data privacy, cybersecurity, and human-robot collaboration, these contexts are NOT purely productivity-focused. They have vulnerability: you cannot fully control how the AI makes decisions, or how your data is used, or how a robot might behave. In these contexts, trust often dominates over perceived usefulness. Research on black-box AI, algorithm aversion, and privacy disclosure consistently shows that people will abandon a useful system if they do not trust it, or will not share data even if the system is beneficial. This is why recent IS work integrates trust with adoption models rather than using TAM alone.

Exam move: Show you understand that TAM is not a universal adoption model. In high-uncertainty, high-consequence, or privacy-sensitive settings, trust becomes primary. A strong answer recognizes when TAM is appropriate (productivity tools, voluntary adoption) and when trust-based models are more powerful (privacy, AI, delegation).

⚠️ Critical Measurement Considerations

Trust measurement: Use validated scales measuring ability, benevolence, and integrity separately. Do not collapse trust to a single item. Specify the target (trust in platform, trust in firm, trust in AI).

TAM measurement: Use Davis-validated items. Adapt them to the specific system and task. Measure both PU and PEOU. Measure intention and, if possible, actual use from logs.

When using both: Measure trust and usefulness independently. Show they predict different outcomes (trust → disclosure; usefulness → use frequency). Avoid treating trust as just another antecedent to usefulness - they are distinct mechanisms.

💡 Exam Strategy

In comps or quals: Show mastery of context-specific mechanism choice. Ask yourself: "Is this an adoption question (TAM) or a vulnerability/disclosure question (Trust)?" Most real-world IS scenarios involve both, so the strongest answer identifies which mechanism is primary for your research question and why the other matters too. In e-commerce, for example, trust is primary for payment disclosure, but TAM helps explain site navigation adoption. In AI adoption, trust often dominates usefulness because explainability and control are salient. Use this distinction to show you understand not just what the theories are, but when and why to apply them.

📝 Practice Questions: Part D (Criminological & Security Theories)

Question 1: Explain Routine Activity Theory (RAT) including the three necessary elements for crime. How does RAT apply to cybercrimes, and what are the key differences between physical and cyber guardianship?

Model Answer (98 words):

Routine Activity Theory (Cohen & Felson, 1979) argues crime requires three converging elements: motivated offender, suitable target (valuable, accessible), and absence of capable guardian. In cyberspace, motivated offenders abound globally; suitable targets include unpatched systems and credential databases; guardians include firewalls, intrusion detection, and security-aware users (Choi, 2008). Key difference: physical guardianship relies on presence (locked doors, security guards); cyber guardianship requires technical capability and vigilance (monitoring logs, patching). Cybercrime's low physical risk and high anonymity increase motivated offenders. Measurement: Target attractiveness, guardianship measures (technical, human). Critique: RAT underemphasizes offender motivation - not all opportunities become crimes.

Question 2: Describe General Deterrence Theory and the three dimensions of sanctions (certainty, severity, celerity). Why does research consistently find certainty more important than severity for deterring cybercrime?

Model Answer (99 words):

General Deterrence Theory (Becker, 1968) argues rational actors avoid crime when expected costs exceed benefits. Three sanction dimensions: Certainty (likelihood of detection/punishment), Severity (magnitude of punishment), Celerity (swiftness of punishment). Certainty consistently outweighs severity because people overweight detection probability in decision-making; extremely severe but unlikely sanctions don't deter (D'Arcy & Herath, 2011). For cybersecurity policy violations, employees respond more to consistent enforcement (certain detection via monitoring) than harsh penalties (severity). Low certainty in cybercrime (anonymity, jurisdiction issues) undermines deterrence. Measurement: Perceived certainty, severity, celerity scales. Critique: Assumes rationality; ignores emotional, social, and moral factors driving compliance.

Question 3: Explain Neutralization Theory and list the five techniques of neutralization. How do employees rationalize security policy violations, and what interventions can reduce neutralization?

Model Answer (100 words):

Neutralization Theory (Sykes & Matza, 1957) argues violators rationalize deviance through five techniques: Denial of responsibility ("not my fault"), Denial of injury ("no harm done"), Denial of victim ("they deserved it"), Condemnation of condemners ("everyone does it"), Appeal to higher loyalties ("had to help a friend"). Employees justify security violations: sharing passwords ("efficiency matters more"), using personal devices ("company policy is unrealistic"). Interventions: security education emphasizing real harm (counters denial of injury), accountability mechanisms (counters responsibility denial), and positive security culture (reduces condemnation) (Siponen & Vance, 2010). Measurement: Neutralization scales. Critique: Post-hoc rationalizations; doesn't explain initial violation motivation.

Author: Student-compiled resource for UNT BCIS PhD program

Original Sources: BCIS 6670 (IS Theory), BCIS 6680 (Research Methods), BCIS 6360 (Advanced Topics)

📋 Changelog

  • v1.0 (February 2026) - Revised for PhD Comps:
    • ✅ Fixed theory count inconsistency (standardized to 77 core theories across all references)
    • ✅ Added professional disclaimer clarifying this is student-compiled, not official UNT material
    • ✅ Softened overclaiming language ("curated collection" vs "complete encyclopedia")
    • ✅ Created Exam Blueprint section with Fast Track (7-14 days) and Standard (3-6 weeks) study paths
    • ✅ Added new Part RM: Research Methods & Study Design with 8 foundational topics (validity, CMB, causality, mixed methods, SLR, grounded theory, quantitative methods, qualitative methods)
    • ✅ Implemented priority tagging system (Core/Important/Optional) to guide exam preparation focus
    • ✅ Added practice questions with model answers following exam rubric (90-100 words, citations, structured format)

📖 How to Cite This Document

IS Theory Encyclopedia (v1.0). (2026). Student-compiled PhD comprehensive exam study guide. University of North Texas BCIS Program. Unpublished compilation.

Note: This is a student resource, not a peer-reviewed publication. Always cite original theory sources (e.g., Davis, 1989; Gregor, 2006) in academic work, not this compilation.
